title: Spaghetti Bolognese
description: The weeknight classic. Slow-simmered mince, rich tinned tomato, a knob of butter to finish.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze the sauce (without pasta) for up to 3 months. Reheat until piping hot.
make_ahead: The sauce is even better the next day — make it up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently while the spaghetti cooks.
leftovers: Spare sauce is brilliant on a jacket potato or pressed into a cheese toastie.

substitutions.swap: pork mince, or half pork and half beef for a softer, richer sauce
any hard grating cheese — a block of tasty does the job
any long pasta, or penne if that's what's in the cupboard

steps.title: Dice the aromatics
steps.content: Dice onion, mince garlic.
steps.detail: Halve and peel the onion, keeping the root on to stop your eyes watering. Make lengthwise cuts, then across — aim for small, even dice (about 5mm). Smash garlic with the flat of a knife to loosen the skin, then mince or grate. Having everything prepped before you start the heat is called mise en place — makes the cook relaxed, not frantic.
tips: If garlic sticks to the knife, a drop of oil rubbed on the blade stops it clinging.

steps.title: Brown the mince
steps.content: Brown mince in olive oil, 6 min.
steps.detail: Heat a glug of oil in a heavy pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Crumble the mince in in large chunks — don't break it up straight away. Let it sit untouched for 2 min to deep-brown on the bottom. Then start breaking up with a wooden spoon as you stir. That browning is flavour. Cook until no pink remains.
tips: If the mince releases a pool of liquid, keep going — it has to evaporate before real browning starts.
warnings: Wash hands, board and anything raw mince touched before moving on — don't let it near food you'll eat raw.

steps.title: Soften onion and garlic
steps.content: Add onion and garlic, cook until soft.
steps.detail: Drop heat to medium. Tip in onion and garlic — they'll sizzle and soften in the mince fat. Cook 5 min, stirring occasionally, until the onion is translucent and starting to gold at the edges. If the pan looks dry, a splash of water helps. Season with a big pinch of salt now.
tips: As the onion releases moisture, scrape the browned bits off the pan base — that's free flavour for the sauce.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Tip in tomatoes, simmer 20 min.
steps.detail: Crush whole tomatoes with your hand as you pour them in. Add a pinch of salt, a crack of pepper, and a knob of butter if you've got one — it rounds off the acid. Simmer uncovered on low — big lazy bubbles, not a rolling boil — for 20 min, stirring every few minutes. It should reduce to a rich, glossy sauce that coats a spoon.
tips: Splash a little water into each empty tin, swirl and tip it in — no tomato left behind.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti.
steps.detail: Bring a big pot of water to a rolling boil. Salt it heavily — it should taste like sea water. Add spaghetti, stir to prevent clumping. Cook 1 min less than the packet time for al dente. Before draining, scoop out a cup of pasta water — the starchy water emulsifies the sauce and pasta.
tips: Set a timer off the packet, minus one minute — then taste a strand; it should have a slim pale core.

steps.title: Marry pasta and sauce
steps.content: Toss through sauce, top with parmesan.
steps.detail: Drag the pasta straight from the pot into the sauce with tongs — a little water clinging is fine. Toss for a minute over low heat. If the sauce looks tight, splash in pasta water. Parmesan grated over the top, crack of pepper, eat immediately while it's still steaming.
tips: Grate the parmesan finely so it melts into the sauce instead of sitting on top.

title: Green Thai Curry
description: Bright, punchy green curry with chicken thigh and coconut milk.
equipment: wide deep frying pan or wok
medium saucepan with a tight lid
storage: Curry keeps 2–3 days airtight in the fridge; cool any leftover rice quickly and eat it within a day. Freezes up to 2 months — the coconut milk may separate, so stir well while reheating until piping hot.
make_ahead: The curry can be made a day ahead — reheat gently, and add the lime, basil and chilli fresh at serving time.
leftovers: Leftover curry is great tossed through rice noodles the next night.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast (simmer 8–10 min so it doesn't dry out) or cubes of firm tofu
soy sauce — you lose some funk but keep the salt
Thai basil if you can get it; otherwise coriander works

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Cook the rice.
steps.detail: Rinse 2 cups jasmine rice until the water runs clear. Pot with 3 cups water, bring to a boil, cover, drop to the lowest heat for 12 min, then rest covered 5 min and fluff with a fork. Start this first so it’s hot to serve under the curry.
tips: Drain the rinsed rice well — extra water on the grains throws the ratio off and turns it gluey.

steps.title: Bloom the paste
steps.content: Fry curry paste in a splash of oil, 1 min.
steps.detail: Heat a tablespoon of oil in a wide pan over medium. Spoon in the curry paste and stir constantly for a full minute — you'll see it go from matte to shiny, smell it bloom. This step wakes the paste up; skip it and the curry stays flat. If it starts to catch, drop the heat.
tips: Have the chicken cut and ready before the paste hits the pan — this step moves fast.
warnings: Curry paste can spit when it hits hot oil — keep your face back as you stir.

steps.title: Coat the chicken
steps.content: Add diced chicken, toss to coat.
steps.detail: Cut thigh into bite-sized pieces (about 3cm — bigger than you think, they shrink). Tip into the paste and stir until every piece wears a green coat. Cook 2 min to just start colouring the edges — not fully cooking through yet.
tips: Kitchen scissors are quicker than a knife for cubing slippery thigh.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife with hot soapy water after cutting raw chicken.

steps.title: Simmer gently
steps.content: Pour in coconut milk, simmer 12 min.
steps.detail: Shake the tin first — the cream and water often separate. Pour it all in, including any solid cream on top. Stir to dissolve the paste into the coconut milk. Bring to a bare simmer (small bubbles) and cook 10–12 min until the chicken is cooked and the sauce has tightened slightly. Don't boil hard — it splits coconut milk. If you're unsure about the chicken, cut the biggest piece in half — it should be white right through with no pink.
tips: It's ready when the sauce coats the back of a spoon and a thin film of oil beads on the surface.

steps.title: Balance the seasoning
steps.content: Season with fish sauce and lime.
steps.detail: Off heat for tasting (flavours are less bold hot). Stir in fish sauce, then squeeze half the lime. Taste. It should be balanced — salty from fish sauce, sour from lime, a touch sweet from the coconut. Adjust in tiny increments. More lime if flat, more fish sauce if too sweet.
tips: Taste it on a spoonful of rice if the rice is done — that's how you'll actually eat it.

steps.title: Serve and garnish
steps.content: Over rice, basil and chilli.
steps.detail: Pile over jasmine rice. Tear basil (don't chop — basil bruises, turns dark). Sliced fresh chilli on top for heat. Extra lime wedges on the side. A spoon is better than a fork — you want broth with every bite.
tips: The seeds carry most of a chilli's heat — flick them out for a gentler topping.

title: Chicken Tikka Masala
description: Yoghurt-marinated chicken in a tomato-cream sauce. Better than takeaway.
equipment: medium saucepan with a tight lid
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge, or freeze up to 2 months — the creamy sauce can split slightly, so stir well while reheating gently until piping hot. Cool leftover rice fast and eat within a day.
make_ahead: Marinate the chicken the night before — overnight in yoghurt makes it noticeably more tender. The finished curry also reheats beautifully the next day.
leftovers: Roll leftovers into a warm wrap with extra yoghurt and salad for a quick lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — cut the final simmer to 5–6 minutes so it stays juicy
coconut cream (slightly sweeter) or full-fat evaporated milk
garam masala with a pinch of smoked paprika for a deeper, smokier sauce

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Marinate chicken in yoghurt and curry powder.
steps.detail: Cube chicken thighs into 3cm pieces. In a bowl, mix with yoghurt, curry powder, a big pinch of salt, and a squeeze of lemon if you've got one. Yoghurt tenderises the meat — its lactic acid breaks down fibres gently. 30 min is good; overnight is transformative. Cover and fridge.
tips: Full-fat Greek yoghurt clings better and is less likely to split in the hot pan.
warnings: Wash hands, bowl-edges and board after handling raw chicken — and never reuse the marinade raw.

steps.title: Sear in batches
steps.content: Sear chicken in hot pan, set aside.
steps.detail: Heat a splash of oil in a heavy pan until shimmering. Shake excess marinade off each piece and sear in batches — overcrowding steams. 2 min a side for deep gold char patches. Don't cook through yet; it finishes in the sauce. Remove to a plate.
tips: Dark charred spots on the yoghurt coating are flavour, not a mistake — that's the tikka character.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.content: Soften onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Same pan — don't wash, those fond bits are flavour. Lower to medium. Finely chopped onion, a splash of oil if dry. 6 min till soft and golden at the edges. Add grated garlic the last minute.
tips: If the fond starts turning black, splash in a little water and scrape — it lifts straight off into the onions.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.content: Add tomatoes, simmer 10 min, then cream.
steps.detail: Tip in tomatoes (crush with your hand). Stir, season, simmer 10 min uncovered till thick and the raw tomato edge is gone. Off heat, stir in cream — don't let it boil hard once cream's in, it can split. While the sauce simmers, get the rice on: rinse 2 cups basmati, add 3 cups water and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then lid on over the lowest heat for 12 minutes and rest it off the heat, still covered.
tips: For a takeaway-silky sauce, blitz it smooth with a stick blender before the cream goes in.

steps.title: Finish the chicken
steps.content: Return chicken, simmer 8 min.
steps.detail: Chicken back with any resting juices. Simmer gently 8 min till cooked through (juices run clear when pierced). Taste — salt, a pinch of sugar if too sharp. If you're unsure, cut the largest piece in half — it should be opaque right through with no pink at the centre.
tips: It's done when the sauce clings to the chicken rather than running straight off the pieces.

steps.title: Serve it up
steps.content: Over basmati, scatter coriander.
steps.detail: Serve over fluffy basmati rice. Tear coriander over the top — a lot of it. A dollop of extra yoghurt on the side is lovely. Warm naan if you have it.
tips: Coriander stems carry more flavour than the leaves — chop them fine and scatter those too.

title: Haloumi Grain Bowl
description: Crispy haloumi, roast pumpkin, rocket, lemon. Lunch goals.
equipment: medium saucepan with a tight lid
storage: Keep the components separate and airtight: pumpkin and undressed rocket last 3 days in the fridge, but cool the rice quickly and use it within a day. Fried haloumi doesn't keep — re-fry a fresh batch. Not one for the freezer.
make_ahead: Roast the pumpkin and cook the rice up to 2 days ahead — but fry the haloumi fresh; it turns rubbery as it cools.
leftovers: Leftover roast pumpkin mashes into a great sandwich spread or folds into a frittata.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato — same size chunks, same roasting time
quinoa, or couscous if you want it on the table quicker
baby spinach for a softer, milder base

steps.title: Roast the pumpkin
steps.content: Roast pumpkin with oil and cumin, 25 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: Chunk pumpkin into 3cm cubes, skin on or off. Toss on a tray with a glug of olive oil, a teaspoon of cumin, flakes of salt, a grind of pepper. Spread in a single layer with space between pieces — crowded veg steams instead of caramelising. 25 min at 200°C, give the tray a shake halfway. Done when edges are deep gold and a knife slides through easily.
tips: Line the tray with baking paper — the cumin-oil coating loves to weld itself to bare metal.

steps.title: Steam the rice
steps.content: Cook rice.
steps.detail: One cup basmati, rinsed under cold water till the water runs clear — that washes off surface starch and gives fluffier grains. Into a pot with 1.5 cups water, pinch of salt, lid on. Bring to a boil, drop to the lowest flame, 12 min. Don't lift the lid. Off heat, still covered, 5 min. Fork through to separate.
tips: No glass lid? Listen instead — when the bubbling goes quiet, the water's been absorbed.

steps.title: Crisp the haloumi
steps.content: Fry haloumi slices till golden both sides.
steps.detail: Slice haloumi into 1cm slabs — thinner goes rubbery, thicker won't get a crust. Pat each piece dry with paper towel; surface moisture prevents browning. Dry pan over medium-high, no oil (haloumi is already rich). Lay slices in and don't touch for 2 min — let the crust form. Flip once. Eat within a few minutes; haloumi squeaks on the tooth when it cools.
tips: Time it so the haloumi finishes last — it's at its golden best in the first five minutes out of the pan.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Assemble bowl: rice, rocket, pumpkin, haloumi.
steps.detail: Wide shallow bowls work better than deep ones — spread the beauty out. Warm rice as the base, big handfuls of rocket tumbled on one side, pumpkin clumped on another, haloumi slabs leaning together. Let colours sit next to each other rather than tossing — you eat with your eyes first.

steps.title: Dress and finish
steps.content: Squeeze lemon over the lot.
steps.detail: Halve the lemon and squeeze firmly over everything — cut-side-up stops pips dropping. The acid lifts the pumpkin's sweetness and cuts through haloumi's salt. Another drizzle of olive oil, crack of pepper. If you have dukkah or toasted seeds, scatter those too. Eat warm or room temp — this bowl doesn't mind either.
tips: Roll the lemon firmly on the bench before halving — it gives up noticeably more juice.

title: Sausage Pasta Bake
description: Snags out of their skins, tomato, pasta, a crust of cheese. Feeds a crowd.
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge and freezes in portions for up to 3 months. Reheat covered at 180°C, or microwave until piping hot in the middle.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole thing up to a day ahead and keep it in the fridge unbaked — add about 10 minutes to the oven time when baking from cold.
leftovers: It reheats so well the leftovers might be the best bit — pack portions for lunches.

substitutions.swap: any sausage works — Italian pork are ideal — or use 500 g of plain mince and season it well
tasty cheese, or a half-and-half mix for more flavour and stretch
a teaspoon of dried oregano stirred into the sauce instead

steps.title: Brown the sausage meat
steps.content: Squeeze snag meat out of skins, brown in pan.
steps.detail: Slit each snag lengthways with a small knife, peel the skin off like a banana. The meat inside is your mince, free of charge, already seasoned. Heat a glug of oil in a big heavy pan over medium-high. Break the sausage meat in with your fingers in rough chunks. Let sit 2 min for colour, then break up with a wooden spoon. Cook till no pink remains and some edges have browned, 6 min.
tips: Cold snags peel easier — the skin pulls away cleanly straight from the fridge.
warnings: Raw sausage meat is raw mince — wash hands and anything it touched before you move on.

steps.title: Soften the aromatics
steps.content: Add diced onion and garlic, soften.
steps.detail: Lower heat to medium. Finely dice a brown onion — aim for small, even pieces so they melt into the sauce. Tip in with grated or crushed garlic. Everything sizzles in the sausage fat (flavour!). Stir 5 min till onion is translucent and softening. A pinch of salt now helps draw moisture out.
tips: If the sausage fat looks sparse, a small glug of oil keeps everything sizzling instead of sticking.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Pour in tomatoes, simmer.
steps.detail: Pour in two tins of tomatoes, crushing whole ones with your hand as you go. Splash some water into each tin to rinse out the rest and tip that in too. Salt, pepper, pinch of sugar if the tomatoes taste sharp. Bring to a bare bubble, drop the heat, 10 min uncovered till the sauce thickens and the raw tomato edge is gone.
tips: Let the sauce get properly thick now — it loosens again with pasta water and keeps softening in the oven.

steps.title: Undercook the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta, reserving some water.
steps.detail: Big pot, heavily salted water (taste like the sea), rolling boil. Cook pasta 2 min LESS than the packet says — it'll finish cooking in the oven and you don't want mush. Before draining, scoop out a cup of pasta water. That starchy water loosens the sauce later if it tightens up.
tips: Stir the pot in the first minute — that's when pasta most wants to clump and stick.

steps.title: Assemble the bake
steps.content: Combine, tip into dish, top with mozzarella.
steps.detail: Fold the pasta through the sauce with a big splash of reserved water — it should look a little loose; it'll drink up more in the oven. Tip into a baking dish. Tear mozzarella into chunks and dot evenly across the top — don't carpet it flat, you want molten pockets and crispy edges.
tips: If your mozzarella is the fresh, wet kind, pat it dry first — less water means a crisper top.

steps.title: Bake until blistered
steps.content: Bake 15 min at 220°C until bubbly.
steps.detail: 220°C oven for 15 min till the top is bubbling and blistered with gold patches. If it's looking pale, flash under the grill for 90 seconds — watch it like a hawk, cheese goes from gold to burnt in seconds. Rest 5 min before scooping — the sauce sets up and stops being molten. Tear basil over the top.
tips: Sit the dish on a baking tray if it's full to the brim — saves scrubbing the oven floor if it bubbles over.

title: Sunday Roast Chook
description: Whole chicken, crispy skin, lemon and garlic.
storage: Strip the meat once cool and keep it airtight in the fridge for 3 days, or freeze for 2 months. Eat it cold or reheat gently until piping hot — and keep the carcass for stock.
make_ahead: Salt the chicken and leave it uncovered on a plate in the fridge overnight — the skin dries out and roasts extra crisp the next day.
leftovers: Leftover chook means sandwiches, fried rice or a quick noodle soup — and the carcass simmers into a great stock.

substitutions.swap: half potato, half pumpkin or sweet potato — they roast happily in the same time
a quartered onion in the cavity if you're out of lemons

steps.title: Heat the oven
steps.content: Preheat oven to 200°C.
steps.detail: Get the oven going first thing — by the time you've prepped, it's ready. A roast needs high heat. Pull the chicken out of the fridge when you turn the oven on — cold meat cooks unevenly.
tips: Set the rack in the lower half of the oven — a tall bird can catch on the top element.

steps.title: Dry and season
steps.content: Pat chook dry, rub with oil and salt.
steps.detail: Paper towels inside and out — dry skin = crispy skin. Rub all over with olive oil, then coat heavily with sea salt and cracked pepper. Don't skimp on seasoning — a 1.6kg bird can take a good tablespoon of salt spread around. Massage into the legs, under the wings, everywhere.
tips: Season from high above the bird — the salt spreads more evenly on the way down.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash hands and every surface it touched with hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Stuff the cavity
steps.content: Stuff cavity with lemon and garlic.
steps.detail: Halve a lemon and a whole garlic head across the equator. Stuff inside the cavity — they steam the bird from inside and flavour the juices. A few thyme sprigs if you have them. Tying the legs with string keeps the thighs juicy but it's optional.

steps.title: Roast on the spuds
steps.content: Roast on potatoes, 75 min.
steps.detail: Chunk potatoes (skin on), toss in oil and salt. Spread in a roasting tray, sit the chicken on top breast-side-up. The chicken fat drips onto the potatoes — best roast potatoes in the world. 75 min for a 1.6kg bird: juices run clear when pierced in the thigh, and a thermometer reads 75°C in the thickest part. Baste halfway with pan juices if you remember.
tips: If the breast is browning too fast, cap it loosely with foil — it slows the colour without steaming the skin.

steps.title: Rest the bird
steps.content: Rest 10 min before carving.
steps.detail: Non-negotiable. Lift the chicken onto a board, tent loosely with foil. The juices that would run out onto the board now redistribute into the meat. Use the 10 min to stir pan juices into a quick gravy if you like — a splash of stock, boil hard, done.
tips: Carve the legs off first, then slice the breast across the grain — neater slices, juicier bites.

title: Veggie Fried Rice
description: Leftover rice, a fridge raid, one pan. Dinner in 10.
storage: Best eaten straight away. Because the rice is already a day old, fridge any leftovers airtight for 1 day at most and reheat until piping hot right through. Don't freeze.
make_ahead: This is the make-ahead: cook the rice a day early, spread it to cool fast, then fridge it covered — day-old rice fries far better than fresh.
leftovers: A fried egg on top turns a reheated portion straight back into dinner.

substitutions.swap: any frozen veg mix, or corn kernels
finely diced capsicum — add it with the garlic just the same
tamari to keep it gluten-free

steps.title: Scramble the eggs
steps.content: Scramble eggs, set aside.
steps.detail: Crack two eggs into a bowl and beat with a splash of soy if you're organised. Hot wok or wide pan, small glug of oil. Pour eggs in — they should sizzle on contact. Scramble with a spatula in big folds for 30 sec till just set, still glossy. They'll finish cooking from residual heat. Tip onto a plate.
tips: Beat until no clear streaks of white remain — streaky eggs cook unevenly in a hot pan.

steps.title: Fry the aromatics
steps.content: Fry garlic and diced carrot.
steps.detail: Same pan (don't wash it), more oil if needed. Drop heat to medium. Grated garlic for 20 sec till fragrant — not brown. Small-diced carrot goes in (2mm cubes cook fast). 2 min stirring till the carrot just softens at the edges but keeps a little bite.
warnings: Garlic goes bitter fast at wok heat — if it turns dark brown, wipe the pan out and start this step again.

steps.title: Crank the heat
steps.content: Add peas and cold rice, toss over high heat.
steps.detail: Now crank the heat to max. Tip in frozen peas and the cold leftover rice — cold rice is KEY. Fresh warm rice turns gluey when stir-fried. Use a spatula to break up rice clumps and toss everything till the rice is heated through and starting to crisp at the edges, 3 min. High heat, fast hands.
tips: Press the rice flat against the hot pan for 20 seconds between tosses — that's where the crispy bits come from.

steps.title: Season the wok way
steps.content: Pour soy sauce down the side of the pan.
steps.detail: Instead of pouring soy onto the rice (it pools and seasons unevenly), tilt the pan and pour soy down the side — it hits the hot metal, sizzles and evaporates a bit, coats evenly as you toss. 2 tablespoons is plenty. This is the wok-hei trick.
tips: Taste before adding more soy — it keeps concentrating as it reduces in the pan.

steps.title: Bring it together
steps.content: Fold in eggs and spring onion.
steps.detail: Break the scrambled eggs into bite-sized chunks with the spatula and fold back through with sliced spring onions. 30 sec to warm the eggs. Off heat, finish with a drizzle of sesame oil if you've got some. Eat straight from the pan; fried rice gets claggy if it waits.
tips: Save a few of the green spring onion tops to scatter raw over the top.

title: Pumpkin & Chickpea Curry
description: Sweet pumpkin, creamy coconut, warming curry powder.
equipment: large heavy saucepan or deep frying pan with lid
medium saucepan with a tight lid
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge and freezes for up to 3 months — the coconut milk may separate, so stir well while reheating until piping hot. Cool leftover rice quickly and eat it within a day.
make_ahead: Like most curries it's better the next day — make it up to 2 days ahead, but stir the spinach in fresh as you reheat.
leftovers: Blitz leftovers with a cup of stock into a spiced pumpkin soup.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato — same size chunks, roughly the same simmer
frozen spinach (squeeze it dry first) or shredded silverbeet
tinned brown lentils or butter beans — anything that just needs warming through

steps.title: Start the base
steps.content: Soften onion with curry powder.
steps.detail: Finely dice a brown onion. Heat a glug of oil in a heavy pan over medium. Add onion with a pinch of salt — salt pulls moisture out and speeds softening. Cook 6 min till translucent. Sprinkle in 2 tablespoons of curry powder and stir constantly for 30 sec to wake the spices up and coat the onion. It'll smell incredible — that's the signal to move on. Get the rice going now too: rinse 2 cups basmati, add 3 cups water and a pinch of salt, bring to the boil, then lid on over the lowest heat for 12 minutes and rest it off the heat, still covered.
tips: Ground spices scorch quickly — if the curry powder starts to catch, a splash of water rescues it.

steps.title: Coat the pumpkin
steps.content: Add diced pumpkin, toss to coat.
steps.detail: Cube pumpkin into 2.5cm chunks, skin off (or on if you don't mind — it softens in the coconut milk). Tip into the spiced onions and toss till every cube wears an orange jacket. 2 min to start the edges softening. This builds the pumpkin's flavour before the liquid goes in.
tips: Cut a slice off one side of the pumpkin first so it sits flat — cubing is safer and much easier.

steps.title: Simmer till tender
steps.content: Pour in coconut milk, simmer 20 min.
steps.detail: Shake the tin of coconut milk first (cream and water separate). Pour it all in. Splash half a cup of water into the empty tin, swirl, tip in too. Bring to a bare simmer — small lazy bubbles, not a rolling boil, which would split the coconut milk. Lid on slightly ajar, 18 min till pumpkin is fork-tender.
tips: Fork-test a cube at 15 minutes — pumpkin sizes vary and mush sneaks up quickly.

steps.title: Add the greens
steps.content: Add chickpeas and spinach for last 3 min.
steps.detail: Drain and rinse the chickpeas (the tin brine can taste metallic). Stir in with two handfuls of spinach for the last 3 min. Spinach wilts almost instantly; chickpeas just need warming through. If the sauce looks tight, loosen with a splash of water. Taste — salt, maybe a squeeze of lime to lift it.

steps.title: Serve with rice
steps.content: Serve over basmati.
steps.detail: Fluff basmati with a fork. Ladle curry into shallow bowls, rice alongside (not under — it goes mushy). A dollop of yoghurt cools it if the spice has kicked up, torn coriander on top. Pappadums or flatbread if you've got them — curry wants something to scoop with.

title: Lemon Chicken & Rice
description: One-pan, lemon-bright, weeknight winner.
equipment: large deep frying pan with a tight-fitting lid
storage: Cool leftovers quickly and fridge airtight — because of the cooked rice, eat within a day, reheated until piping hot right through. Skip the freezer; the rice turns mushy.
leftovers: Shred the chicken through the rice and crisp the lot in a hot pan next day, pilaf-style.

substitutions.swap: skin-on thigh is best, but drumsticks work — add 5 minutes to the simmer
3 cups of water plus a crumbled stock cube
dill (lovely with lemon) or sliced spring onion tops

steps.title: Crisp the skin
steps.content: Sear chicken in oil, skin-side down.
steps.detail: Pat chicken thighs dry with paper towel — wet skin won't crisp. Season both sides well with salt and pepper. Heat a glug of oil in a heavy oven-safe pan over medium-high. Lay thighs in SKIN-SIDE DOWN and don't touch them for 5 min — let the skin render its fat and turn mahogany. Flip for 1 min to seal the other side.
tips: The thighs will release from the pan on their own when the skin's ready — if they're stuck, give them another minute.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash your hands, and don't let the tongs that placed it touch cooked food later without a hot wash.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Remove, add garlic and rice, toast 1 min.
steps.detail: Lift chicken out to a plate (it's not cooked through yet, that's fine — finishes later). Leave the golden fat in the pan. Drop heat to medium. Add four garlic cloves, thinly sliced — they should sizzle but not burn. Tip in rinsed rice and stir 60 sec till every grain is glossed with chickeny fat.
warnings: Sliced garlic burns fast in hot fat — if it's colouring before the rice goes in, pull the pan off the heat.

steps.title: Deglaze and season
steps.content: Pour in stock and lemon juice.
steps.detail: Pour in 3 cups of chicken stock and the juice of a whole lemon. Scrape the bottom of the pan with a wooden spoon to lift any browned bits — that's your flavour. Season with salt and pepper, bring to a boil.
tips: Taste the liquid now — it should be a touch saltier than soup, because the rice will absorb the seasoning.

steps.title: Simmer under the lid
steps.content: Return chicken, cover, simmer 22 min.
steps.detail: Nestle the chicken thighs skin-side up on top of the rice — they'll poke out above the liquid, which keeps the skin crispy while the rice cooks below. Lid on tight, drop to the lowest flame. 22 min. Don't lift the lid — every peek lets steam escape and the rice ends up unevenly cooked. If the rice is still firm at the end of the rest, add a splash of water, lid back on over low heat for 5 more minutes.

steps.title: Rest and finish
steps.content: Rest 5 min, scatter parsley.
steps.detail: Off heat, lid still on, 5 min. Critical rest — the rice absorbs the last of the stock and the chicken juices redistribute. Scatter with roughly chopped parsley and serve straight from the pan. Lemon wedges on the side for anyone who wants more zing. Before serving, check the biggest thigh — juices should run clear with no pink near the bone. Any doubt: lid back on over low heat for another 5 minutes.

title: BBQ Snag Sanga
description: Bread, snag, grilled onions, sauce. A national treasure.
storage: Cooked snags and onions keep 3 days airtight in the fridge — reheat until piping hot. Assembled sangas don't keep; build them fresh.
make_ahead: The onions can be cooked a day ahead and rewarmed in the pan while the snags grill.
leftovers: Slice leftover snags into tomorrow's fried eggs, or pile the onions onto a steak sandwich.

substitutions.swap: soft white rolls if you must, but plain sliced white is the classic for a reason
barbecue sauce, or mustard for the contrarians

steps.title: Heat the barbecue
steps.content: Fire up the BBQ.
steps.detail: Gas BBQ: turn all burners to medium-high, lid down, 10 min. You want the grills hot enough that a drop of water hisses off instantly. Scrub the grills clean with a wire brush while it heats. Charcoal BBQ: light coals 30 min ahead; ready when they're glowing orange with a light grey dust on top. Oil the grills lightly with a tongs-held paper towel dipped in oil.
tips: No wire brush? A scrunched ball of foil held in tongs scrubs the grills fine.
warnings: Charcoal: never add firelighter fluid to coals that are already lit.

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.content: Slice onions, fry in butter until golden.
steps.detail: Halve the onions and slice them as thin as you dare — they cook down a LOT. Knob of butter in a pan on the BBQ side burner (or a pan off to the side of a coal BBQ). Medium-low heat. Cook 20 min, stirring every few minutes, till deeply golden and sweet. A pinch of salt helps, and don't rush — patient onions are the heart of a good sanga.
tips: If the onions catch before they're golden, a splash of water deglazes the pan and keeps them going.

steps.title: Grill the snags
steps.content: Grill snags, turning often.
steps.detail: Snags on the grill, lid down for gas or cover slightly for coal. Turn every 2 min — this stops them splitting and gives even colour all round. 12 min total till firm to the squeeze and deep gold. Don't prick them; juices that run out are flavour that's lost. If flare-ups happen, move them to a cooler spot. Not sure they're done? Cut one open — the middle should show no pink and the juices should run clear.
tips: Medium heat, not roaring — burnt outside and raw inside is the classic snag mistake.
warnings: Use separate tongs for raw and cooked snags, or give them a good hot wash in between.

steps.title: Build the sanga
steps.content: Butter bread, fold around snag and onions.
steps.detail: Slice of white bread per snag. Smear with butter (it stops the bread going soggy from sausage fat). Snag laid diagonally across (corner-to-corner is the classic Bunnings fold), pile of onions on top, fold bread over into a triangle. Tomato sauce drawn on in a spiral is the traditional move. Eat standing up.

title: Prawn Pad Thai
description: Rice noodles, prawns, peanuts, lime. Takeaway at home.
storage: With prawns involved, keep leftovers no more than 1–2 days airtight in the fridge, and reheat hard and fast in a pan until piping hot. Don't freeze — the noodles turn to mush.
make_ahead: Mix the sauce and soak the noodles up to a few hours ahead — once the wok is hot, the cooking waits for nothing.

substitutions.swap: thin-sliced chicken thigh (cook 4–5 minutes) or cubes of firm tofu
crunchy is fine — or tamarind paste with a little extra sugar for the classic sour edge
soy sauce at a pinch — saltier, less funky, still good

steps.title: Whisk the sauce
steps.content: Mix fish sauce, peanut butter, lime for sauce.
steps.detail: In a small bowl, whisk 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp peanut butter, juice of half a lime, 1 tsp sugar or honey. Loosen with a splash of hot water till it pours easily. Taste — should hit salty, sour, sweet, a touch nutty. Adjust in tiny increments. Pre-mixing means you're not scrambling once the wok is hot.
tips: If the peanut butter won't whisk smooth, 10 seconds in the microwave loosens it right up.

steps.title: Flash-fry the prawns
steps.content: Fry prawns 2 min, remove.
steps.detail: Pat prawns dry. Hot pan or wok, splash of oil. Prawns in a single layer, don't crowd — they steam instead of sear if piled up. 60 sec one side till pink and curling, flip, 60 sec the other side. That's it — any longer and they're rubbery. Lift onto a plate.
tips: A prawn curled into a tight O is overdone — pull them off at a loose C shape.
warnings: Raw prawns: keep them cold until the pan is hot, and wash hands and board afterwards.

steps.title: Scramble the eggs
steps.content: Scramble eggs in pan.
steps.detail: Same pan, drop heat to medium. Crack 2 eggs straight in. Let them sit 20 sec, then scramble with a spatula in big soft folds till just set. Push to one side of the pan. Fast — you don't want dry egg.

steps.title: Toss the noodles
steps.content: Toss cooked rice noodles with sauce (use spaghetti in a pinch).
steps.detail: Rice noodles should be soaked per packet (usually 8 min in hot water till pliable but still firm). Drain. Tip into the pan with the sauce. Use tongs to toss everything — noodles soak up sauce fast. 60 sec till glossy and coated. If it looks dry, splash of water. Spaghetti pre-cooked 1 min short of packet works surprisingly well in a pinch.
tips: Bite a noodle to check the soak — it should bend easily with no chalky white core.

steps.title: Finish and garnish
steps.content: Return prawns, top with spring onion and chilli.
steps.detail: Prawns back in with resting juices. Toss 20 sec to warm through. Off heat. Thin-sliced spring onions, fresh chilli, torn coriander, chopped peanuts over the top. Extra lime wedge on the side. Eat immediately, chopsticks or fork — whatever works.

title: Smashed Avo on Toast
description: The brunch standard. Sourdough, avo, feta, lemon, chilli flakes.
storage: Doesn't keep — assemble and eat straight away. An unused avo half lasts a day in the fridge with the stone in and the cut side covered.

substitutions.swap: goat's cheese, or skip it and add a soft-boiled egg
sliced fresh chilli, or a spoon of dukkah for crunch

steps.title: Toast the base
steps.content: Toast bread.
steps.detail: Thick-cut sourdough if you have it. Toast well — you want a sturdy base that holds avo without going soggy. Under the grill toasts more evenly than a pop-up. One side, flip, watch for deep gold.
tips: Under the grill it's 2–3 minutes a side — stay close, it turns from gold to burnt fast.

steps.title: Smash the avo
steps.content: Mash avo with lemon and salt.
steps.detail: Ripe avo gives easily when you press near the stem. Halve, twist apart, lift out the stone. Scoop flesh into a bowl. Squeeze lemon and season with salt flakes — add lemon BEFORE mashing so it doesn't brown. Mash with a fork — chunky, not puréed. Texture is good.
tips: Rock-hard avo? Sit it in a paper bag with a banana for a day or two — don't fight an unripe one.

steps.title: Top with feta
steps.content: Pile on toast, crumble feta.
steps.detail: Spread avo generously, right to the edges. Feta crumbled between your fingers (more rustic than cubed). Don't hold back on the feta — it balances the richness.

steps.title: Finish and season
steps.content: Drizzle oil, chilli flakes.
steps.detail: Good olive oil in a proper drizzle. Chilli flakes or fresh chilli if you've got it. A final crack of salt on top. Eat immediately — avo toast waits for no one.
tips: This is where your best olive oil belongs — here it's a dressing, not a cooking fat.

title: Beef Tacos
description: Soft tortillas, spiced mince, avo, lime.
storage: Cooked mince keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge or freezes for 3 months; reheat until piping hot. Dice the avocado and tomato fresh each time — they don't keep.
make_ahead: The spiced mince can be cooked a day ahead — reheat it with a splash of water while the tortillas warm.
leftovers: Leftover mince turns into nachos, a burrito bowl or a jacket-potato topping with zero extra effort.

substitutions.swap: pork or chicken mince — same method, same spices
leave it out and add extra lime if coriander tastes soapy to you
lemon at a pinch — it's the acid you're after

steps.title: Brown the spiced mince
steps.content: Brown mince with cumin and paprika.
steps.detail: Hot pan, splash of oil. Crumble mince in and let it sit 2 min for a deep brown crust before breaking up. Add cumin, paprika, salt, pepper; stir through. Cook 6 min till no pink remains. A splash of water at the end loosens it into tacos-friendly texture.
tips: The crust is where the flavour lives — resist stirring until you can smell deep browning.
warnings: Wash hands, board and anything that touched raw mince before you start on the fresh toppings.

steps.title: Make the fresh toppings
steps.content: Dice tomato, avo. Squeeze lime.
steps.detail: Small dice on the tomato — drain on paper towel for 2 min if juicy so tacos don't get soggy. Halve and scoop avo, chop rough. Have a bowl of lime wedges, chopped coriander, hot sauce ready.
tips: A ripe avo yields to a gentle squeeze at the stem end — hard ones can ripen next to bananas for next time.

steps.title: Char the tortillas
steps.content: Warm tortillas.
steps.detail: Small, soft corn tortillas are traditional but flour is fine. Warm each directly on a gas flame or dry pan for 15 sec per side till pliable and a few char spots. Keep warm wrapped in a clean tea towel — they go stiff fast.
tips: If you're using a gas flame, tongs are your friend — flip the moment the edges start to lift.

steps.title: Assemble your tacos
steps.content: Build at the table.
steps.detail: Lay out components and let everyone build their own. Tortilla, mince, tomato, avo, coriander, hot sauce, big squeeze of lime. Two tortillas stacked per taco is the Mexico City trick — the second catches drippings. Eat over a plate.
tips: Don't overfill — about three tablespoons of filling per taco folds without splitting.

title: Zucchini Slice
description: The lunchbox legend. Eggs, zucchini, bacon, a bit of cheese.
storage: Keeps 3–4 days airtight in the fridge, or freeze individual squares wrapped for up to 2 months. Reheat in a 160°C oven so it doesn't turn rubbery.
make_ahead: Bakes ahead beautifully — make it the night before and eat it fridge-cold or gently rewarmed.
leftovers: Fridge-cold squares are lunchbox gold, or sandwich a slab in a toastie with chutney.

substitutions.swap: leave it out for a vegetarian slice, or swap in diced ham or a cup of corn kernels
tasty, colby or any melting cheese you have
self-raising flour gives a slightly puffier slice — either works

steps.title: Grate and wring the zucchini
steps.content: Grate zucchini, squeeze out moisture.
steps.detail: Coarse-grater on the biggest holes. Grate the zucchini into a clean tea towel or a colander over a bowl. Sprinkle a big pinch of salt through, rest 5 min — the salt draws out water. Then wring HARD. This step is non-negotiable; skip it and your slice is a soggy puddle. You should pour off at least a cup of liquid. Wet zucchini is the enemy.
tips: The wrung zucchini should feel like a dense, dryish ball about half its original bulk — keep going if it still drips.

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.content: Mix with beaten eggs, flour, diced bacon, onion, cheese.
steps.detail: Beat 5 eggs in a big bowl with a fork. Tip in the squeezed zucchini, 1 cup plain flour, 4 diced rashers of bacon (raw is fine, it'll cook through), one finely diced brown onion, and 100g grated cheddar. Salt, pepper. Stir with a wooden spoon till just combined — overmixing develops gluten and makes it tough.
tips: Snip the bacon with kitchen scissors straight into the bowl — quicker and neater than a knife.
warnings: Raw bacon and egg are in this bowl — wash hands and utensils before touching anything served uncooked.

steps.title: Fill the tin
steps.content: Pour into lined tin.
steps.detail: Line a 20x30cm slice tin with baking paper — grease first so the paper sticks. Pour the mix in and level with a spatula. It looks loose; it'll set as it cooks. Give the tin a gentle tap on the bench to release any air pockets.
tips: Leave the baking paper overhanging the two long sides — instant handles for lifting the slice out later.

steps.title: Bake until set
steps.content: Bake 35 min at 180°C until set and golden.
steps.detail: 180°C oven, middle rack, 35 min. Done when the top is deep gold and a skewer in the centre comes out clean. The edges should be pulling away from the paper. If it's browning too fast, loosely tent with foil. Cool in the tin 10 min before lifting out — lets it set. Slice into squares; eats hot, warm, or fridge-cold for lunchboxes.
tips: Ovens vary — have a first look at 30 min the first time you make it.

title: Chicken Caesar Salad
description: Crisp lettuce, smoky bacon, parmesan, a punchy dressing.
equipment: small jar or bowl for the dressing
storage: Dressed salad doesn't keep — the leaves collapse within hours. Store components separately: cooked chicken 2–3 days airtight in the fridge, dressing 2 days; don't freeze any of it.
make_ahead: The dressing keeps 2 days in a jar in the fridge and the croutons stay crunchy overnight in an airtight container — cook the chicken and eggs fresh.
leftovers: Undressed leftovers roll into a great wrap — chicken, bacon and leaves with a smear of the dressing.

substitutions.swap: 1 tsp worcestershire sauce gives a similar savoury depth
chicken thighs — more forgiving, cook a couple of minutes longer
stale sourdough makes the best croutons, but any sturdy loaf works

steps.title: Cook and rest the chicken
steps.content: Grill chicken, slice.
steps.detail: Pat breasts dry, season both sides with salt and pepper. If they're thick, butterfly them for even cooking. Hot pan, splash of oil, medium-high. 5 min a side without moving them — let the crust form. Internal temp 74°C in the thickest part. Rest on a board 5 min (juices settle back in), then slice across the grain into thick strips. Carry-over heat keeps working during the rest, taking the thickest part past the 74°C safe mark before you slice.
tips: No thermometer? The thickest part should feel firm and springy, and the juices should run clear when sliced.
warnings: Board, knife and hands that touched raw chicken get hot soapy water before anything else.

steps.title: Crisp the bacon
steps.content: Fry bacon until crisp, tear.
steps.detail: Lay rashers flat in a cold pan, no oil — bacon renders its own. Turn to medium. 6 min, flipping once, till deeply gold and crisp at the edges. Drain on paper towel. When cool enough to handle, tear into shards rather than chop — rustic texture wins.
tips: Keep the rendered fat in the pan when you're done — it's the flavour base for the croutons.

steps.title: Fry the croutons
steps.content: Toast bread, cube for croutons.
steps.detail: Same pan still has bacon fat. Cube bread into 2cm chunks and toss in the fat over medium. 4 min, tossing often, till golden and crunchy on all sides. Those are your croutons — infinitely better than shop-bought.
tips: Cut the cubes evenly so they colour at the same rate.

steps.title: Soft-boil the eggs
steps.content: Boil eggs 6 min, halve.
steps.detail: Lower eggs gently into simmering water on a spoon. Timer for EXACTLY 6 min — set white, still a bit custardy in the centre. Straight into iced water to stop the cook and loosen the shell. Peel under a gentle stream. Halve lengthways.
tips: Slightly older eggs peel much more easily than very fresh ones.

steps.title: Dress and assemble
steps.content: Toss leaves with dressing, top with all.
steps.detail: Tear cos leaves into big bite-sized pieces into the biggest bowl you own. Dressing (anchovy, garlic, dijon, lemon, oil, parmesan) drizzled a little at a time, tossing with your hands till every leaf is barely coated. Don't drown it. Top with chicken, bacon shards, croutons, halved eggs, shaved parmesan. Pepper, lemon wedge. To make the dressing: mash the anchovies and garlic to a paste, whisk in the dijon and the lemon juice, then slowly whisk in about 60 ml of olive oil until creamy, and finish with a spoonful of grated parmesan.
tips: Dress just before serving — cos wilts within minutes of meeting the dressing.

title: Tuna Pasta
description: Pantry-raid dinner. Spaghetti, tuna, lemon, chilli.
storage: Keeps 1–2 days airtight in the fridge — it's seafood, so don't push it. Reheat gently in a pan with a splash of water; it doesn't freeze well.
leftovers: Eat leftovers cold as a pasta salad with a fresh squeeze of lemon and a little more oil.

substitutions.swap: tinned salmon behaves exactly the same way
a big pinch of dried chilli flakes
basil, rocket or baby spinach stirred through at the end

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook pasta.
steps.detail: Big pot of heavily salted water — taste like the sea, that's your only pasta seasoning. Rolling boil, then spaghetti. Stir once to prevent clumping. Cook 1 min less than the packet for al dente. Before draining, scoop out a full cup of pasta water — that starchy liquid is what makes the sauce cling.
tips: Bite a strand to check — al dente shows a tiny white dot at the core.

steps.title: Sizzle garlic and chilli
steps.content: Warm oil with garlic and chilli.
steps.detail: Wide pan, glug of good olive oil, two thinly sliced garlic cloves and a finely chopped chilli (or a big pinch of flakes). Medium-low heat — cold start is fine too. You want the garlic pale gold and fragrant, never brown. Brown garlic is bitter and will ruin the dish. 3 min gentle sizzle.
tips: Tilt the pan now and then so the oil pools and the garlic fries evenly.

steps.title: Fold in tuna and zest
steps.content: Flake in tuna, lemon zest.
steps.detail: Off heat. Drain tuna well (oil or springwater, doesn't matter) and flake straight into the pan with a fork. Grate in the zest of half a lemon (not the white pith, bitter). Stir once — the residual heat warms the tuna without drying it out.
tips: Big flakes eat better than mush — fold gently, don't stir hard.

steps.title: Toss it all together
steps.content: Toss with pasta and parsley.
steps.detail: Drained pasta into the pan with a good splash of reserved water. Juice of half a lemon, torn parsley, toss hard with tongs for 60 sec — the water emulsifies with the oil into a glossy sauce. More water if it looks tight. Crack of pepper, eat immediately. No cheese traditionally — tuna doesn't want it.
tips: If the sauce looks oily rather than creamy, add another splash of pasta water and keep tossing — it will come together.

title: Red Lentil Dahl
description: The humble hero. Creamy, spiced, cheap.
equipment: medium saucepan with a lid for rice
storage: The dahl keeps 3–4 days airtight in the fridge or freezes for 3 months; reheat until piping hot, loosening with water. Cooked rice is different — cool it fast and eat within a day.
make_ahead: Dahl genuinely improves overnight as the spices settle in — make it up to 3 days ahead and reheat with a splash of water.
leftovers: Thick leftover dahl makes a great toastie filling or a sauce for roast vegetables.

substitutions.swap: 1 tsp each ground cumin, turmeric and garam masala
a knob of butter or 150 ml cream stirred through at the end (no longer vegan)
1 tsp ground ginger at a pinch
any long-grain rice, or warm flatbread instead

steps.title: Soften the aromatics
steps.content: Soften onion, garlic, ginger.
steps.detail: Finely dice a brown onion, grate 3 garlic cloves and a thumb of ginger. Heat 2 tbsp oil (or ghee if you've got it) in a heavy pan over medium. Onion in with a pinch of salt, cook 6 min till translucent and starting to gold. Add garlic and ginger for the last minute — they burn quicker than onion.
tips: The onion is ready when it smells sweet rather than sharp.

steps.title: Bloom the spices
steps.content: Add spices, toast 1 min.
steps.detail: Sprinkle in 2 tbsp curry powder (or a mix of turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala if you're spice-stocked). Stir constantly for 60 sec in the hot oil — blooming wakes up the spices and releases their essential oils. You'll smell the difference. Don't walk away; spices burn in seconds.
tips: Have the tomatoes open and the lentils rinsed before you start — the spices won't wait for you.

steps.title: Add lentils and liquids
steps.content: Tip in lentils, tomato, coconut milk, 3 cups water.
steps.detail: Rinse 1.5 cups of red lentils under cold water till it runs clearer — washes off starch and dust. Tip into the pan with a tin of tomatoes (crush with your hand), 200ml coconut milk, and 3 cups of water. Stir, scraping up any spice stuck to the pan — that's all flavour.
tips: No need to soak red lentils — a good rinse is all they want.

steps.title: Simmer until creamy
steps.content: Simmer 25 min until thick.
steps.detail: Bring to a bare simmer, drop heat low. 25 min uncovered, stirring every 5 min so the lentils don't catch on the bottom. They'll break down into a creamy porridge consistency. If it gets too thick, splash of water. Too thin, simmer longer. Season well at the end — lentils need more salt than you think. This is the moment to get the rice on: rinse the basmati until the water runs clearish, add 1.5 times its volume of water, lid on, lowest heat for 12 minutes, then rest it off the heat with the lid still on.
tips: It's done when a spoon dragged across the bottom leaves a trail that fills back in slowly.

steps.title: Plate up with rice
steps.content: Serve over rice.
steps.detail: Fluff basmati. Ladle dahl into bowls, rice on the side. A swirl of yoghurt or extra coconut milk on top looks the part. Tear coriander over. If you have time, fry a sliced garlic clove in ghee till golden with a pinch of chilli flakes and spoon that over — that's tarka, the traditional finish, and it transforms the bowl.

title: Mushroom Risotto
description: Slow-stirred, silky, generous with parmesan.
storage: It's cooked rice, so cool leftovers quickly and keep 1 day airtight in the fridge; reheat gently with extra stock or water to loosen. It won't be as silky second time around, and it doesn't freeze well.
make_ahead: Fry the mushrooms up to a day ahead; the risotto itself waits for no one — start it about 30 minutes before you want to eat.
leftovers: Roll cold risotto into balls, crumb and shallow-fry — arancini, arguably better than the original.

substitutions.swap: extra stock with a squeeze of lemon at the end
chicken stock if you're not keeping it vegetarian
grana padano or pecorino

steps.title: Sweat the onion
steps.content: Soften onion and garlic in butter.
steps.detail: Finely dice a brown onion and grate 2 garlic cloves. Melt a good knob of butter in a heavy wide pan over medium. Onion in, pinch of salt, 8 min till soft and translucent — don't colour, this isn't fry-brown territory, we want sweetness. Garlic for the last minute.
tips: If the onion starts colouring, the heat is too high — add a splash of water and turn it down.

steps.title: Toast the rice
steps.content: Add rice, toast 2 min.
steps.detail: Tip in 1.5 cups of arborio or short-grain rice (jasmine will do in a pinch but it's less traditional). Stir 2 min till every grain is glossy with butter and the edges turn slightly translucent — that's toasting. Toasted rice won't go gluey and will hold its bite.
tips: Listen — toasted grains make a faint clicking sound against the pan.

steps.title: Cook off the wine
steps.content: Splash in wine, stir till absorbed.
steps.detail: Crank heat to medium-high. Pour in half a cup of white wine — it'll hiss and bubble hard. Keep stirring till it's almost fully absorbed and the alcohol has cooked off (smells winey, not sharp). 2 min. This is the flavour backbone.
tips: Stand back a touch as you pour — the first hiss throws steam.

steps.title: Ladle in the stock
steps.content: Add stock a ladle at a time, stirring, 20 min.
steps.detail: Warm the stock in a pot nearby — cold stock shocks the rice. Ladle by ladle: pour in enough to just cover the rice, stir continuously till mostly absorbed, then another. Repeat for 18–20 min. The stirring releases starch that makes risotto creamy. Taste at 18 min — rice should be tender with a tiny bite at the centre.
tips: It's ready when a spoon dragged through leaves a slow-closing path — loose like lava, not standing stiff.

steps.title: Finish and rest
steps.content: Fold in fried mushrooms and parmesan.
steps.detail: Off heat. Fold in a handful of mushrooms that you've separately fried hard in butter till deep gold (mushrooms need high heat alone, else they go grey and limp). Add 50g grated parmesan and another knob of butter — that's the mantecatura, the silk-making finish. Cover, rest 2 min. It should spread slowly like lava on the plate, not stand up stiff.
tips: Fry the mushrooms in the second pan while the stock goes in — high heat, don't crowd them, salt only once golden.

title: Fish Tacos
description: Flaky barramundi, lettuce slaw, lime crema.
storage: Cooked fish keeps 1–2 days airtight in the fridge — flake it cold through a salad or reheat very gently. Store components separately; assembled tacos don't keep.
make_ahead: The crema and shredded lettuce can be made a few hours ahead and kept covered in the fridge; cook the fish just before eating.
leftovers: Flake leftover fish through a salad and use the leftover crema as the dressing.

substitutions.swap: any firm white fish — snapper, flathead or ling
sour cream
finely shredded cabbage holds its crunch even longer

steps.title: Pan-fry the fish
steps.content: Season fish with paprika, pan-fry 3 min each side.
steps.detail: Pat barramundi fillets dry with paper towel — wet fish won't form a crust. Dust both sides with smoked paprika, salt, pepper. Hot pan with a splash of oil, medium-high. Fillets in and don't touch for 3 min — let the spice crust form. Gentle flip with a fish spatula, another 3 min. Fish is done when it flakes easily with a fork and the flesh is opaque.
tips: If the fillet resists the spatula, it isn't ready — it releases on its own once the crust has formed.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife after handling the raw fish — the slaw and crema are eaten uncooked.

steps.title: Prep the slaw
steps.content: Shred lettuce.
steps.detail: Core the lettuce and slice thinly across the grain — ribbons work better than chunks in a taco. Iceberg or cos both work. Keep crisp in iced water if you're prepping ahead, then spin dry.
tips: A pinch of salt and a squeeze of lime through the shreds just before serving turns plain lettuce into a proper slaw.

steps.title: Whisk the crema
steps.content: Stir yoghurt with lime juice for crema.
steps.detail: In a bowl, combine half a cup of yoghurt with the juice of one lime, a pinch of salt, a crushed garlic clove if you want more kick. Whisk till smooth. This is your crema — cooling, tangy, cuts through the rich fish.
tips: Taste as you go — it should be tangy enough to make you blink.

steps.title: Warm tortillas and build
steps.content: Flake fish, fill warm tortillas, top with slaw and crema.
steps.detail: Warm tortillas on a dry pan or direct gas flame for 15 sec a side — you want pliable, with a few char spots. Keep them wrapped in a clean tea towel so they don't stiffen. Flake the fish into chunks with a fork. Pile fish in each tortilla, shredded lettuce, generous spoon of crema, a squeeze of extra lime, torn coriander. Two tortillas stacked holds messy fillings.
tips: Warm tortillas two at a time if your pan is big enough — the fish shouldn't sit around.

title: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato sauce. Weekend brunch.
equipment: large heavy frying pan with a lid (cast iron is ideal)
storage: Best eaten straight away — poached eggs don't reheat well. Leftover sauce (without eggs) keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge or freezes for 2 months.
make_ahead: The sauce keeps 3 days in the fridge — reheat it in the pan until bubbling, then crack in the eggs fresh.
leftovers: Leftover sauce is a head start on tomorrow's pasta sauce, or the base for another round of eggs.

substitutions.swap: any colour capsicum, or a big handful of halved cherry tomatoes
goat's cheese, or a dollop of plain yoghurt after cooking
toasted pita, flatbread or anything that scoops

steps.title: Soften the veg
steps.content: Soften onion, capsicum, garlic.
steps.detail: Slice onion and capsicum thinly. Cast iron or a heavy pan that can go straight to the table is ideal. Warm a glug of oil over medium, add onion and capsicum, cook 8 min till truly soft and starting to catch at the edges. Garlic (grated or minced) the last minute — burnt garlic ruins everything.
tips: Slice the capsicum about as thick as the onion so they soften at the same rate.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Add paprika, then tomatoes, simmer 10 min.
steps.detail: Stir paprika into the hot veg for 30 seconds to bloom its flavour. Tip in tomatoes, crush with a spoon. Season with salt, a pinch of sugar, cracked pepper. Simmer on low for 10 min till thick enough that a spoon leaves a trail through it.
tips: If the sauce spits, drop the heat — low and slow thickens it without redecorating the stovetop.

steps.title: Nestle in the eggs
steps.content: Make wells, crack eggs into them.
steps.detail: Use the back of a spoon to make little wells in the sauce — as many as eggs you have. Crack each egg into a small bowl first (so you can fish out any shell), then slide into its well. This lets the whites sit IN the sauce, not float on top.
tips: Season each egg with a little salt and pepper as it goes in.

steps.title: Cover and set the whites
steps.content: Cover, cook 5 min till whites set.
steps.detail: Lid on, low heat. 5 min for whites just-set with runny yolks — the goal. Check at 4 min — they keep cooking off the heat. If your pan has no lid, a large upside-down plate or foil works.
tips: Give the pan a gentle shake — set whites hold still while the yolks should still wobble.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.content: Crumble feta, serve with bread.
steps.detail: Dot feta between eggs — it softens in the warmth. Scatter parsley if you have it. Serve straight from the pan with crusty bread for scooping. This is finger food with cutlery.
tips: Warm or toast the bread while the eggs cook so everything lands together.

title: Pesto Pasta
description: Fresh basil, parmesan, garlic. Fifteen-minute dinner.
equipment: food processor or mortar and pestle
storage: Tossed pasta keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge — eat it cold or barely warmed, as hot reheating dulls the basil. Pesto on its own freezes well for 3 months.
make_ahead: Pesto keeps 3 days in a jar in the fridge with a thin film of oil over the top, or freeze it in ice-cube trays for months.
leftovers: Leftover pesto lifts scrambled eggs, sandwiches and roast potatoes.

substitutions.swap: cashews, walnuts or almonds — cheaper and still good
half basil, half baby spinach still tastes fresh and costs less
pecorino or grana padano — any hard grating cheese

steps.title: Blitz the pesto
steps.content: Blitz basil, garlic, pine nuts, parmesan, oil, lemon.
steps.detail: Into a food processor (or mortar and pestle for the purists): basil leaves (ideally sunny, not refrigerator-cold), a peeled garlic clove, a handful of pine nuts, grated parmesan, good olive oil, a squeeze of lemon, pinch of salt. Pulse rather than blitz constantly — you want a rough paste, not a smooth sludge. Taste — salt, more lemon, more cheese until it sings.
tips: Toast the pine nuts in a dry pan for a couple of minutes first — nuttier, and worth the extra dish.

steps.title: Boil the pasta
steps.content: Cook pasta, reserve a splash of water.
steps.detail: Big pot, heavily salted water. Cook 1 min less than the packet says — the residual heat finishes it. Before you drain, scoop out half a cup of pasta water.
tips: Short shapes with ridges or twists hold pesto better than smooth ones.

steps.title: Marry pasta and pesto
steps.content: Toss pasta with pesto off the heat.
steps.detail: KEY: off the heat. Hot pasta can cook the basil and dull the bright green. Drag pasta into the pesto with tongs, toss vigorously. Add splashes of reserved water as you go — the starch makes it loose and glossy rather than paste-clumped. More cheese on top, eat immediately while vivid.
tips: Save a spoonful of pesto to dollop on top at the end — the fresh hit lifts the whole bowl.

title: Chicken Schnitty
description: Pub classic. Crunchy crumb, squeeze of lemon.
equipment: rolling pin or heavy pan for flattening
storage: Cooked schnitty keeps 2–3 days airtight in the fridge; re-crisp in a 180°C oven for 10 min rather than the microwave, which turns the crumb soggy. Crumbed uncooked ones freeze for 2 months.
make_ahead: Crumb the schnitzels up to a day ahead and keep them loosely covered on a plate on the bottom shelf of the fridge — the coating actually sets better.
leftovers: A cold schnitty sandwich with mayo and lettuce is a genuine delicacy — or top with napoli sauce and cheese and grill it into a parmy.

substitutions.swap: panko for a shaggier, crunchier crust, or blitzed stale bread
pork loin steaks bashed thin work exactly the same way

steps.title: Butterfly and flatten
steps.content: Butterfly and bash chicken thin.
steps.detail: Lay breast flat, hold it down with one hand, slice horizontally through the middle to open it like a book — but don't cut all the way through. Open it out. Put between two sheets of baking paper and bash with a rolling pin or heavy pan till evenly 1cm thick. Even thickness = even cooking. Don't skip the paper; raw chicken shrapnel isn't the vibe.
tips: Bash from the centre outwards with flat, firm whacks — you're persuading it thinner, not punishing it.
warnings: Everything the raw chicken touches — board, paper, pin, hands — gets hot soapy water before you move on.

steps.title: Set up a crumbing line
steps.content: Flour, egg, crumb.
steps.detail: Three shallow plates: seasoned flour, 2 beaten eggs, breadcrumbs. Dredge each piece flour first (shake off excess — clumps go soggy), then egg (let drip), then press firmly into crumbs on both sides. Firm pressing = crumbs stick. Rest on a rack 5 min if you can — helps the crust set.
tips: Use one hand for dry and one for wet — otherwise you end up crumbing your own fingers.

steps.title: Shallow-fry until golden
steps.content: Shallow-fry in butter, 4 min each side.
steps.detail: Wide pan, medium heat, enough butter + oil to come 5mm up the sides (butter adds flavour, oil stops it burning). When a crumb sizzles on contact, lay schnitties in — don't crowd. 4 min per side till the crumb is deep gold and the chicken is 74°C internal. Drain on paper towel. The heat keeps climbing while they drain — fillets this thin finish past the 74°C safe mark by the time they reach the plate.
tips: If the crumb is browning well before 4 min, the heat's too high — the chicken inside needs the full time.
warnings: Lay each schnitty down away from you so hot fat splashes away from your arm, not toward it.

steps.title: Plate up hot
steps.content: Serve with lemon wedges.
steps.detail: Plate hot, lemon wedges on the side. Squeeze over at the table — waiting lemon goes floppy on the crumb. Classic sides: chippies, a slaw, or a big pile of peas with butter. Anchovy mayo or tartare if you're fancy.

title: Sweet Potato Soup
description: Velvety, warming, crowd-pleasing.
storage: Keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge or freezes for 3 months. Coconut milk can split slightly after freezing — whisk or blitz while reheating and it comes back together.
make_ahead: Even better the next day — make it up to 3 days ahead and reheat gently on the stove.
leftovers: Thin leftovers with stock for a second-night soup, or keep it thick and use it as a sauce for roast veg.

substitutions.swap: pumpkin — same weight, same method
150 ml cream or a good knob of butter (no longer vegan)
chicken stock if you're not keeping it vegetarian
2 tsp curry powder for a spiced version

steps.title: Sweat the aromatics
steps.content: Soften onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Finely dice a brown onion and grate 2 garlic cloves. Glug of olive oil in a heavy pot over medium. Onion with a pinch of salt, 6 min till translucent. Garlic for the last minute — any longer and it burns at the base of the pot. Bitter garlic = bitter soup.
tips: A lid half-on speeds the softening without letting the onion colour.

steps.title: Coat the veg in cumin
steps.content: Add cubed sweet potato and cumin.
steps.detail: Peel sweet potato (or leave skin on — it's fine and nutritious) and cube into 3cm chunks. Even size = even cooking. Tip into the softened onion with a teaspoon of cumin. Stir 2 min to let the cumin wake up and coat the veg with oil.
tips: Halve the sweet potato flat-side down before cubing so it can't roll on the board.

steps.title: Simmer till tender
steps.content: Pour in stock, simmer 20 min.
steps.detail: Pour in a litre of vegetable stock — should just cover the veg. Bring to a boil, drop to a simmer, lid slightly ajar. 20 min till sweet potato falls apart when prodded with a knife. If it's not completely tender, don't blitz yet — you'll end up with lumps.
tips: Skim any foam that rises early in the simmer for a cleaner-tasting soup.

steps.title: Blitz in the coconut milk
steps.content: Blitz with coconut milk until silky.
steps.detail: Off heat. Add 200ml coconut milk. Stick blender straight in the pot — safest method, no transfers. Blitz 2 min till silky and velvety. If using a jug blender, work in batches and cover the lid with a tea towel (steam pressure pops lids). Taste — salt, pepper, a squeeze of lime lifts it. Reheat gently if needed.
tips: Too thick after blitzing? Loosen with hot water rather than more coconut milk so the flavour stays balanced.
warnings: Keep the stick blender head fully submerged before switching on — hot soup spray burns.

title: Prawn Linguine
description: Garlic, chilli, white wine, parsley.
storage: Seafood pasta keeps 1 day airtight in the fridge at most — reheat gently with a splash of water just until hot. Don't freeze it; prawns turn rubbery.

substitutions.swap: extra pasta water plus a squeeze more lemon
frozen raw prawns are great — thaw fully and pat very dry
spaghetti or fettuccine

steps.title: Boil the linguine
steps.content: Cook pasta.
steps.detail: Big pot of heavily salted water, rolling boil. Cook linguine 2 min less than packet — it'll finish in the sauce. Before draining, scoop a full cup of pasta water. That starchy liquid is the sauce's secret weapon for glossy clinginess.
tips: Start the sauce as soon as the pasta goes in — both finish at about the same time.

steps.title: Warm the garlic oil
steps.content: Sizzle garlic and chilli in oil.
steps.detail: Wide pan, good glug of olive oil, 4 garlic cloves thinly sliced, one fresh chilli sliced (or a pinch of flakes). Medium-LOW heat, not high. You want gentle sizzle for 2 min till garlic goes pale gold and fragrant — never dark. Dark garlic is bitter and ruins everything.
tips: Starting the garlic in cold oil gives you far more control than dropping it into a hot pan.

steps.title: Sear prawns, splash wine
steps.content: Add prawns, cook 2 min, deglaze with wine.
steps.detail: Crank heat to medium-high. Pat prawns dry, tip in. They should sizzle. 60 sec one side till pink and curling, flip, 60 sec. Pour in half a cup of white wine — it'll bubble hard. Cook 90 sec till it reduces by half and loses its boozy sharpness.
tips: Overcooked prawns curl into tight O's — pull them while they're still a loose C shape.
warnings: Keep raw-prawn juices off anything you'll serve — wash hands and tongs before plating.

steps.title: Toss and finish
steps.content: Toss with pasta, parsley, lemon.
steps.detail: Drain pasta straight into the pan with a big splash of reserved water. Torn parsley, juice of half a lemon, toss hard with tongs for 60 sec — water emulsifies with oil into a light sauce that cloaks every strand. Crack of pepper, eat immediately. No cheese with seafood pasta in Italy — prawns have their own sweetness.
tips: Done right, the sauce clings to every strand with barely a puddle left in the pan.

title: Moroccan Lamb
description: Slow-spiced lamb with chickpeas, couscous.
equipment: heatproof bowl with a plate or lid (for the couscous)
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze the lamb (without couscous) for up to 3 months. Reheat until piping hot with a splash of water to loosen.
make_ahead: The lamb tastes even better the next day — make the whole pot up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently while the couscous soaks.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers into a pita with a dollop of yoghurt, or over a baked sweet potato.

substitutions.swap: beef mince works, though lamb's richness suits the spices better
sultanas, or chopped dried apricots
rice or quinoa, cooked per the packet

steps.title: Brown the mince in batches
steps.content: Brown the lamb mince.
steps.detail: Heat a glug of oil in a heavy pot until shimmering. Add the lamb mince and let it brown properly in batches, breaking up the clumps with a spoon — crowding the pot steams the meat instead of colouring it, and that deep brown crust is where the flavour comes from. Season with salt and pepper, then tip each batch out and set aside.
tips: The sizzle goes quiet while the water cooks off — real browning only starts once you hear it crackle again.
warnings: Wash hands, board and spoon after handling raw mince — keep it clear of anything served raw.

steps.title: Soften and spice the onions
steps.content: Soften onions with spices.
steps.detail: Drop heat to medium. Tip in two finely diced brown onions with a pinch of salt. Scrape up any browned bits (fond) stuck to the pot — that's gravy gold. 8 min till soft. Stir in 2 tsp cumin and 1 tsp cinnamon for 30 sec to bloom the spices.
tips: If the fond starts going from brown to black, splash in a little water and keep scraping.

steps.title: Build the braise
steps.content: Return lamb, add tomato, chickpeas, raisins, stock.
steps.detail: Lamb and any juices back in. Tip in a tin of tomatoes (crush with your hand), a tin of drained chickpeas, the raisins for a gentle sweetness, and 500 ml chicken stock. Stir and scrape the base so nothing catches.
tips: Half-fill the empty tomato tin with water and swish it into the pot — no flavour left behind.

steps.title: Simmer low and slow
steps.content: Simmer 35 min, lid off the last 10.
steps.detail: Bring to a gentle simmer, lid on, on the lowest heat for 25 min so the spices marry into the mince. Then lid off, nudge the heat up, and reduce for 10 min to a thick, glossy sauce that clings. Taste for salt — slow-cooked spice often needs a final pinch.
tips: Raisins sink and can catch — scrape the base when you stir during the uncovered stretch.

steps.title: Serve over couscous
steps.content: Scatter coriander.
steps.detail: Off heat. Tear a big handful of coriander over the top. Serve over couscous (cover dry couscous in equal hot water, rest 10 min, fluff) with a dollop of yoghurt. Harissa or chilli flakes for anyone who wants heat.
tips: Get the couscous soaking when the simmer has about 10 minutes to go, so everything lands hot together.

title: Breakfast Frittata
description: Sunday clean-out the fridge eggs.
equipment: oven-safe frying pan (cast iron is ideal)
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; eat cold or reheat gently in a low oven. Best not frozen — the potato and spinach go watery.
make_ahead: Par-boil the potatoes up to a day ahead and keep them in the fridge — breakfast comes together twice as fast.
leftovers: A cold wedge in a bread roll with chutney is a proper lunch.

substitutions.swap: goat's cheese, or grated cheddar for something milder
silverbeet (stems out), or thawed frozen spinach squeezed very dry
leftover cooked potatoes — skip the par-boil entirely

steps.title: Start the potatoes
steps.content: Par-boil diced potato.
steps.detail: Dice two potatoes into 1cm cubes. Into a pot of cold salted water (starting cold cooks them evenly). Bring to a boil, simmer 5 min — just till a knife goes through with slight resistance. They'll finish in the pan. Drain and pat dry; wet potato won't crisp.
tips: Same-size cubes cook at the same speed — take an extra 30 seconds with the knife.

steps.title: Crisp the potato base
steps.content: Fry onion and potato until golden.
steps.detail: Good glug of oil in an oven-safe pan (cast iron ideal) over medium-high. Tip in the par-boiled potatoes and a finely diced brown onion. Spread flat and leave 3 min for deep gold crust before tossing. Total 8 min till golden all over and onion is soft.
tips: If the onion browns faster than the potato, drop the heat a notch — it should soften, not scorch.

steps.title: Wilt the spinach
steps.content: Add spinach to wilt.
steps.detail: Two big handfuls of spinach straight into the hot pan. Toss with tongs — it'll wilt in 60 sec from two handfuls to a small pile. Surprising, but that's spinach for you.
tips: If the pan looks dry after the spinach, another small glug of oil stops the eggs sticking later.

steps.title: Add eggs and feta
steps.content: Pour over beaten eggs, dot with feta.
steps.detail: Beat 8 eggs with salt, pepper, splash of milk if you like a lighter set. Pour over the potato mix — don't stir; let the eggs pool and settle. Crumble feta on top in rough chunks. 3 min over medium till the edges are setting but the top is still wobbly.
tips: No oven-safe handle? Wrap it in a double layer of foil before it goes under the grill.

steps.title: Grill and rest
steps.content: Finish under grill 5 min till puffed.
steps.detail: Straight under a hot grill for 5 min till puffed, set, and deep gold on top. Watch it — grills go fast. Rest 5 min in the pan before cutting into wedges. Eats hot, warm, or cold from the fridge tomorrow for lunch.
tips: Sit the pan about 10 cm below the element — too close and the top browns before the middle sets.
warnings: The pan handle comes out screaming hot — drape a tea towel or mitt over it as a reminder.

title: Steak & Chips
description: Hot pan, butter, a pile of chips. No frills.
equipment: heavy frying pan (cast iron is ideal)
storage: Leftover steak keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge — slice thinly and eat cold, or flash it through a hot pan. Chips go soggy; re-crisp in a 200°C oven for 8–10 minutes. Don't freeze.
leftovers: Thin-sliced cold steak with mustard in a crusty roll beats any deli sandwich.

substitutions.swap: rosemary — one sprig, it's stronger
sweet potato chips cook in about 20 minutes but won't crisp as hard

steps.title: Get the chips roasting
steps.content: Roast chip-cut potatoes 25 min.
steps.detail: Cut potatoes into 1cm-thick chips, skin on. Toss on a tray with 3 tbsp oil, salt, pepper. Spread in a single layer — crowded chips steam. 220°C oven, 25 min, flipping once halfway. They're done when deeply gold with crispy edges and tender middles. Time this to finish just as the steaks rest.
tips: Heat the empty tray in the oven while you cut — chips hitting hot metal start crisping straight away.

steps.title: Temper and season the steaks
steps.content: Pat steaks dry, season.
steps.detail: Take steaks out of the fridge 30 min before cooking — cold meat cooks unevenly. Pat BONE dry with paper towel, both sides. Surface moisture steams; we want searing. Season generously with sea salt flakes and cracked pepper, pressing it in. Don't oil the steak; oil the pan.
warnings: Wash hands and anything the raw steak touched before you handle other food.

steps.title: Sear and baste
steps.content: Blazing hot pan, 3 min a side, butter basted.
steps.detail: Heavy pan (cast iron ideal) on the highest flame you've got till you see wisps of smoke. Splash of oil, steaks in. Don't touch for 3 min — let the crust form. Flip once. After flipping, add a knob of butter, smashed garlic, thyme. Tilt the pan and spoon the foaming butter over the top repeatedly for 2 min. For 2cm thick medium-rare.
tips: A meat thermometer takes out the guesswork: 52–55°C in the centre for medium-rare, probed side-on.
Run the rangehood or crack a window — a proper sear makes smoke.
warnings: Foaming butter can spit — tilt the pan away from you as you baste.

steps.title: Rest, slice, serve
steps.content: Rest 5 min. Serve with chips.
steps.detail: Lift steaks onto a board, tent loosely with foil. 5 min rest is NON-negotiable — juices redistribute into the meat instead of pooling on the board. Slice against the grain (perpendicular to muscle fibres) into thick slabs. Chips alongside, flaky salt, pan butter spooned over.
tips: Pour any board juices back over the sliced steak — that's free sauce.

title: Veggie Wrap
description: Lunch for humans who hate the sad desk salad.
storage: Best eaten straight away — an assembled wrap goes soggy within a few hours. Cooked haloumi keeps 2 days in the fridge but turns rubbery cold; re-warm it in a dry pan.

substitutions.swap: slices of firm tofu, pressed dry and pan-fried the same way
baby spinach, or shredded iceberg for crunch
a smear of hummus if you're out

steps.title: Fry the haloumi
steps.content: Grill haloumi slices.
