steps.detail: Slide onto a plate pleated-side up so the crispy bases face you. Dipping sauce: equal parts soy and rice vinegar (or balsamic at a pinch) with a few drops of sesame oil and chilli oil. Eat with chopsticks while hot — the crispy base softens as they sit.

title: Chicken Fajitas
description: Sizzling peppers and chicken, warm tortillas, let everyone build their own.
storage: Cooked chicken and veg keep 3 days airtight in the fridge; reheat hard and fast in a hot pan. Store tortillas and toppings separately — cut avocado only lasts a day, even with lime.
make_ahead: Slice the chicken and veg in the morning and keep them covered in the fridge; toss the chicken with the spices and lime only 10–30 minutes before cooking.
leftovers: Pile leftovers into a tortilla with cheese and toast it for a next-day quesadilla, or serve over rice as a burrito bowl.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — slice slightly thicker and pull it the moment it's cooked through
plain Greek yoghurt
skip it, or smash frozen peas with lime for a budget stand-in

steps.title: Slice everything thin
steps.content: Slice chicken and veg into fajita strips.
steps.detail: Slice chicken thighs across the grain into 1cm strips, 7cm long — fajita-style. Slice capsicums and onion into matching long strips, same thickness. Uniform size means everything cooks at the same speed and looks proper piled in a tortilla.
tips: Cold-from-the-fridge chicken is firmer and slices more cleanly.
warnings: Use a separate board for the raw chicken, or wash it well before the veg goes on.

steps.title: Spice the chicken
steps.content: Toss chicken with spices, lime juice.
steps.detail: Big bowl: chicken strips, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp paprika, salt, pepper, the juice of a lime. Toss till every strip is coated. 10 min while you prep the veg — the acid starts tenderising the meat.
tips: Don't leave it past 30 minutes — lime juice starts to 'cook' and toughen the surface of the meat.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Blast in very hot pan 5 min, remove.
steps.detail: Biggest pan you own, smoking-hot. Splash of oil. Chicken in a single layer, don't touch for 2 min for sear. Then toss fast till cooked through, 3 more min. Crowding steams instead of sears — work in two batches if needed. Lift out with tongs.
tips: Get the rangehood going before you start — a proper sear at this heat makes smoke, and that's correct.
warnings: Check the fattest strip before it comes out — no pink in the middle, juices running clear.

steps.title: Blister the veg
steps.content: Char peppers and onion 4 min.
steps.detail: Same blazing pan, more oil. Capsicum strips and onion wedges in, don't touch for 90 sec for char marks. Then toss 2 min more — you want some char patches with crunch, not limp veg. Fajita means 'strips', and that smoky char is the whole point.
tips: Leave a little of the root end on the onion wedges and they'll hold together in the pan.

steps.title: Bring it back together
steps.content: Return chicken, toss.
steps.detail: Chicken back in with any resting juices, toss 30 sec to warm through and let the flavours marry. Squeeze of more lime. Serve STRAIGHT from the sizzling pan — it's the classic presentation, and it keeps the heat in.

steps.title: Warm tortillas and build
steps.content: Serve with tortillas, sour cream, avocado, coriander.
steps.detail: Warm tortillas 15 sec a side on a dry pan. Let everyone build their own at the table: tortilla, chicken and veg, sliced avocado, sour cream, torn coriander, hot sauce. Extra lime wedges. Build, roll, eat, repeat.
tips: Stack warmed tortillas under a clean tea towel — they'll stay soft and hot for 10 minutes.

title: Cheesy Quesadillas
description: Molten cheese, charred tortilla, lazy-dinner glory. Fifteen minutes top to bottom.
storage: Cooked quesadillas keep 2 days airtight in the fridge; re-crisp in a dry pan, never the microwave. Not worth freezing.
make_ahead: Fold the quesadillas an hour or two ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — they go into the dry pan straight from cold.
leftovers: Chop a leftover wedge over a fried egg for a very good breakfast.

substitutions.swap: tinned corn, or any leftover roast veg chopped small
all cheddar works — you lose a bit of stretch, not flavour
the green tops of the spring onions if coriander's not your thing

steps.title: Prep the fillings
steps.content: Finely slice spring onion and capsicum.
steps.detail: Slice 2 spring onions thinly (whites and greens) and cut one red capsicum into small 5mm dice. Small dice matters — large chunks pop out when you fold and bite. Have cheese pre-grated (sharp cheddar + mozzarella blend melts best).
tips: Grate from a block if you can — bagged pre-grated cheese is dusted with starch and melts less smoothly.

steps.title: Fill and fold
steps.content: Scatter cheese + veg over half a tortilla, fold.
steps.detail: Lay a tortilla flat. Scatter a generous handful of grated cheese over one half, then the capsicum and spring onion on top, then another handful of cheese (cheese both sides = glue that holds everything together). Fold the empty half over — half-moon shape.
tips: Leave a 1 cm border bare at the edge — cheese to the rim leaks the moment it melts.

steps.title: Toast till molten
steps.content: Cook in a dry pan till gold and melted, flip, 2 min a side.
steps.detail: Dry pan (no oil — the cheese creates its own seal), medium heat. Quesadilla in. 2 min first side till deep gold and you can hear cheese sizzling against the pan. Press gently with a spatula to help the bonding. Flip carefully with a wide spatula — 2 min the other side. Cheese should be visibly bubbling out the sides.
tips: Two fit in a big pan at once — point the folded edges to the centre and flip them away from each other.

steps.title: Rest and slice
steps.content: Slice into wedges, top with coriander.
steps.detail: Slide onto a board, rest 60 sec (molten cheese burns tongues). Sharp knife, slice into wedges like a pizza. Tear coriander over.

steps.title: Set out the dip
steps.content: Sour cream for dipping.
steps.detail: Small bowl of sour cream for dipping — a splash of hot sauce stirred through is a good move. Guacamole or salsa alongside if you've got them. Eat hot, otherwise the cheese sets into a solid brick.

title: Palak Paneer
description: Vivid spinach sauce, golden cubes of paneer, warm naan. Indian date-night hero.
equipment: pot with a lid for the rice
storage: The curry keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge (cooked rice only 1 day); reheat gently without boiling or the cream can split. Freezes for 2 months, though the green dulls a little.
make_ahead: The spinach-tomato base keeps 2 days in the fridge — fry the paneer fresh and fold it in with the cream as you reheat.
leftovers: Fold leftovers through hot rice with a fried egg on top, or use as the filling for a toasted wrap.

substitutions.swap: firm tofu, pressed very dry and fried the same way
coconut cream — lighter, and dairy-free apart from the paneer
frozen spinach — thaw and squeeze dry, and skip the blanching step

steps.title: Blanch and blitz spinach
steps.content: Blanch spinach 1 min, blitz to a paste.
steps.detail: Big pot of water to a rolling boil, salted. 400g spinach in for EXACTLY 60 sec — you want vivid green, wilted not stewed. Drain straight into a bowl of iced water to shock-stop the cooking. Squeeze dry in a tea towel (the water is bitter). Blitz with 2 tbsp water to a rough paste. This technique keeps colour and flavour.
tips: Set up the iced water before the spinach hits the pot — 60 seconds goes very fast.

steps.title: Fry the paneer
steps.content: Cube and pan-fry paneer till gold, set aside.
steps.detail: Cube 250g paneer into 2cm pieces. Pat very dry — wet paneer won't crust. Hot pan, splash of oil or ghee, medium-high. Cubes in a single layer, don't touch for 90 sec per side till deep gold all around. Remove to a plate. Paneer doesn't melt so you can really commit to the sear.
tips: If the cubes stick, wait — paneer lets go of the pan on its own once a crust forms.
warnings: Damp paneer spits in hot oil — pat the cubes properly dry first.

steps.title: Bloom the spices
steps.content: Soften onion, garlic, ginger with spices.
steps.detail: Same pan. Drop heat to medium. Splash of oil. One finely diced onion, 3 grated garlic cloves, a thumb of grated ginger, 6 min till soft. Then 1 tsp cumin, 1/2 tsp turmeric, pinch of chilli, cook 30 sec till fragrant — the spices bloom in the hot oil.
tips: Grate the garlic and ginger straight in on a microplane — quicker, and none of the juice is lost.

steps.title: Build the green sauce
steps.content: Add tomato, simmer 10 min, then stir in spinach.
steps.detail: Tip in a tin of tomatoes (crush with your hand). Simmer 10 min till thickened and the raw tomato edge is gone. Stir in the spinach paste. 3 min to warm through — don't cook long once spinach is in or it loses the bright green.
tips: The base is ready for the spinach when a dragged spoon leaves a track that holds for a second.

steps.title: Bring it together
steps.content: Fold in cream and paneer, warm through.
steps.detail: Pour in 100ml cream. Slide paneer back in. Stir gently — paneer is delicate and will crumble. 3 min on low heat to warm everything and let flavours marry. Don't boil once cream's in — can split. Taste — salt, a squeeze of lemon if it's flat.

steps.title: Plate up with rice
steps.content: Serve over basmati.
steps.detail: Fluff basmati. Ladle palak paneer into bowls, rice alongside. Warm naan (or flatbread) for scooping. A swirl of extra cream on top is optional but pretty. Torn coriander scattered.

title: Chana Masala
description: Spiced chickpeas that get better on day two. Pantry staple hero.
equipment: food processor (or box grater and knife)
large heavy frying pan or saucepan
medium saucepan with lid for the rice
storage: The curry keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge and freezes for 3 months; reheat till piping hot. Cooked rice is a 1-day keeper — cool it fast and get it in the fridge.
make_ahead: Like most curries it's better tomorrow — make it up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently with a splash of water. Cook the rice fresh.
leftovers: Spoon leftovers over toast with a fried egg, or roll into a wrap with a swipe of yoghurt for lunch.

substitutions.swap: garam masala with an extra 1/2 tsp cumin — a touch sweeter but just as good
any long-grain rice, or skip it and scoop with flatbread
1 tsp ground ginger stirred in with the spices at a pinch

steps.title: Blitz the aromatic paste
steps.content: Blitz onion, garlic, ginger to paste.
steps.detail: Food processor (or a sharp knife with patience): one brown onion roughly chopped, 3 garlic cloves, a thumb of ginger. Blitz to a rough paste. Not smooth — you want texture. This paste fries down into a richly flavoured base. Prep saves faff mid-cook.
tips: No processor? A box grater does onion and ginger fast — grate straight into a bowl, then crush the garlic in.

steps.title: Fry down the paste
steps.content: Fry paste with spices till fragrant.
steps.detail: Glug of oil in a heavy pan over medium-high. Tip in the paste and stir almost constantly for 8 min — it goes from wet to dry to deeply golden-brown. This is bhunao, the frying-down that's the backbone of Indian cooking. Don't rush it; patience here = flavour later. Then 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp turmeric, 2 tbsp curry powder. Stir 60 sec.
tips: If the paste starts catching, a splash of water lifts it off the pan — then keep frying.
warnings: Ground spices burn in seconds — the 60-second stir is enough, then get the tomatoes in.

steps.title: Simmer the tomato base
steps.content: Add tomato, simmer 10 min.
steps.detail: Tip in a tin of tomatoes (crush with your hand). Scrape up any stuck paste — that's all flavour. Simmer 10 min till thickened and the raw tomato edge is gone. Oil may separate and pool on top — traditional sign that the base is properly cooked.
tips: Whole tinned tomatoes taste better than pre-diced here — crush them straight into the pan.
warnings: Tomatoes spit when they hit the hot paste — tip them in away from you.

steps.title: Simmer the chickpeas
steps.content: Tip in chickpeas + liquid, simmer 15 min.
steps.detail: Tip in 2 drained-and-rinsed tins of chickpeas PLUS about 1/2 cup of their tin liquid (aquafaba — gives body to the sauce). Bare simmer, 15 min for flavours to soak into the chickpeas. Season well. A splash of water if it gets too thick.
tips: Get the basmati on now — it'll be ready right when the curry is.
A bare simmer is a few lazy bubbles — a hard boil drives off the liquid before the flavour soaks in.

steps.title: Mash to thicken
steps.content: Mash a few chickpeas to thicken.
steps.detail: Scoop out about 1/4 cup of the chickpeas, mash them with a fork, and stir back into the pot. This thickens the sauce and binds it without a cornflour slurry. Old Indian grandma trick.
tips: A potato masher pressed straight into the pot works too — three or four presses is plenty.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Serve over rice with coriander.
steps.detail: Taste — should be deeply savoury with a citrusy edge. Squeeze of lemon if it's flat. Ladle over basmati rice, torn coriander, optional dollop of yoghurt to cool. Flatbread alongside. Leftovers are even better day two — the flavours keep developing.
tips: Salt in stages and taste as you go — chickpeas drink up more seasoning than you'd expect.

title: Quick Chicken Biryani
description: Saffron-stained rice, marinated chicken, caramelised onion. A celebration in a pot.
equipment: large heavy-bottomed pot with a tight-fitting lid
storage: Because of the rice, cool leftovers quickly, fridge within the hour and eat within a day, reheated till piping hot. Skip the freezer once it's assembled.
make_ahead: Marinate the chicken the night before — the yoghurt tenderises it overnight. The crispy onions can also be fried a day ahead and kept airtight.
leftovers: Fry a leftover portion in a little oil till the rice crisps at the edges — day-two biryani has its fans for a reason.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast works but check it early — it dries out faster than thigh
1/2 tsp ground cinnamon stirred in with the curry powder
extra coriander if mint is hard to find

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Marinate chicken in yoghurt + spices, 15 min.
steps.detail: Cube chicken thighs into 3cm pieces. Big bowl with 1 cup yoghurt, 2 tbsp curry powder, 1 tsp turmeric, 3 grated garlic cloves, a thumb of grated ginger, big pinch of salt. Mix till coated. Minimum 15 min; overnight is transformative — yoghurt tenderises, spices penetrate.
tips: The salt matters as much as the spices — it's the only chance seasoning gets to reach the centre of each piece.
warnings: Wash hands, board and anything the raw chicken or its marinade touched before moving on.

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.content: Fry sliced onions till deep brown, set aside.
steps.detail: Heavy-bottomed pot with a tight lid (critical for biryani). Heat 3 tbsp oil, thinly slice 2 onions. Fry over medium-high, stirring often, 15 min till DEEPLY brown (not just gold — properly caramelised). These crispy onions are the garnish and flavour both. Reserve half on paper towel.
tips: They'll look done at 8 minutes — keep going. The last few minutes take them from gold to the deep brown that makes biryani.

steps.title: Sear the marinated chicken
steps.content: Brown chicken in the same pot.
steps.detail: Same pot (don't wash — residual oil flavoured with onion is good). Medium-high heat. Tip in marinated chicken with all its yoghurt. Sear 5 min — it'll sizzle and brown. A cinnamon stick, 2 bay leaves if you have them.
tips: The yoghurt will catch a little on the pot base — that browned layer is flavour, not a mistake.

steps.title: Layer the rinsed rice
steps.content: Rinse rice, layer on top of chicken.
steps.detail: Rinse 2 cups of basmati till the water runs clear — washes surface starch for separate grains. Layer on top of the chicken WITHOUT stirring. This layering is what makes it biryani vs stew.
tips: Use the back of a spoon to spread the rice level without pressing it down into the chicken.

steps.title: Add water and onions
steps.content: Pour 3 cups water over, season.
steps.detail: Pour 3 cups of water (or stock) gently down the side of the pot — don't disturb the rice layer. Big pinch of salt. A scatter of half the reserved crispy onions across the rice top. The water level should just cover the rice.
tips: Salt and taste the water before it goes in — the chicken below isn't cooked through yet, so don't taste from the pot. Bland water, bland rice.

steps.title: Steam and rest
steps.content: Cover tight, cook 18 min on low, rest 10.
steps.detail: Cover with a tight lid (lay a tea towel under the lid for extra seal — dum cooking). Bring to a boil, then drop to LOWEST heat for 18 min. Off heat, DO NOT LIFT THE LID for another 10 min — this rest is crucial; steam finishes the rice.
tips: Use your smallest burner on its lowest setting — the pot should whisper, not hiss.
warnings: If you use the tea-towel trick, fold the corners up on top of the lid — dangling edges near a flame can catch.

steps.title: Fluff and garnish
steps.content: Fluff, top with fried onion, herbs.
steps.detail: Lift the lid — the smell is the reward. Fluff gently with a fork, bringing some chicken up from underneath without mashing rice. Scatter remaining crispy onions, torn mint and coriander across the top. Lemon wedges. A bowl of yoghurt on the side with cucumber is classic raita. Check a piece of chicken from the bottom before serving — it should reach 74°C at the centre with no pink. If in doubt, lid back on over low heat for another 5 minutes.

title: Falafel Bowl with Garlic Yoghurt
description: Crunchy chickpea patties, grains, pickled veg, creamy sauce. Meal-prep friendly.
storage: Cooked falafel keep 3 days airtight in the fridge and freeze for 3 months; re-crisp in a 180°C oven for 10 minutes. Keep the sauce separate and don't dress the rocket until serving.
make_ahead: Blitz and shape the patties up to a day ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — they fry better after a proper chill. The quinoa and sauce keep 3 days.
leftovers: Stuff leftover falafel into a warm wrap with the garlic sauce, or crumble them over a salad.

substitutions.swap: couscous or brown rice — anything that makes a base
baby spinach or shredded lettuce
chickpea flour (besan) keeps it gluten-free and adds flavour

steps.title: Blitz the falafel mix
steps.content: Blitz drained chickpeas with garlic, onion, herbs, cumin, flour.
steps.detail: Food processor: 2 drained-and-rinsed tins of chickpeas, 3 garlic cloves, half a red onion roughly chopped, big handful of parsley, big handful of coriander, 2 tsp cumin, 1/4 cup flour, pinch of chilli, salt. Blitz in pulses till a rough paste — NOT smooth. Texture matters; smooth falafel is sad hummus in a ball. Fridge 15 min to firm up.
tips: Dry the chickpeas well after rinsing — a wet mix needs extra flour and fries up soggy.

steps.title: Shape the patties
steps.content: Shape into small patties.
steps.detail: Scoop heaped tablespoons and shape into flat patties (or balls). Flat cooks faster and crisps more edges. 12–14 patties roughly. If the mix is too wet to shape, extra spoon of flour. Too dry and crumbly, a splash of water.
tips: Wet hands stop the mix sticking while you shape.

steps.title: Fry till deep gold
steps.content: Pan-fry in oil till gold, 3 min a side.
steps.detail: Wide pan, 1cm of oil, medium-high heat. When a crumb sizzles on contact, lower patties in carefully. Don't touch for 3 min — you want deep gold crust. Flip once. 3 more min. Drain on a rack (not paper — steams the undersides soft).
tips: If a test patty breaks up in the oil, work another spoonful of flour through the mix.
Adjust the heat between batches — the oil runs hotter as the pan empties.
warnings: Lower the patties in away from you — hot oil splashes are no joke.

steps.title: Simmer the quinoa
steps.content: Cook quinoa.
steps.detail: Rinse 1 cup quinoa in a sieve for 30 sec (washes off saponin which tastes soapy). Pot with 2 cups water, pinch salt, lid on. Boil, drop to low, 15 min. Off heat 5 min covered. Fluff.
tips: It's done when the grains turn translucent and show a little white spiral, and the water's gone.

steps.title: Whisk the garlic sauce
steps.content: Whisk yoghurt with lemon, garlic for sauce.
steps.detail: Small bowl: 1/2 cup yoghurt, juice of a lemon, 1 grated garlic clove, pinch of salt, pinch of cumin. Whisk till smooth. Too thick? Splash of water. Taste — should be tangy and garlicky.
tips: Make it 10 minutes ahead if you can — the raw garlic mellows as it sits.

steps.title: Assemble the bowls
steps.content: Build bowls: quinoa, rocket, cucumber, falafel, sauce.
steps.detail: Deep bowls. Quinoa as the base, rocket on one side, cucumber batons tumbled across, 3 falafel perched on top. Generous spoon of sauce drizzled over everything. Pickled turnip or onion if you have them. Flatbread on the side — the bowl is good but the wrap version is better.

title: Chicken Shawarma Wraps
description: Spice-rubbed chicken, garlic sauce, pickled veg, all rolled tight.
equipment: heavy frying pan or barbecue grill
storage: Cooked chicken keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge; reheat till piping hot or eat it cold in a wrap. Keep salad and sauce separate — assembled wraps go soggy within the hour.
make_ahead: Marinate the chicken the night before — the yoghurt does its best work overnight. The garlic sauce keeps 3 days in the fridge too.
leftovers: Leftover chicken makes a great loaded salad bowl with the same sauce, or a next-day toasted wrap.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — pound it even first and watch it closely, it cooks faster
pita pockets or any flatbread
regular sweet paprika — you lose the smoke but it still works

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.content: Mix half yoghurt with spices, lemon, garlic.
steps.detail: In a big bowl: half a cup of yoghurt, 2 tsp cumin, 1 tbsp paprika, 1 tsp turmeric, 4 grated garlic cloves, juice of one lemon, olive oil, pepper, big pinch of salt. Whisk smooth. Vivid orange-yellow and fragrant — that's the shawarma marinade. The yoghurt tenderises while the spices colour and flavour.
tips: Zest the lemon in before juicing if you want extra punch — the fragrant oils live in the skin.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Marinate chicken 30 min or overnight.
steps.detail: Tip chicken thighs straight into the marinade, toss with your hands to coat every piece thoroughly. 30 min minimum; overnight is transformative — yoghurt's lactic acid gently breaks down the muscle fibres. Cover and fridge.
tips: Leave the thighs whole — they stay juicier on the heat and you're slicing after cooking anyway.
warnings: Your hands have touched raw chicken — wash them and anything the marinade dripped on before moving on.

steps.title: Char the chicken
steps.content: Grill or pan-fry till charred, 4 min a side.
steps.detail: Hot pan, splash of oil over medium-high (or BBQ grill). Shake excess marinade off each piece, lay in pan. Don't touch for 4 min — you want deeply charred patches that mimic spinning-vertical-spit authenticity. Flip once, 4 min. 75°C internal at the thickest part. Rest 5 min.
tips: Don't scrape off every scrap of marinade — a thin coat chars into the crust; a thick coat just burns.
warnings: Bin the used marinade — it's had raw chicken sitting in it and can't be reused as a sauce.

steps.title: Slice across the grain
steps.content: Slice thin.
steps.detail: On a board, slice chicken thinly across the grain — thin slices soak up sauce better and look more authentic than chunks. Pour any resting juices over the slices; don't waste them.
tips: A long sharp knife and a gentle sawing motion keep the slices thin and tidy.

steps.title: Whisk the garlic sauce
steps.content: Stir remaining yoghurt with garlic, lemon.
steps.detail: Small bowl: remaining half cup yoghurt, 2 grated garlic cloves, juice of half a lemon, splash of olive oil, pinch of salt. Whisk smooth. This is tangy garlic sauce, the traditional shawarma partner — cools the spicy chicken and adds brightness.
tips: Taste it — it should bite with garlic. It mellows against the spiced chicken.

steps.title: Roll the wraps
steps.content: Warm wraps, fill with chicken, salad, sauce.
steps.detail: Warm flatbreads 15 sec a side on a dry pan. Smear sauce down the middle, pile sliced chicken over, handful of shredded lettuce, diced tomato and cucumber, thinly sliced red onion, torn parsley. Roll tight, cut in half on the bias. Eat over a plate — it gets messy.
tips: Fold the bottom up first, then roll in from one side — the fold stops the fillings escaping out the end.

title: Classic Meat Pie
description: Puff lid, rich mince filling. A servo staple made properly.
equipment: large heavy frying pan or saucepan
baking tray for cooling the filling
storage: Baked pie keeps 3 days in the fridge; reheat in a 180°C oven for about 20 minutes — the microwave turns the pastry soft. Freezes well for 3 months, baked or unbaked.
make_ahead: The filling can be made up to 2 days ahead and kept in the fridge — it has to be fully cold before it meets the pastry anyway. The whole pie can be assembled and frozen unbaked.
leftovers: A cold wedge is a proper lunchbox move — or reheat a slice and add mash and peas for a second dinner.

substitutions.swap: soy sauce with a dash of vinegar gets you close
chicken stock, or 300 ml water plus a beef stock cube
shortcrust for the base if you like a sturdier bottom — keep puff for the lid

steps.title: Brown the mince base
steps.content: Brown mince, add onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Hot heavy pan, splash of oil. Crumble 700g mince in in big chunks and don't touch for 3 min — let a deep brown crust form. Then break up with a wooden spoon. Tip in one finely diced brown onion and 2 grated garlic cloves. Cook 6 min till onion is soft and mince is well-browned. Season well.
tips: If the pan turns wet and grey instead of sizzling, the heat's too low — tip off any liquid and let it come back up.
warnings: Wash hands, board and spoon handle after touching raw mince.

steps.title: Build the gravy
steps.content: Stir in flour, then stock, tomato, Worcestershire.
steps.detail: Sprinkle 2 tbsp flour over the mince, stir constantly for 60 sec to cook the raw flour taste out — you're making a filling-thickening roux. Pour in 300ml beef stock in a steady stream while stirring, then half a tin of tomatoes (crush with hand) and 1 tbsp Worcestershire.
tips: Add the stock in stages, stirring each addition fully in — dumping it all at once makes lumps.

steps.title: Simmer, then cool
steps.content: Simmer 20 min till thick, cool.
steps.detail: Bring to a simmer, drop heat low. 20 min stirring occasionally till the sauce is thick and glossy — should hold a trail when you drag a spoon through. COOL fully before filling — hot filling melts the bottom pastry and makes the pie soggy. Spread on a tray to speed cooling.
tips: Spread the filling no deeper than 2 cm on the tray and it'll be cool enough in about 30 minutes.

steps.title: Assemble the pie
steps.content: Line pie tin with pastry, fill with mince, top with another pastry sheet.
steps.detail: Grease a pie tin. Roll out one sheet of puff pastry to line, letting it overhang the edges — you'll trim later. Fill with the cold mince mixture. Lay the second sheet of puff pastry on top for a flaky lid. Press the edges together and crimp with a fork.
tips: Thaw pastry only till it bends — fully soft puff sticks and tears. Keep the second sheet in the fridge till you need it.

steps.title: Egg wash and vent
steps.content: Egg wash lid, cut a vent.
steps.detail: Beat an egg with a splash of milk. Brush the lid generously with egg wash — this is what gives pies their deep gold shine. Cut a small X in the centre of the lid with a sharp knife. This vents the steam; without it the pie swells and splits.
tips: Keep the egg wash on the pastry only — wash pooled on the tin edge glues the pie in.

steps.title: Bake the pie
steps.content: Bake 25 min at 200°C till deep gold.
steps.detail: 200°C oven, middle rack, 25 min till the top is deep gold and you can hear the filling bubbling gently through the vent. Rest 10 min before cutting — filling is lava straight out of the oven. Tomato sauce on the side is mandatory. Mushy peas alongside if you're feeling nostalgic.
tips: If the lid is browning too fast, lay a sheet of foil loosely over the top and keep baking.

title: Beef Stroganoff
description: Tender beef, mushrooms, silky sour cream sauce, over noodles. Nan food.
storage: Keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge. Reheat gently over low heat — boiling splits the sour cream. It doesn't freeze well; the sauce separates.
make_ahead: Slice the beef and veg up to a day ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — the cooking itself is quick and best done fresh.
leftovers: Fold leftovers through hot pasta or spoon over a baked potato — gentle reheat only.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh strips — cook them right through instead of medium-rare
crème fraîche (more split-resistant), or Greek yoghurt stirred in off the heat
fettuccine, mash or rice all carry the sauce fine
hot English mustard — use half as much

steps.title: Slice the beef
steps.content: Slice beef thin, toss with salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Freeze steak 15 min first — makes thin slicing much easier. Slice ACROSS the grain into 5mm strips. Toss in a bowl with a generous pinch of salt and pepper. That's the minimum seasoning — the sauce will add more.
tips: A partly frozen steak should still bend a little — rock solid means it's been in too long.
warnings: Wash the board, knife and your hands after slicing raw beef, before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Flash-sear the beef
steps.content: Sear in very hot pan 90 sec, remove.
steps.detail: Heavy pan smoking-hot, splash of oil. Strips in a single layer, don't crowd. 90 sec without touching — let them sear to deep brown. Toss once, 30 more sec. Medium-rare at most — they'll warm through later. Crowding steams; work in 2 batches if needed. Lift out to a plate.
tips: Pat the strips dry before they hit the pan — wet beef steams grey instead of searing brown.
warnings: The pan should be just smoking — expect spatter as the beef goes in, so stand back a touch.

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Sauté mushrooms, onion, garlic in butter.
steps.detail: Same pan, knob of butter. Drop heat to medium. Halved mushrooms in, don't touch for 2 min — you want deep gold, not grey. Then one thinly sliced onion and 2 grated garlic cloves, 6 min till soft and the mushrooms are properly browned. A pinch of salt helps.
tips: Mushrooms squeak and release water first — keep going till it evaporates and they turn gold.
Get the noodle water on now — everything from here moves quickly.

steps.title: Make the sour-cream sauce
steps.content: Deglaze with stock, stir in dijon and sour cream.
steps.detail: Pour in 200ml beef stock, scrape the pan bottom for fond. Bring to a simmer. Whisk in 1 tbsp dijon and 1 cup sour cream. Drop heat low — boiling splits sour cream into a curdled mess. 3 min gentle warming till glossy and thickened.
tips: Pull the pan off the heat while you whisk the sour cream in, then return it to low — cheap insurance against splitting.

steps.title: Warm the beef through
steps.content: Return beef, warm through.
steps.detail: Beef back in with all resting juices. Fold gently through the sauce, 60 sec to warm through — don't boil or the beef overcooks to tough. Taste — salt, pepper, a squeeze of lemon if it's too rich.
tips: Warm through means just that — the strips should stay blush-pink in the middle.

steps.title: Butter noodles and serve
steps.content: Serve over buttered noodles with parsley.
steps.detail: Butter hot cooked noodles (egg noodles traditionally) — toss with a knob of butter so they don't clump. Pile into shallow bowls. Stroganoff ladled generously over. Torn parsley on top, crack of pepper. Comfort food incarnate.

title: Cottage Pie
description: Savoury mince under a golden mash lid. A hug in a dish.
equipment: large heavy pot or deep frying pan
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge and freezes whole or in portions for 3 months. Reheat at 180°C until piping hot in the centre.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole pie a day ahead and keep it in the fridge unbaked — add 10 minutes to the baking time from cold. The mince alone keeps 2 days.

substitutions.swap: lamb mince — which technically makes it a shepherd's pie
soy sauce with a splash of vinegar
skip it and add an extra carrot

steps.title: Sweat the veg
steps.content: Sweat diced carrot, celery, onion, garlic.
steps.detail: Small even dice on 2 carrots, 2 celery stalks, 1 onion, 2 grated garlic cloves. Small dice matters — you want a piece of each in every forkful. Heavy pot, splash of oil over medium. Veg with a pinch of salt, 10 min till onion is translucent and carrot is softening. This is the mirepoix, the flavour backbone.
tips: Cut the carrot into sticks first, then across into dice — even pieces cook evenly.

steps.title: Brown mince, make gravy
steps.content: Brown mince, add stock and Worcestershire, simmer 15 min.
steps.detail: Crank heat up. Push veg to the sides, crumble 700g mince into the centre of the pan. Don't touch for 3 min — deep brown crust. Then break up and cook 5 min more. Stir veg back through. Pour in 400ml beef stock and 2 tbsp Worcestershire. Simmer 15 min till thickened and the meat is sitting in a rich gravy.
tips: If liquid pools when the mince goes in, tip it off — mince browns dry, boils wet.
warnings: Wash hands and board after handling the raw mince.

steps.title: Stir through the peas
steps.content: Stir in peas.
steps.detail: A cup of frozen peas straight in for the last 2 min — no need to thaw. Stir through gently. Peas are colour and sweetness.

steps.title: Make the mash
steps.content: Boil potatoes, mash with butter and milk.
steps.detail: Peel and chunk 1.2kg potatoes. Into a pot of cold salted water, bring to boil, simmer 18 min till a fork slides through. Drain, rest 2 min to dry in the pot. Mash with 60g butter and 100ml milk till silky. Salt, pepper.
tips: Warm the milk and butter together before mashing them in — cold milk seizes the mash.

steps.title: Assemble the pie
steps.content: Tip mince into dish, pile mash on top, rough with a fork.
steps.detail: Tip the mince into a baking dish. Dollop mash across the top in heaps, then spread with a fork, going right to the edges (seals the filling in). Make furrows across the top with the fork — more surface = more crispy golden peaks. Grated cheese on top is optional but wonderful.
tips: If the mash drags the filling up as you spread, let the mince cool 10 minutes first — it firms up and holds.

steps.title: Bake till bubbling
steps.content: Bake 30 min at 200°C till golden.
steps.detail: 200°C oven, middle rack, 30 min till the top is deep gold and the filling is bubbling at the edges. If it's pale, flash under the grill 2 min. Rest 5 min — filling is volcanic straight out. Peas or green beans on the side. Sunday night comfort.
tips: Sit the dish on a baking tray — bubbled-over gravy is far easier to clean off a tray than the oven floor.

title: Caprese Salad
description: Mozzarella, tomato, basil, olive oil. Summer on a plate — buy the best of everything.
storage: It doesn't keep — dressed tomatoes weep and the basil blackens, so make only what you'll eat now. Undressed leftovers hold covered in the fridge for a day at best.
make_ahead: Only this: take the tomatoes and mozzarella out of the fridge an hour before — room temperature is half the recipe.
leftovers: Chop any undressed leftovers with yesterday's bread and extra oil for a rough panzanella.

substitutions.swap: skip it entirely — plenty of Italians do
bocconcini, or buffalo mozzarella if the budget stretches

steps.title: Slice tomato and cheese
steps.content: Slice tomato and mozzarella.
steps.detail: Use the best tomatoes you can find — a caprese lives or dies by them. Ripe, room-temp (cold fridge tomatoes = tasteless). Slice 1cm thick. Do the same to the fresh mozzarella. Room-temp mozzarella, always; cold cheese is bouncy, warm cheese is velvety.
tips: A serrated knife slices ripe tomatoes cleanly where a straight blade squashes them.

steps.title: Layer the platter
steps.content: Alternate on a platter with basil leaves.
steps.detail: Large platter. Alternate slices of tomato and mozzarella overlapping slightly in a line, with big basil leaves tucked between them. Don't chop basil — whole leaves are the traditional look. This dish is about restraint and beauty; no fussing.

steps.title: Dress with restraint
steps.content: Dress with oil, a few drops of balsamic, sea salt.
steps.detail: Drizzle generously with your BEST olive oil (this is the moment to use the expensive bottle). A few drops of balsamic — a little goes a long way. Flaky sea salt — the kind you can see. No pepper needed, but a crack if you must.
tips: Salt just before serving — salted tomatoes start weeping within minutes.

steps.title: Serve straight away
steps.content: Eat immediately.
steps.detail: Serve immediately — cold-from-the-fridge tomatoes weep water, dressed tomatoes lose their shape. Eat within 20 min of dressing. Crusty bread on the side to mop up the tomato-oil-balsamic pool at the bottom of the plate — that's the best bit.

title: Tomato Bruschetta
description: Charred bread, sun-ripe tomato, basil. A backdrop to wine.
storage: The tomato topping keeps a day covered in the fridge; bring it back to room temperature before using. Assembled bruschetta doesn't keep at all.
make_ahead: The tomato mix can sit for up to an hour and only improves — toast the bread at the last minute.
leftovers: Leftover topping is great stirred through hot pasta or spooned over scrambled eggs.

substitutions.swap: red wine vinegar for a sharper edge
any sturdy loaf — ciabatta, or a baguette split lengthways

steps.title: Salt the tomatoes
steps.content: Dice tomatoes, salt lightly, drain.
steps.detail: Dice the tomatoes into small 1cm pieces — small enough to sit on top of bread without rolling off. Tip into a bowl, sprinkle lightly with salt, toss. Rest 5 min in a sieve over a bowl — the salt draws out excess water, which would make bread soggy. Drain.
tips: Save the drained tomato water — it makes a cracking salad dressing with oil and vinegar.

steps.title: Dress the tomato mix
steps.content: Toss with torn basil, oil, balsamic.
steps.detail: Back in the bowl: a big glug of good olive oil, a splash of balsamic, a big handful of torn basil (not chopped — bruising turns basil black). Toss gently. Taste for salt. The tomato mix should be glossy and bright. Rest 10 min for flavours to mingle.

steps.title: Char the bread
steps.content: Grill bread both sides.
steps.detail: Thick-cut sourdough, both sides under a hot grill (or a ridged grill pan for char marks). Watch it — goes from gold to burnt fast. You want deep gold with some charred edges. Chewy, not fragile.
tips: The second side always toasts faster than the first — check it early after flipping.

steps.title: Rub with garlic
steps.content: Rub hot bread with garlic clove.
steps.detail: Cut a garlic clove in half. Rub the cut face of the garlic over the HOT toast — the bread acts like a microplane, grating the garlic into every nook. Hot bread is crucial; cold bread doesn't release the garlic oils. A drizzle of olive oil.
tips: One or two passes per slice is plenty — raw garlic gets fierce fast.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Pile tomato on top.
steps.detail: Pile the tomato mix generously on top, letting juice soak into the bread (that's the whole point). Extra basil leaves on top, crack of pepper. Eat within 5 min before the bread goes fully soggy. Stand up, lean over a plate. Juice on the chin is a sign of a life well-lived.
tips: Spoon the topping on with a slotted spoon and let each scoop drain a second — the bread stays crisp longer.

title: Parsley Tabouleh
description: A salad that's actually herbs. Bright, green, and unreasonably good.
equipment: salad spinner or clean tea towel
storage: Keeps a day airtight in the fridge — after that the herbs darken and the tomato waters it down. Not one for the freezer.
make_ahead: Made a few hours ahead it only improves — the 10-minute rest is the bare minimum.
leftovers: Pile leftovers into a pita with hummus and whatever protein is around.

substitutions.swap: fine bulgur soaked the same way is the traditional grain
a quarter of a red onion, very finely diced
extra parsley — it's still tabouleh without the mint

steps.title: Soak the couscous
steps.content: Cover couscous in hot water, rest 10 min, fluff.
steps.detail: Half a cup of couscous in a bowl. Cover with an equal amount of hot water (or stock for extra flavour), pinch of salt, splash of olive oil. Cover with a plate. Rest 10 min. Fluff vigorously with a fork to separate every grain — lumps ruin tabouleh.
tips: Spread the fluffed couscous on a plate to cool quickly — warm grains wilt the herbs later.

steps.title: Chop the herbs fine
steps.content: Very finely chop parsley, mint, spring onion.
steps.detail: Tabouleh is a HERB salad that happens to have grains in it — not the other way round. 3 big handfuls of parsley, one handful of mint, 3 spring onions. Rinse, dry in a salad spinner (wet herbs make soggy tabouleh), then chop VERY fine. A sharp knife, patience, rocking motion. Stems too (flavour) — just tough bits removed.
tips: Gather the herbs into a tight bundle under your fingers and slice thin first — fine chopping goes twice as fast from there.

steps.title: Dice the tomatoes
steps.content: Dice tomato small.
steps.detail: Dice 2 ripe tomatoes into small 5mm cubes. Drain on paper towel 2 min if they're particularly juicy. Keep the pieces small — big chunks feel wrong in tabouleh.

steps.title: Dress and toss
steps.content: Combine everything with lemon and oil.
steps.detail: Biggest bowl you have. Combine cooked couscous, chopped herbs, tomatoes. Dress with juice of 2 lemons and 1/3 cup good olive oil. Toss well. The proportion should look VERY green — if you see mostly grain, you need more herbs.
tips: Add the lemon juice gradually and taste — lemons vary wildly in how much juice and punch they carry.

steps.title: Rest before serving
steps.content: Season, rest 10 min before serving.
steps.detail: Season generously with salt and pepper — tabouleh needs more salt than feels right. Rest 10 min before serving for flavours to meld. Eats chilled or room temp. Pita bread and hummus alongside, or as part of a mezze spread.
tips: Taste again after the rest, not just before — salt and lemon both settle in as it sits.

title: Cinnamon French Toast
description: Custard-soaked bread, a golden crust, maple dripping off the edges.
storage: Best straight off the pan. Cooked slices keep a day in the fridge and re-crisp in a dry frying pan or the toaster; they also freeze flat for a month and toast straight from frozen.
make_ahead: Whisk the custard the night before and keep it covered in the fridge — breakfast becomes a 10-minute job.

substitutions.swap: brioche or fruit loaf for the luxury version — any thick-cut stale white loaf works
honey, or a shake of cinnamon sugar
banana or blueberries — whatever's around

steps.title: Whisk the custard
steps.content: Whisk eggs, milk, cinnamon, vanilla.
steps.detail: Jug or wide bowl: 3 eggs, 1/3 cup milk, 1 tsp cinnamon, 1 tsp vanilla. Whisk vigorously for 30 sec till completely smooth and slightly frothy. No streaks of egg white. The cinnamon needs to be dispersed, not floating in pockets.
tips: If the cinnamon floats in clumps, whisk it into a spoonful of milk first — as a paste it disperses evenly.

steps.title: Soak the bread
steps.content: Soak bread 30 sec a side.
steps.detail: Use day-old thick-cut bread (fresh bread falls apart in custard). Dip each slice in the egg mix — 30 sec per side, pressing gently so it soaks through without disintegrating. Lift and let excess drip off. Thin bread will sog; stale bread absorbs perfectly.
tips: Only fresh bread in the house? Leave the slices out for 20 minutes or dry them 5 minutes in a low oven first.

steps.title: Fry in foaming butter
steps.content: Fry in butter 2 min a side till gold.
steps.detail: Big knob of butter in a non-stick pan, medium heat (NOT high — custard needs time to cook through without burning outside). When the butter foams, bread slices in. 2 min per side till deep gold. Butter each pan-full freshly — burnt butter from the previous batch tastes bitter. Press the centre of a slice — it should spring back rather than squelch. A squelch means the custard inside needs another minute.
tips: The first slice is your thermostat — if it browns in under a minute, the pan's too hot for the custard to set inside.

steps.title: Stack and syrup
steps.content: Stack, dust with cinnamon, drown in syrup.
steps.detail: Stack three slices high on a warm plate. Dust with extra cinnamon. Drown in maple syrup — real maple, not the fake stuff. A pat of butter on top melting down into the cinnamon-syrup puddle. This is the move.

steps.title: Finish with strawberries
steps.content: Strawberries on top.
steps.detail: Hulled and sliced strawberries piled on top — their acid cuts through the richness. Icing sugar dusted over from a sieve if you're going for Instagram. Eat immediately before the bottom goes soggy.

title: Yoghurt & Granola Bowl
description: Toasted oats, honey-crunchy, cold yoghurt, bright fruit. Breakfast or honest dessert.
equipment: large baking tray lined with baking paper
storage: Granola keeps 2 weeks in an airtight jar at room temperature (or a month in the fridge in humid weather). Assembled bowls don't keep — yoghurt softens the crunch fast.
make_ahead: The granola is the make-ahead — a batch keeps 2 weeks in an airtight jar, so breakfast takes 30 seconds all week.
leftovers: Sprinkle it over ice cream, or bag up the big clusters as a snack.

substitutions.swap: maple syrup or golden syrup, same amount
any nuts or seeds — pepitas and cashews are great
any fruit — banana, mango or stewed apple

steps.title: Mix the dry base
steps.content: Mix oats, chopped nuts, cinnamon.
steps.detail: Big bowl: 3 cups rolled oats (not instant — instant becomes dust when baked), 1 cup chopped almonds, 1/2 cup chopped walnuts, 1 tsp cinnamon. Toss with your hands to coat the nuts with cinnamon. Mix is dry at this stage — that's right.
tips: Chop the nuts roughly to oat-flake size — big chunks fall to the tray and burn before the oats colour.

steps.title: Coat with warm honey
steps.content: Warm honey, oil, vanilla; toss through oats.
steps.detail: Small pot over low heat: 1/2 cup honey, 1/3 cup vegetable oil, 1 tsp vanilla. Warm 2 min till loose and pourable. Stir into oats and toss thoroughly — every flake should be coated glistening. A pinch of salt in the oat mix balances the sweetness.
tips: Scrape the pot out quickly while the syrup's warm — it thickens and grips as it cools.

steps.title: Bake low and slow
steps.content: Spread on a tray, bake 25 min at 160°C, stirring twice.
steps.detail: Spread in a thin even layer across a lined baking tray. No mountains — mountains steam. 160°C oven (low and slow is the secret), 25 min. Stir twice during baking — push edges to the middle, middles to the edges, so everything browns evenly. Done when deeply gold all over.
tips: Ovens run uneven — if one corner browns first, that's your cue to stir and rotate the tray.
warnings: Honey coatings tip from golden to burnt fast in the last 5 minutes — check at the 20-minute mark.

steps.title: Cool to crisp
steps.content: Cool (it crisps as it cools).
steps.detail: Here's the trick: granola isn't crispy when you pull it out — it crisps as it cools. Let the tray sit on a rack till COMPLETELY cool, 30 min. Then break into chunks with your hands. If you break it up while warm, you lose the clusters everyone loves.

steps.title: Layer the bowl
steps.content: Layer yoghurt, granola, blueberries in a bowl.
steps.detail: Deep bowl. Layer: spoonfuls of yoghurt, then granola, then blueberries (or any fruit — mango, banana, strawberries all work), repeat. Drizzle of extra honey if you want it sweeter. Eat with a long spoon to reach the bottom layers.

title: Passionfruit Pavlova
description: Crisp shell, marshmallow heart, cream, tropical fruit. The argument we'll never settle with NZ.
equipment: large glass or metal mixing bowl
storage: The bare shell keeps 2 days in an airtight container in a cool, dry spot. Once topped it doesn't really keep — fridge leftovers overnight at most and expect a softer, weepier pav. Don't freeze.
make_ahead: Bake the shell up to a day ahead and keep it in a dry airtight container at room temperature. Whip the cream and top with fruit just before serving.
leftovers: Smash leftover pav into glasses with the cream and fruit for an instant Eton mess.

substitutions.swap: tinned passionfruit pulp when they're out of season
kiwi fruit or extra berries when mangoes aren't around

steps.title: Whip the whites
steps.content: Whip whites till soft peaks.
steps.detail: Eggs SPOTLESSLY clean — any trace of yolk or fat prevents whites whipping. Six whites into a perfectly clean glass or metal bowl (plastic holds fat residue). Start the mixer low, ramp up to medium. After 2 min they'll look foamy; after 4 min they'll form soft peaks that flop slightly when you lift the whisk.
tips: Wipe the bowl and beaters with vinegar or a cut lemon before you start — cheap insurance for stubborn whites.
Save the yolks — they keep 2 days covered in the fridge and make lemon curd or carbonara.

steps.title: Beat in the sugar
steps.content: Add sugar a spoon at a time till glossy and thick.
steps.detail: Slow trickle of 1.5 cups caster sugar, ONE TABLESPOON at a time, beating continuously in between. Never dump it all in — dissolves poorly, pavlova weeps. After all the sugar is in, beat 5 more min on high till glossy and thick — rubbing a pinch between fingers should feel SMOOTH, not gritty. That's the test.
tips: Done right, the mix holds a stiff, shiny peak that stands straight off the whisk.

steps.title: Fold in the vanilla
steps.content: Fold in vanilla.
steps.detail: Drop the mixer to low and fold in 1 tsp vanilla. That's it — for pavlova you don't add vinegar and cornflour in this simpler version (classic version does), or you can sift in 1 tsp cornflour here for the classic marshmallow middle.

steps.title: Shape the pav
steps.content: Pile on baking paper into a round, shape edges high.
steps.detail: Draw a 22cm circle on baking paper with a pencil, flip the paper (so the pencil isn't in contact with pav) onto a tray. Pile the mixture inside the circle. Shape with a spatula: slightly taller edges with a dip in the middle — this catches cream and fruit at the end. Don't flatten.
tips: Dab a little meringue under each corner of the paper to glue it to the tray.

steps.title: Bake low and slow
steps.content: Bake 90 min at 120°C, cool in oven with door ajar.
steps.detail: 120°C oven (LOW is essential — high heat browns and cracks), bottom-middle rack, 90 min. The shell should be pale ivory, dry to touch, hollow-sounding when tapped. Turn oven OFF, prop the door open with a wooden spoon, leave pav inside till fully cool — 2 hours or overnight. Rapid cooling cracks the shell.
tips: Small surface cracks are fine — the cream covers them. Deep browning means your oven runs hot.
warnings: Don't open the oven during the bake — the temperature drop cracks and deflates the shell.

steps.title: Whip the cream
steps.content: Whip cream, pile on top.
steps.detail: 300ml cream whipped to soft peaks (cold cream, cold bowl — splits if warm). Don't over-whip or you have butter. Pile over the cooled pav generously — messy is good. Swirl with the back of a spoon.
tips: Stop whipping while the cream still slumps softly — it firms a little more as you spread it.

steps.title: Top with fruit
steps.content: Crown with passionfruit pulp, sliced fruit.
steps.detail: Crown with the pulp of 4 passionfruit (scoop out with a spoon), sliced fresh strawberries, sliced mango. Don't top more than 30 min before serving — fruit juice will soak into the pav and weep. Summer in a dessert.
tips: Slice the cheeks off the mango, score a grid and turn inside out for tidy cubes.

title: Apple & Oat Crumble
description: Soft cinnamon apples, buttery oat lid. Winter antidote.
storage: Keeps 3 days covered in the fridge, and the baked crumble freezes for up to 2 months. Reheat in a 160°C oven for 15–20 minutes to re-crisp the top — the microwave turns it soft.
make_ahead: The unbaked topping keeps a week in the fridge or 3 months frozen. You can also assemble the whole dish a day ahead and bake it straight from the fridge with an extra 5 minutes.
leftovers: Cold crumble with thick Greek yoghurt is a legitimate breakfast.

substitutions.swap: pecans or slivered almonds, or leave the nuts out entirely
raw or white sugar — you lose a little caramel depth, that's all
firm pears for half the apples makes a lovely variation

steps.title: Prep the apples
steps.content: Peel and chunk apples, toss with sugar, cinnamon, lemon.
steps.detail: Peel 6 apples, quarter, core, slice into 1cm wedges. Granny Smith holds shape best; Pink Lady is sweeter. In a bowl, toss with 1/2 cup sugar, 1 tsp cinnamon, 2 tbsp flour (thickens the juice into glossy syrup), and the juice of half a lemon (stops browning AND lifts flavour).
tips: Toss the apple as you go — the lemon can't stop browning on wedges it hasn't touched.

steps.title: Fill the dish
steps.content: Pile in a baking dish.
steps.detail: Butter a 20cm baking dish. Tip apple in, mounding slightly in the centre — it'll flatten as it cooks. Don't pack tight; some air helps steam escape. Cover loosely with foil while you make the crumble.

steps.title: Make the crumble
steps.content: Rub butter into flour, stir in oats, chopped walnuts, more sugar.
steps.detail: In a bowl: 3/4 cup flour. Add 120g cold butter in cubes and rub between your fingertips till it looks like breadcrumbs — some bigger chunks are fine and they make better crumble. Stir in 1 cup rolled oats, 1/2 cup chopped walnuts, 1/2 cup brown sugar. Mix. It should be sandy with some clumps.
tips: Warm hands melt the butter — run your wrists under the cold tap or chill the bowl for 5 minutes first.

steps.title: Top the apples
steps.content: Scatter over apples.
steps.detail: Remove the foil from the apples. Scatter the crumble over the top evenly — don't press down; you want it loose so steam escapes and the topping crisps. Cover edges completely so apple doesn't dry out.

steps.title: Bake till golden
steps.content: Bake 40 min at 180°C till gold.
steps.detail: 180°C oven, middle rack, 40 min. Done when the top is deep gold, the edges are bubbling with thick apple juice, and you can smell cinnamon from three rooms away. If browning too fast, loosely cover with foil.
tips: Sit the dish on a lined tray — bubbling apple juice loves to escape.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Cream or ice cream.
steps.detail: Rest 10 min — filling is volcanic straight out, and the juice needs to set slightly. Big scoops into bowls with a generous pour of thickened cream (which gently melts into the hot crumble) or a scoop of vanilla ice cream. Custard if you're a purist.

title: Tiramisu
description: Coffee-soaked sponge, mascarpone clouds, a cocoa dust. Sunday-lunch encore.
equipment: deep serving dish, about 20 x 30 cm
storage: Keeps 2 days covered in the fridge — it contains raw egg, so don't stretch it further. Freezing turns the cream grainy; give it a miss.
make_ahead: Tiramisu is a make-ahead dessert by design — assemble it the day before; it's at its best after 12–24 hours in the fridge.

substitutions.swap: plain sponge cake cut into fingers and dried out in a low oven
full-fat cream cheese beaten with a splash of cream at a pinch — tangier, still good
strong plunger or moka coffee; instant works at 2 heaped teaspoons per cup

steps.title: Separate the eggs
steps.content: Separate eggs.
steps.detail: Eggs straight from the fridge (easier to separate cold). Crack on a flat surface, pour the yolk between the two shell halves letting the white fall into a bowl. Don't break the yolk; any trace of yolk in the whites prevents them whipping. 4 yolks in one bowl, 4 whites in another spotlessly clean bowl.
tips: Separate each egg over a small bowl first, then add the white to the main bowl — one burst yolk won't ruin the lot.
warnings: The eggs stay raw in this dessert — use the freshest you can and keep the mix cold at every stage.

steps.title: Ribbon the yolks
steps.content: Beat yolks with sugar till pale; fold in mascarpone and vanilla.
steps.detail: With electric beaters or a stand mixer, whip the yolks with 3/4 cup sugar for 3 min till pale yellow, thick, and ribboning — when you lift the beater, a slow ribbon of mix should fall back on itself and sit briefly before sinking. That's aeration — the whole structure of tiramisu. Fold in 500g room-temp mascarpone and 1 tsp vanilla till just smooth.
tips: Take the mascarpone out of the fridge 30 minutes ahead — cold mascarpone folds in lumpy.

steps.title: Fold in the whites
steps.content: Whip whites to soft peaks, fold in.
steps.detail: In the clean bowl, whip the whites to soft peaks — flop slightly when you lift the beater. Don't over-whip to stiff peaks; too stiff and folding is brutal, and you lose air. Take a third of the whites and vigorously stir into the mascarpone mix to lighten it. Then fold the rest in gently with a big spatula — sweeping strokes, turn the bowl, bottom up, don't mash.
tips: A few streaks of white left behind is better than a flat mix from over-folding.

steps.title: Fold through the cream
steps.content: Whip cream, fold through.
steps.detail: Separate bowl: whip 300ml cream to soft peaks (cold cream, cold bowl). Gently fold into the mascarpone mix. Now you have a three-part aeration: yolk ribbon, whipped whites, whipped cream. The result is cloud-light.

steps.title: Dip the biscuits
steps.content: Dip biscuits in coffee, lay in dish.
steps.detail: Brewed coffee (2 cups, cooled) in a shallow bowl. A splash of marsala or coffee liqueur is traditional if you want grown-up. Dip each savoiardi biscuit for 2 SECONDS only — any longer and they fall apart. You want them damp, not sopping. Lay in a single layer in a deep dish.
tips: Count 'one, two' out loud per biscuit — it keeps the dunk honest.

steps.title: Layer it up
steps.content: Spread half the cream, repeat layers.
steps.detail: Spread half the mascarpone cream evenly over the soaked biscuits with a spatula. Then repeat: another layer of dipped biscuits, then the rest of the cream on top. Smooth the surface.
tips: Save your smoothest spreading for the top layer — that's the one everyone sees.

steps.title: Chill and dust
steps.content: Chill 6 hr, dust with cocoa before serving.
steps.detail: Cover and chill at LEAST 6 hours — ideally overnight. This rest is non-negotiable; the flavours marry, the biscuits soften to tender, the texture becomes heaven. Just before serving, dust generously with unsweetened cocoa through a fine sieve. Slice with a knife dipped in hot water for clean edges. Note: this contains raw egg — use very fresh or pasteurised eggs, and skip it for anyone pregnant, very young, elderly or immunocompromised.
tips: Dust the cocoa just before it hits the table — it stays velvet-dark instead of soaking in.

title: Lemon Tart
description: Sharp, silky curd in a buttery crust. Afternoon-tea royalty.
equipment: baking paper and rice or baking weights
storage: Keeps 3 days covered in the fridge. The whole tart freezes for up to a month — thaw overnight in the fridge and expect slightly softer pastry.
make_ahead: The pastry disc keeps 2 days in the fridge or a month frozen, and you can blind-bake the shell a day ahead — keep it airtight at room temperature.

substitutions.swap: limes for a lime tart — use 5, they give less juice
pure or pouring cream — anything but light

steps.title: Make the pastry
steps.content: Rub cold butter into flour with 1/4 cup sugar, add yolk.
steps.detail: In a bowl: 1.5 cups flour, 1/4 cup sugar, pinch of salt. Cube 150g cold butter and add. Rub between your fingertips till it looks like coarse breadcrumbs — some bigger bits fine. Add one egg yolk and 2 tbsp iced water. Gather into a ball WITHOUT kneading — overworking makes tough pastry. Flatten into a disc, wrap, fridge 30 min.
tips: If the dough won't gather, add iced water a teaspoon at a time — it should just hold together when squeezed.

steps.title: Line the tin
steps.content: Press into tart tin, chill 30 min.
steps.detail: Roll the dough out between two sheets of baking paper into a 30cm circle — rolling between paper stops sticking and keeps the pastry cool. Peel off the top sheet, invert into a 23cm tart tin (loose-bottom = easier unmoulding), peel off the second sheet. Press into corners, trim the top edge. Prick the base all over with a fork. Fridge 30 min firm-up — prevents shrinkage.
tips: If the pastry tears, patch it with a scrap and press it in — nobody sees the base.

steps.title: Blind-bake the shell
steps.content: Blind-bake 15 min at 180°C.
steps.detail: Line the pastry with baking paper, fill with rice or baking weights (holds shape as it cooks). 180°C oven, middle rack, 15 min till the edges are pale gold. Remove paper and weights, 3 more min to dry out the base (prevents a soggy bottom). Cool slightly.
tips: Push the rice right into the corners — that's where the pastry slumps first.

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.content: Whisk eggs, remaining sugar, lemon juice and zest, cream.
steps.detail: Jug: 4 whole eggs plus 1 yolk, 1 cup sugar, zest and juice of 4 lemons, 1/2 cup thickened cream, 1 tsp vanilla. Whisk smooth. Strain through a sieve into a clean jug — catches any stray zest pith or egg string. Silky smooth is the aim.
tips: Zest the lemons before you juice them — it's near impossible afterwards.

steps.title: Fill the shell
steps.content: Pour into shell.
steps.detail: Place the tart shell (still in tin) on a baking tray. Pour the filling in carefully — almost to the top but not over. Filling in the oven is safer if you're nervous about spills: place tart on pulled-out rack, pour, slide in.

steps.title: Bake till just set
steps.content: Bake 25 min at 160°C till just set.
steps.detail: 160°C oven (LOW is crucial — high heat scrambles eggs), 25 min till the filling has a slight wobble in the centre only. Edges set, middle jiggles like set jelly. Overbake and it cracks; underbake and it weeps. Cool completely at room temp.
tips: Check at 20 minutes — the filling keeps setting from residual heat after it comes out.

steps.title: Chill and slice
steps.content: Chill 3 hr, slice.
steps.detail: Chill 3 hours for clean slicing — sets to glass-smooth cuts. Dust with icing sugar through a sieve just before serving, or torch the top to scatter caramelised sugar like a brûlée for extra drama. Fresh berries alongside.
tips: Wipe the knife clean between cuts for glassy slices.

title: Anzac Biscuits
description: Oat, coconut, golden syrup. Crisp-edged, chewy-hearted.
storage: An airtight tin keeps them a week — they soften in humid weather, so 5 minutes in a 150°C oven re-crisps them. Baked biscuits freeze for 2 months.
make_ahead: Rolled dough balls freeze well — bake them from frozen with an extra 2 minutes.
leftovers: Crushed Anzacs make a great crumble topping or ice-cream sprinkle.

substitutions.swap: golden syrup is the traditional choice; honey or treacle shift the flavour but work

steps.title: Combine the dry mix
steps.content: Mix oats, flour, sugar in a bowl.
steps.detail: Big bowl: 1 cup rolled oats, 1 cup plain flour, 3/4 cup sugar, 3/4 cup desiccated coconut. Stir to combine. Coconut isn't in the ingredients list but traditional Anzacs include it — add it if you have it, skip if not. Dry mix ready, set aside.

steps.title: Melt the butter
steps.content: Melt butter with golden syrup.
steps.detail: Small pot over low heat: 125g butter, 2 tbsp golden syrup (honey in a pinch but golden syrup is traditional). Warm till the butter has melted and just starting to bubble. Stir till combined. Don't boil — just melted.
tips: Warm the tablespoon under the hot tap first and the golden syrup slides straight off.

steps.title: Fizz the bicarb
steps.content: Dissolve bicarb in 2 tbsp hot water, stir into butter.
steps.detail: In a small cup: 1 tsp bicarb in 2 tbsp BOILING water. It'll fizz and foam — that's the reaction that makes Anzacs tender. Pour into the butter-syrup mix. It'll foam up spectacularly. Stir.
tips: Use a bigger cup than seems sensible — the fizz doubles in volume.

steps.title: Bring the dough together
steps.content: Tip into dry mix, combine.
steps.detail: Pour the frothy butter mix into the dry ingredients. Stir with a wooden spoon till everything comes together into a sticky, lumpy dough. If it's too dry, splash of water. Too wet, sprinkle of flour.

steps.title: Roll and space the balls
steps.content: Roll spoonfuls onto trays, flatten.
steps.detail: Line 2 trays with baking paper. Roll heaped teaspoons into balls, place on trays WITH SPACE between — Anzacs spread dramatically. Flatten each slightly with the palm of your hand. Don't fuss — rustic is the look.
tips: Damp hands stop the sticky dough clinging while you roll.

steps.title: Bake low and slow
steps.content: Bake 15 min at 160°C till deep gold. Cool on tray.
steps.detail: 160°C (LOW) oven, middle rack, 15 min. Low temp is the secret to the chewy centre; high temp burns edges before middles cook. Pull them when DEEP GOLD — they firm up as they cool, soft out of the oven is right. Cool on the tray entirely. Airtight tin, up to a week.
tips: Swap and rotate the trays halfway if your oven browns unevenly.

title: Campfire Damper
description: A quick scone-loaf. Butter and honey while it's still warm.
storage: Damper is best the day it's baked — it stales overnight. Keep leftovers wrapped at room temperature for a day, or freeze slices for a month and toast from frozen.
leftovers: Day-old damper toasts beautifully, and blitzes into decent breadcrumbs.

substitutions.swap: plain flour plus 2 tsp baking powder per cup
half milk, half water — or a cup of beer for a pub-style damper

steps.title: Rub in the butter
steps.content: Rub butter into flour and salt.
steps.detail: Big bowl: 3 cups self-raising flour, 1 tsp salt. Cube 60g cold butter and add. Rub between your fingertips till the mix looks like breadcrumbs — some bigger chunks are fine. The butter coats the flour particles, giving a tender crumb.
tips: Shake the bowl now and then — unrubbed lumps of butter bounce to the surface.

steps.title: Mix in the milk
steps.content: Mix in milk till just combined.
steps.detail: Make a well in the centre. Pour in 1.25 cups of milk. Stir with a wooden spoon, bringing flour from the edges into the milk, till it just comes together into a shaggy dough. Don't overmix — tough damper results. Use a light hand.
tips: Dry flour left in the bottom of the bowl? Add milk a teaspoon at a time, no more.

steps.title: Shape and slash
steps.content: Shape into a round on a tray, slash a cross on top.
steps.detail: Flour the bench, tip dough out, knead gently just to bring together — 30 seconds only. Shape into a round about 18cm across, 5cm thick. Transfer to a lined tray. With a sharp knife, slash a deep cross into the top, about 1cm deep — makes the wedges easier to tear.
tips: Wet the knife blade before slashing — it drags less through the sticky dough.

steps.title: Dust the top
steps.content: Dust with flour.
steps.detail: Dust the top generously with flour — gives the rustic, bushman damper look, and helps the crust form. A scrape of butter across the top if you want extra richness (melts in and keeps the crust tender).

steps.title: Bake till hollow
steps.content: Bake 25 min at 200°C till hollow when tapped.
steps.detail: 200°C oven, middle rack, 25 min. Done when the top is deep gold and the bottom sounds HOLLOW when you tap it with your knuckles. That hollow sound is the universal bread doneness test. If in doubt, give it another 5 min.
tips: Flip it with a tea towel to tap the base — the top can sound dull even when it's done.

steps.title: Serve it warm
steps.content: Hot, with butter and honey.
steps.detail: Best eaten warm within an hour — damper stales fast (no yeast, no preservatives). Tear into wedges, slather with butter and golden syrup or honey while still steaming. A cuppa on the side. The bushman's standby; a camping fire version cooked in foil in coals is even better.

title: Lamingtons
description: Sponge, chocolate icing, coconut. The most Australian afternoon tea.
equipment: two wide bowls for icing and coconut
storage: Airtight tin, 3 days at room temperature. Freeze un-iced sponge for a month, or finished lamingtons for 2 months — thaw at room temperature.
make_ahead: Bake the sponge a day ahead — day-old sponge is firmer and far easier to dip without crumbling.
leftovers: A slightly stale lamington split and sandwiched with jam and whipped cream is a fine second act.

substitutions.swap: shredded coconut for a shaggier coat
plain flour plus 4 tsp baking powder

steps.title: Cream the base
steps.content: Cream butter and sugar, beat in eggs and vanilla.
steps.detail: Stand mixer or beaters: 125g soft butter and 1 cup sugar beaten 4 min till PALE and fluffy. Pale = aerated = light sponge. Add 3 eggs ONE AT A TIME, beating between each (dumping all in at once = curdled mess). 1 tsp vanilla.
tips: If the mix curdles as the eggs go in, beat in a spoonful of the measured flour to bring it back.

steps.title: Fold in flour and milk
steps.content: Alternate flour and milk into batter.
steps.detail: Sift 2 cups self-raising flour over the bowl. Add half the 1/2 cup milk. Fold with a spatula just till combined. Add remaining flour AND milk. Fold again till smooth. Alternating adds keeps the batter from seizing. Don't beat — overmixing makes tough sponge.

steps.title: Bake the sponge
steps.content: Bake in a lined tray 25 min at 180°C.
steps.detail: Line a 20x30cm tin with baking paper, overhanging for easy lifting. Pour batter in, smooth top with a spatula. 180°C oven, middle rack, 25 min. Done when a skewer in the centre comes out clean and the sponge springs back when pressed gently.
warnings: Don't open the oven before the 20-minute mark — a rush of cold air sinks a sponge.

steps.title: Cool and cut
steps.content: Cool, cut into squares.
steps.detail: Rest in the tin 10 min, then lift out onto a rack. Cool COMPLETELY — ideally overnight. Icing a warm cake = cake falls apart. Cut into 16 even squares (4x4 grid works neatly). For really neat lamingtons, trim the edges first.
tips: A serrated bread knife in long, gentle strokes gives the cleanest sponge edges.

steps.title: Make the icing
steps.content: Whisk cocoa with 2 cups icing sugar and a splash of boiling water for glaze.
steps.detail: Bowl: 1/3 cup cocoa, 2 cups icing sugar. Whisk. Add boiling water 2 tbsp at a time, whisking, till smooth and slightly thinner than pouring honey — should drip off a spoon in a ribbon. Too thick and coverage is patchy; too thin and it pools.
tips: Icing thickening as you work? Whisk in a teaspoon of boiling water to loosen it again.

steps.title: Dip and roll
steps.content: Dunk each square, then roll in coconut.
steps.detail: Bowl of icing on one side, plate of desiccated coconut on the other. Spear each square with a fork, dunk into icing coating all sides, let excess drip off, then drop into the coconut and roll to coat completely. Fingers get messy — that's half the fun.
tips: Set up a line — cake, icing, coconut, rack — and work left to right; it goes twice as fast.

steps.title: Set the icing
steps.content: Rest on a rack till set.
steps.detail: Place on a wire rack to set for 1 hour till the icing firms. Any sooner and they slump when stacked. Airtight tin, eat within 3 days. Halfway technique: split each cooled square and sandwich with jam and whipped cream before icing — that's the jazzed-up lamington.

title: Crispy Tofu Stir-Fry
description: Golden tofu cubes, sticky sauce, crunch. Twenty minutes, no meat missed.
equipment: medium saucepan with a lid for the rice
grater for the garlic and ginger
storage: Keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge, though the tofu loses its crunch — re-crisp in a hot pan rather than the microwave. Cool leftover rice quickly, refrigerate within the hour and eat within a day.
make_ahead: Press the tofu and chop all the veg ahead — the actual cooking is 12 minutes and won't wait for you.
leftovers: Makes a good rice-bowl lunch — add a fried egg and some fresh cucumber for crunch.

substitutions.swap: sugar snaps or green beans
brown sugar or maple syrup
broccolini, or frozen florets thawed and dried well

steps.title: Press the tofu
steps.content: Press tofu 10 min, cube.
steps.detail: Drain 400g firm tofu. Wrap in a clean tea towel or paper towels. Place something heavy on top (a cast-iron pan, heavy book) for 10 min — pressing out water means crispier tofu. Wet tofu won't fry; it'll stew into sadness. Then cube into 2cm pieces.
tips: Use the pressing time to get the rice on and all the veg chopped — the wok work is fast once it starts.

steps.title: Crisp the tofu
steps.content: Toss in cornflour, pan-fry till crisp all over.
steps.detail: Toss cubes in 3 tbsp cornflour till every side is coated lightly — this is the crucial move for restaurant-crispy tofu. Hot pan with 3 tbsp oil over medium-high. Cubes in a single layer, don't touch for 2 min per side till each face is deep gold. 4 min total. Turn with tongs. Drain on paper.
tips: If a cube sticks, it isn't ready — it releases on its own once the crust forms.
warnings: Hot oil spits when tofu hits it — lower the cubes in away from your hand.

steps.title: Stir-fry the veg
steps.content: Stir-fry veg with garlic and ginger, 3 min.
steps.detail: Same wok, more oil. 3 grated garlic cloves and a thumb of grated ginger for 30 sec. Broccoli florets, sliced capsicum, snow peas in. Toss hard 3 min over high heat — veg should stay crunchy with slight char.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds in a screaming wok — keep everything moving for that first half-minute.

steps.title: Sauce it
steps.content: Splash in soy, honey, sesame oil.
steps.detail: Splash in 3 tbsp soy, 1 tbsp honey, 1 tbsp sesame oil. Toss 60 sec till everything's glossy and the sauce has reduced slightly. Taste — soy-salty, honey-sweet, sesame-nutty balance.

steps.title: Return the tofu
steps.content: Fold tofu back through.
steps.detail: Return tofu to the pan LAST (preserves the crunch). Toss gently just 30 sec to coat — any longer and the cornflour coating softens. Off heat.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Over jasmine rice.
steps.detail: Pile over hot jasmine rice. Sliced spring onion scattered, sesame seeds sprinkled, splash of extra sesame oil. Chilli flakes if you want heat. Eat immediately while the tofu's still crispy.

title: Loaded Beef Nachos
description: Melty cheese, spiced mince, sour cream, corn chips. A couch night.
storage: Assembled nachos don't keep — the chips go soggy within the hour. Store leftover mince on its own: 3 days in the fridge, 3 months frozen, reheated till piping hot.
make_ahead: The mince mix keeps 3 days in the fridge or 3 months frozen — reheat it till piping hot, then assemble and bake fresh.
leftovers: Leftover mince is burrito or jaffle filling, or spoon it over a baked potato.

substitutions.swap: pork or chicken mince, or a second tin of beans to go meat-free
Greek yoghurt
flat-leaf parsley if you're in the coriander-hater camp

steps.title: Make the spiced mince
steps.content: Brown mince with spices, add beans and tomato, simmer 10 min.
steps.detail: Hot heavy pan, splash of oil. Crumble 500g mince in, don't touch for 3 min for deep brown crust. Break up, cook 4 min more. Add 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp paprika, salt, pepper, toss 30 sec for spice bloom. Tip in a drained tin of beans and half a tin of tomatoes. Simmer 10 min till thick and fragrant.
warnings: Wash hands, board and spoon after the raw mince — nothing raw near the toppings.

steps.title: Lay out the chips
steps.content: Spread chips in a baking dish.
steps.detail: Big oven-proof dish or tray. Tip 150g corn chips in and shake into a single layer — you want every chip to have a chance of catching toppings. Avoid mountains; mountains leave dry chips on top and soggy underneath.

steps.title: Layer mince and cheese
steps.content: Pile mince on top, blanket with cheese.
steps.detail: Spoon the mince mix across the chips, scattering evenly — don't just dump a pile in the middle. Then blanket 200g grated cheddar over the whole lot. Cheese is the glue and the roof; generous application required.
tips: Grate the cheddar yourself — pre-grated cheese is dusted with starch and melts less smoothly.

steps.title: Bake till bubbling
steps.content: Bake 8 min at 200°C till bubbling.
steps.detail: 200°C oven, middle rack, 8 min till the cheese is molten and bubbling and the chips at the edges are extra-crisp. Watch it — cheese goes from gold to burnt fast. A flash under the grill the last 60 sec for extra crust.

steps.title: Load the toppings
steps.content: Top with avo, sour cream, coriander, chilli, lime.
steps.detail: Straight out of the oven, build: dollops of sour cream across the top, chunks of avocado, chopped coriander, fresh chilli slices, a squeeze of lime over everything. Jalapeños if you have them. Eat fast, fingers only. Napkins.
tips: Cut the avocado last, right before it goes on — it browns fast once open.

title: Buffalo Chicken Wings
description: Sticky, spicy, blue-cheese cool-down. Napkin food.
equipment: wire rack over a baking tray
storage: Sauced wings keep 2–3 days airtight in the fridge — reheat in a 200°C oven for 10 minutes until piping hot; the microwave makes them soggy. Freezing isn't worth it, the coating goes soft.
make_ahead: Flour the wings and leave them uncovered on the rack in the fridge for a few hours — drier skin bakes crispier. The sauce keeps a week in the fridge.
leftovers: Strip leftover meat off the bones for a buffalo chicken toastie or wrap with the blue cheese dip.

substitutions.swap: any hot sauce — Frank's is the classic; adjust the honey to taste
skip it and stir lemon juice and salt through the yoghurt for a plainer dip

steps.title: Coat the wings
steps.content: Toss wings with flour, paprika, salt.
steps.detail: Pat wings dry. In a big bowl, toss with 1/4 cup flour, 1 tbsp paprika, generous salt. The flour+paprika coating is what gives the wings their crispy texture straight from the oven — no frying needed. Shake off any excess; thick coating goes doughy.
tips: A large zip-lock bag makes the coating even and keeps the mess contained.
warnings: Raw chicken — wash hands, bowl and board in hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Bake till crisp
steps.content: Bake on a rack 40 min at 220°C, flipping once.
steps.detail: Place wings on a rack over a tray (rack allows heat to circulate all around — crisps bottoms as well as tops; without it, bottoms steam). 220°C oven, middle rack, 40 min, flipping once halfway. Deep gold, crispy-skinned, 75°C internal. Wings are forgiving; a few extra minutes for more crisp is fine.
tips: Line the tray under the rack with foil — the drips burn on, and foil saves the scrubbing.

steps.title: Make the buffalo sauce
