steps.content: Melt butter with sriracha, honey, vinegar.
steps.detail: Small pan: melt 100g butter over low heat. Off heat, whisk in 1/3 cup sriracha (or Frank's if you can find it), 2 tbsp honey, 1 tbsp vinegar. This is the classic buffalo ratio — spicy-sticky-vinegary balance. The butter keeps it glossy and rich.
tips: If the sauce splits, whisk in a teaspoon of hot water off the heat to bring it back.

steps.title: Sauce the wings
steps.content: Toss hot wings in sauce.
steps.detail: Hot wings straight from the oven into a big bowl. Pour sauce over, toss with tongs till every wing is glossed to deep red-orange. Do this the moment the wings come out — residual heat lets them drink up the sauce.

steps.title: Plate with the dip
steps.content: Serve with celery sticks and crumbled blue cheese in yoghurt.
steps.detail: Platter: pile of wings in the centre. Celery sticks (cool crunch, palate cleanser) on the side. Small bowl of blue cheese dip — crumble 50g blue cheese (or brie in a pinch) into 1/2 cup yoghurt, whisk smooth, pinch of salt. Plenty of napkins. Beers cold.

title: Chicken Satay Skewers
description: Grilled chook, peanut-coconut sauce, steamed rice. Backyard favourite.
equipment: medium saucepan with a lid for the rice
storage: Cooked chicken keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge, the sauce 4 days — reheat both till piping hot. Cool leftover rice quickly, refrigerate within the hour and eat within a day.
make_ahead: The satay sauce keeps 4 days in the fridge — make it ahead and you're 15 minutes from dinner. The skewers can sit in their third of the sauce, covered in the fridge, up to a day.
leftovers: Strip leftover chicken into a noodle salad and use the last of the satay sauce as the dressing.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast works but dries faster — pull it the moment it's cooked; firm tofu for a vego version
coconut cream thinned with a splash of water

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Cook the rice.
steps.detail: Rinse 2 cups jasmine rice until the water runs clear. Pot with 3 cups water, bring to a boil, cover, drop to the lowest heat for 12 min, then rest covered 5 min and fluff with a fork. Start this first so it’s hot when the skewers come off.
tips: Resist lifting the lid mid-cook — the trapped steam is doing the work.

steps.title: Thread the skewers
steps.content: Cube chicken, thread onto skewers.
steps.detail: Cube 600g chicken thighs into 3cm pieces. Thread onto skewers (soak wooden ones in water 30 min first or they char in the flames). 4-5 cubes per skewer with a little gap between — space lets heat circulate and gives char on all sides.
warnings: Raw chicken — wash hands, knife and board in hot soapy water straight after threading.

steps.title: Whisk the satay sauce
steps.content: Whisk peanut butter, coconut milk, soy, garlic, ginger, honey.
steps.detail: Whisk 1/2 cup peanut butter, 200ml coconut milk, 2 tbsp soy, 2 grated garlic cloves, a thumb of grated ginger, 1 tbsp honey, juice of half a lime. Whisk till completely smooth. Should be the consistency of thick cream — thin with a splash of water if stiff, thicken with more peanut butter if runny.
tips: Twenty seconds in the microwave softens the peanut butter so it whisks in smoothly.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Brush a third over skewers, marinate 10 min.
steps.detail: Reserve 2/3 of the sauce for serving (never dip cooked food back into raw marinade — food safety basics). Brush the remaining 1/3 over the chicken skewers, coating all sides. Rest 10 min — even quick marinades build flavour.

steps.title: Grill and char
steps.content: Grill ~8 min, turning every 2 min.
steps.detail: BBQ or griddle pan blazing-hot, oil the grills. Skewers on, don't move for 2 min — let char marks form. Turn every 2 min, 8 min total for cooked-through juicy chicken. Internal temp 74°C. Watch carefully — the peanut sugar catches fast. Medium-high, not max.
warnings: Chicken is safe at 74°C at the thickest point — cut the biggest cube to check: no pink inside, juices running clear.

steps.title: Warm the serving sauce
steps.content: Warm remaining sauce, thin with lime juice.
steps.detail: Reserved satay sauce into a small pan over low heat. Thin with the juice of the remaining half lime and a splash of water till it pours easily (chicken side sauces want to drizzle, not sit). Taste — more lime if flat, more soy if bland.
tips: Keep the heat low — a boiled peanut sauce splits and turns stiff.

steps.title: Build the plates
steps.content: Serve over rice with coriander and chilli.
steps.detail: Hot rice piled on plates, skewers leaning across, generous pour of satay sauce over both. Torn coriander and sliced fresh chilli scattered. Cucumber salad on the side cools things. Extra lime wedges. Eat straight off the skewer.

title: San Choy Bow
description: Pork mince in crunchy lettuce cups. A taco from the east.
equipment: wok or large heavy frying pan
grater for the garlic and ginger
storage: The pork mix keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge and freezes for 2 months — reheat till piping hot. Lettuce cups don't store; peel them fresh each time.
make_ahead: The pork mix reheats well — make it a day ahead and warm it through hard; wash and peel the lettuce cups just before serving.
leftovers: Leftover pork mix is excellent over steamed rice or tossed through noodles.

substitutions.swap: chicken or turkey mince
baby cos leaves for sturdier cups
hoisin for a sweeter, stickier version

steps.title: Dice the veg
steps.content: Finely dice mushroom and carrot.
steps.detail: Finely dice 150g mushrooms and 1 carrot — both 5mm cubes. Small dice is essential; this is a finger-food dish where every spoonful needs a bit of everything, not big lumps of vegetable. Prep is half the work.
tips: Square one side of the carrot first so it sits flat — faster and safer dicing.

steps.title: Brown the pork
steps.content: Fry pork till browned and crisp edges.
steps.detail: Hot wok or heavy pan, 1 tbsp oil. Tip 500g pork mince in, break up with a wooden spoon. Don't touch for 2 min to let a brown crust form, then break up and stir-fry 5 min more till no pink remains AND some edges are crispy-brown. Those crisp bits are flavour.
warnings: Wash hands and utensils after the raw mince, and keep it well away from the lettuce.

steps.title: Add aromatics and veg
steps.content: Add garlic, ginger, veg, stir-fry 3 min.
steps.detail: Push pork to the sides. Add 3 grated garlic cloves and a thumb of grated ginger to the clearing, 30 sec fragrant. Then tip in the diced mushroom and carrot, toss together. 3 min hard stir-frying — veg should soften but hold shape and colour.

steps.title: Glaze with the sauces
steps.content: Splash in sauces and sesame oil.
steps.detail: Splash in 2 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tbsp soy, 1 tbsp sesame oil. Toss hard 60 sec till everything's glossy and the sauce has reduced to a shine. Taste — salty-savoury with a kick. Off heat.

steps.title: Fill the lettuce cups
steps.content: Peel lettuce cups, spoon in pork, top with spring onion.
steps.detail: Peel individual iceberg lettuce leaves (keep whole so they cup nicely). Serve pork mix in a bowl alongside the stack of leaves — let everyone build their own. Heaped tablespoon of pork in each cup, scattering of sliced spring onion, squeeze of lime. Eat with hands, two bites each.
tips: Punch out the core and run cold water into the hole — the leaves loosen and peel off whole.

title: Pad See Ew
description: Charred rice noodles, sweet dark soy, gai lan, beef. The wok smell of Bangkok.
storage: Keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge, though the noodles soften. Reheat in a hot frying pan with a splash of water — the microwave turns them gluey — and don't freeze it.
make_ahead: This is all last-minute wok work, but you can slice the beef and halve the greens a few hours ahead and keep them covered in the fridge.
leftovers: Next-day pad see ew revived in a hot pan with a fried egg on top makes a very good lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh sliced thin, or firm tofu — same hot-and-fast sear
gai lan is the classic; choy sum or broccolini both work
vegetarian mushroom oyster sauce, or extra dark soy with a pinch more sugar

steps.title: Soak the noodles
steps.content: Soak noodles per packet.
steps.detail: Flat wide rice noodles (sen yai) per packet — usually 10 min in warm water till pliable but still firm. Drain just before using; don't leave soaking too long or they'll break up in the wok. Toss with a tiny splash of oil to prevent sticking.
tips: They're ready when they bend without snapping but still feel firm — they finish cooking in the wok.

steps.title: Sear the beef
steps.content: Slice beef thin, sear hot wok 60 sec, remove.
steps.detail: Freeze beef 15 min, slice across the grain into 5mm strips. Wok smoking-hot, 1 tbsp oil, swirl. Beef in a single layer, don't touch 60 sec for sear. Toss fast another 30 sec till just cooked. Medium-rare — it'll warm through later. Out to a plate.
tips: Get the wok properly smoking before the beef goes in — if it doesn't sizzle hard on contact, wait longer.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife after the raw beef, and keep it clear of anything already cooked.

steps.title: Scramble the eggs
steps.content: Fry garlic, crack in eggs and scramble.
steps.detail: Same wok, more oil. 3 grated garlic cloves, 30 sec fragrant. Push to the side, crack 2 eggs into the clearing. Let them sit 20 sec, then scramble with the spatula in big folds till just set, 60 sec. Break into chunks.
tips: Big lazy folds keep the egg in chunky pieces that survive the tossing later.
warnings: Garlic burns in seconds at wok heat — if it turns dark brown it's bitter; wipe out and start it again.

steps.title: Char the noodles
steps.content: Add noodles with sauces and sugar, char 2 min.
steps.detail: Crank heat to max. Tip the drained noodles into the wok. Pour over 3 tbsp dark soy, 1 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tsp sugar. Toss with two spatulas (easier to manage wide noodles) — lift from the bottom and fold. 2 min charring — you WANT some noodles to catch and turn deep brown in spots. That char is wok hei, the Bangkok signature.
tips: Let the noodles sit still between tosses — contact time with the hot metal is what builds the char.

steps.title: Wilt greens, return beef
steps.content: Toss in greens and beef.
steps.detail: Add halved bok choy (or Chinese broccoli) and the beef with resting juices. Toss 90 sec till greens are just wilted and everything's hot. Splash of water if it's sticking.
tips: If your bok choy is chunky, throw the stem halves in 30 seconds before the leafy ends.

steps.title: Serve straight away
steps.content: Serve with chilli vinegar.
steps.detail: Plate immediately — this dish loses its magic if it sits. Chilli vinegar on the side (rice vinegar with sliced chilli) is the Thai way — adds sharp heat that cuts through the richness. Fish sauce with sliced chilli (nam pla prik) if you prefer salty. A wedge of lime.
tips: Warm the plates — hot noodles on cold china go claggy fast.

title: Korean Beef Bowl
description: Sweet-salty bulgogi over rice, crunchy veg, a sesame finish.
equipment: medium pot with a tight lid
storage: Cool the rice quickly and fridge everything airtight — rice keeps 1 day, the beef 3 days. Reheat until piping hot and add the egg and kimchi fresh; the glazed mince freezes 2 months.
make_ahead: The sauce keeps a week in a jar in the fridge, so whisk it whenever suits. The cooked mince also reheats well for lunchboxes.
leftovers: The sticky mince is excellent in lettuce cups, or folded through fried rice with the leftover kimchi.

substitutions.swap: sriracha or 1 tsp chilli flakes plus an extra tsp honey — different, still good
brown sugar, same amount
pork or chicken mince — same method, same timing

steps.title: Start the rice
steps.content: Cook the rice.
steps.detail: Rinse 1.5 cups jasmine rice until the water runs clear, then drain. Tip into a pot with 2.25 cups water (1 part rice to 1.5 water), lid on, bring to a boil, then drop to the lowest heat for 12 min. Off the heat, still covered, 5 min more, then fluff with a fork. Start this first so it’s hot and ready when everything else is.
tips: Resist lifting the lid — the trapped steam is doing the cooking.

steps.title: Whisk the sauce
steps.content: Whisk soy, honey, sesame oil, gochujang, garlic, ginger.
steps.detail: Small bowl: 3 tbsp soy, 2 tbsp honey, 1 tbsp sesame oil, 1 tbsp gochujang, 3 grated garlic cloves, a thumb of grated ginger. Whisk till smooth. Taste — sweet, salty, a warm chilli heat from the gochujang. Adjust. Pre-mixing means you're not scrambling once the pan is hot.
tips: Grate the ginger straight from the freezer if you store it there — no stringy bits.

steps.title: Brown and glaze the beef
steps.content: Brown beef in hot pan, pour in sauce, reduce 2 min.
steps.detail: Hot pan, splash of oil over medium-high. Tip in 400g mince, break up with a wooden spoon. Don't touch 3 min for a brown crust on the base. Then break up and cook 3 more min till no pink remains. Pour the sauce in, toss 2 min till glossy and reduced to a sticky glaze that coats every grain of mince.
tips: The glaze is ready when a spoon dragged through leaves a trail that closes slowly.
warnings: Wash your hands and anything the raw mince touched before you move on to the fresh toppings.

steps.title: Julienne the carrot
steps.content: Julienne carrot.
steps.detail: Peel and julienne one carrot into matchstick strips — raw carrot for crunch is the bowl's textural backbone. No cooking needed.
tips: Cut thin slabs first, stack them, then slice into matchsticks — steadier and faster than freehand.

steps.title: Fry the eggs
steps.content: Fry eggs sunny-side up.
steps.detail: Non-stick pan, butter, medium-low. Crack eggs. Sunny-side up: don't touch till whites are set and edges lacy, 3 min. The runny yolk mixed through at the end is what brings everything together.
tips: A lid on for the last 30 seconds sets the top of the white without firming the yolk.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Bowls: rice, beef, carrot, kimchi, egg, spring onion.
steps.detail: Deep bowls. Hot rice base. Beef mince piled on one side, julienned carrot stacked next to it, kimchi in its own corner, egg in the centre or on top. Sliced spring onion and sesame seeds scattered. At the table, mix everything together with a spoon — the broken yolk coats everything in richness.

title: Moroccan Chicken Tagine
description: Spiced chicken, lemon, olives. Slow-cooker Sunday.
equipment: large heavy pot with a tight lid (or tagine)
large heatproof bowl for the couscous
storage: Fridge the tagine airtight up to 3 days, or freeze it up to 3 months; reheat gently until piping hot. Cooked couscous keeps 2 days but is best made fresh.
make_ahead: The tagine improves overnight — make it up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently on the stove. Do the couscous fresh; it takes 10 minutes.
leftovers: Shred leftover chicken back into the sauce and fold it through the couscous for a pilaf-style lunch.

substitutions.swap: drumsticks work well; avoid breast — it dries out over the long simmer
steamed rice or warm flatbread to mop the sauce
a rinsed, chopped preserved lemon if you have one — even better

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Brown chicken in oil, set aside.
steps.detail: Pat chicken thighs dry, season well with salt. Heat 3 tbsp oil in a heavy pot or tagine over medium-high. Brown in BATCHES — 3 min per side till mahogany. Don't crowd; crowded meat steams. Remove to a plate. The fond left on the pot bottom is the flavour foundation.
tips: Dry chicken browns; wet chicken steams — pat it properly with paper towel first.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash hands, board and tongs before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Soften onions, bloom spices
steps.content: Soften onions with spices, 5 min.
steps.detail: Drop heat to medium. Tip in 2 sliced onions with a pinch of salt. Scrape up the fond as you stir. 5 min till soft and starting to colour. Then 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp cinnamon, 1 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp paprika. Stir 30 sec to bloom the spices — the kitchen will smell like a Marrakech market.
tips: If the fond starts going black instead of brown, splash in a little water and drop the heat.

steps.title: Return chicken to stock
steps.content: Add garlic, ginger, stock; return chicken.
steps.detail: 3 grated garlic cloves and a thumb of grated ginger in for 60 sec. Pour in 500ml chicken stock. Return the chicken and any resting juices. Liquid should come halfway up the meat, not drown it.
tips: Too much liquid? Leave the lid off for the first 20 minutes of the simmer rather than pouring any away.

steps.title: Add lemons and olives
steps.content: Preserved-lemon-style: lemon quarters and olives on top.
steps.detail: Quarter 2 lemons and tuck them on top of the chicken skin-side up — as they cook they release their oils and soften into preserved-lemon territory. Scatter a cup of olives over. Don't stir; you want the visual on top.
tips: Bitter-shy? Squeeze one lemon quarter into the pot and just tuck the rest on top.

steps.title: Simmer low and slow
steps.content: Cover, simmer 75 min low and slow.
steps.detail: Cover tight, drop heat to lowest. 75 min gentle simmer — the chicken cooks to fork-tender, the onions dissolve into the sauce, the lemons perfume everything. Peek only every 20 min to check it's not drying — splash of water if the bottom is getting thick.
tips: You want the surface barely moving — a lazy bubble every second or two, not a rolling boil.

steps.title: Steam couscous, serve
steps.content: Fluff couscous, pile in bowls with tagine and coriander.
steps.detail: Pour 2 cups couscous into a bowl with an equal amount of boiling water, splash of olive oil, pinch of salt. Cover with a plate. Rest 10 min, then fluff vigorously with a fork to separate grains. Pile couscous into wide bowls, ladle tagine (chicken, sauce, lemons, olives) over the top. Torn coriander scattered generously. Yoghurt and flatbread on the side.
tips: Still crunchy after the 10 minutes? Splash over a little more boiling water and re-cover for 5.

title: Chicken & Chorizo Paella
description: Golden turmeric & paprika rice, crusty socarrat bottom, seafood optional but encouraged.
equipment: paella pan or your widest frying pan
small pot to heat the stock
storage: Cool leftovers quickly and fridge airtight — with cooked rice and prawns it keeps 1 day only. Reheat in a covered pan with a splash of stock until piping hot; freezing wrecks the rice texture, so skip it.
make_ahead: You can brown the meat and cook the sofrito a few hours ahead; bring the pan back to heat before the rice and stock go in.
leftovers: Cold paella pressed into a hot oiled pan and fried into a crisp cake, fried egg on top — a Spanish household move.

substitutions.swap: skip them and add extra peas, or use calamari rings in the final 5 minutes
any short-grain rice; long-grain won't give the creamy-crusty result
a smoky kransky at a pinch — add an extra pinch of smoked paprika

steps.title: Brown chicken and chorizo
steps.content: Brown chicken and chorizo in a wide pan.
steps.detail: Big wide pan (paella pan if you have one, otherwise the biggest flat skillet). Heat 3 tbsp oil over medium-high. Season chicken thighs, sear both sides till deep gold — 4 min each. Slice chorizo, add when you flip the chicken — it renders its paprika oil into the pan. Remove both to a plate.
tips: That orange chorizo oil left in the pan is the flavour base — don't wipe it out.
warnings: Raw chicken hygiene: wash hands, board and tongs before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Cook the sofrito
steps.content: Soften onion, garlic, capsicum.
steps.detail: Drop heat to medium. Into that orange chorizo-flavoured oil: finely diced onion, 4 grated garlic cloves, diced red capsicum. 8 min till soft and starting to catch. A pinch of salt. Scrape up any fond — flavour.
tips: Soft, sweet and just catching at the edges is the target — rushing this flattens the whole dish.

steps.title: Toast paste and spices
steps.content: Stir in tomato paste, paprika, turmeric.
steps.detail: Push veg to the sides. In the centre: 2 tbsp tomato paste, 1 tbsp paprika, 1/2 tsp turmeric (or saffron if you're posh — steeped in a splash of warm stock). Stir in the hot oil 90 sec till the paste darkens from orange to brick-red — cooks off the raw tinny taste. Mix through veg.
tips: If the spices start smelling acrid rather than sweet, the heat is too high — lift the pan off for a moment.

steps.title: Simmer rice, hands off
steps.content: Add rice and stock, simmer 15 min without stirring.
steps.detail: Tip in 2 cups short-grain rice (bomba ideal, arborio works) and stir to coat in the red mix. Pour in 1L hot stock. Chicken and chorizo return with resting juices, arranged on top. From this moment, DO NOT STIR — the point of paella is the bottom crust. 15 min simmer on medium-low. After 15 minutes most of the stock should be gone from the surface and small steam holes should show in the rice — that means you're on track.
tips: If the liquid vanishes early but the rice is still chalky, add hot stock in small splashes and shake the pan — never stir.

steps.title: Nestle prawns and peas
steps.content: Nestle prawns and peas on top, cover 5 min.
steps.detail: Nestle 300g prawns into the rice and scatter a cup of frozen peas across the top. Lid on (or foil), cook 5 min till prawns are pink and curled. Rice should have absorbed almost all the liquid.
tips: Push the prawns down so they touch the hot rice — sitting on top they steam unevenly.

steps.title: Crisp the socarrat
steps.content: Crisp the bottom 2 min on high.
steps.detail: Uncover. Crank heat up HIGH for 2 min — listen for a crackling sound from the bottom. That's the socarrat, the caramelised rice crust that's the soul of paella. Don't move the pan; trust the crackle. Stop when you smell caramel (not burn).
tips: If your burner is small, rotate the pan every 30 seconds so the crust builds evenly.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Rest, scatter lemon.
steps.detail: Off heat. Cover with a tea towel (not a lid — you don't want condensation) and rest 5 min — rice finishes absorbing. Lemon wedges scattered over. Serve DIRECTLY from the pan to the table — the whole drama of paella is eating from the communal dish with spoons.

title: Tom Yum Goong
description: Hot-and-sour prawn soup. Lemongrass, lime, chilli — turns a head-cold around.
storage: Fridge airtight up to 2 days at most — the prawns set the limit. Reheat to a bare simmer, not a hard boil, and squeeze in fresh lime to revive it; don't freeze.
make_ahead: The infused broth from step 1 can be made a day ahead; reheat it and add the mushrooms, prawns and lime fresh.
leftovers: Strain leftover broth and use it to cook rice or noodles — it carries all the aromatics.

substitutions.swap: thinly sliced chicken breast — simmer 4–5 minutes till cooked through
use fewer and skip the bruising for a gentler bowl
vegetable stock works fine

steps.title: Infuse the broth
steps.content: Simmer stock with ginger, chillies, tomato quarters, 10 min.
steps.detail: Into a pot: 1L chicken stock, a thumb of sliced ginger (or galangal if you can find it), 3 bruised fresh chillies (whack with the back of a knife to release oils), 2 tomatoes cut into quarters, 2 smashed lemongrass stalks if available. Bring to a simmer, 10 min for the aromatics to infuse. Broth becomes fragrant and warming.
tips: Leave the aromatics in chunky pieces — tom yum is traditionally eaten around them, not strained.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.content: Season with fish sauce.
steps.detail: Off the heat briefly while tasting, stir in 3 tbsp fish sauce. This is the salty anchor of Thai soups. Back on heat. Taste — should be savoury with background heat. Adjust fish sauce.
tips: Add two tablespoons first, taste, then the third — stock saltiness varies a lot between brands.

steps.title: Simmer the mushrooms
steps.content: Add mushrooms, simmer 2 min.
steps.detail: Slice 150g mushrooms and add. Bare simmer 2 min — they'll darken and absorb the aromatic broth. Don't overcook or they turn rubbery.

steps.title: Poach the prawns
steps.content: Add prawns, cook till pink, 2 min.
steps.detail: 400g peeled prawns in. They'll turn pink and curl in 2 min — any longer and they're rubbery. The moment they're opaque through, they're done.
warnings: Wash hands and board after handling raw prawns — keep them clear of the garnishes.

steps.title: Add lime off heat
steps.content: Off heat, pour in lime juice.
steps.detail: OFF heat (critical — boiling destroys the fresh hit of lime), pour in the juice of 3 limes. The broth turns from gold-brown to cloudy-creamy — that's the classic tom yum colour. Taste again — should be hot, sour, salty, layered.
tips: Roll the limes hard under your palm before juicing — you'll get noticeably more out of them.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.content: Finish with coriander and spring onion.
steps.detail: Ladle into bowls. A big handful of torn coriander and sliced spring onions scattered on top — not into the pot, or they stew. Extra lime wedge on the side for anyone who wants more sour. Jasmine rice on the side — the broth wants something to slurp alongside.

title: French Onion Soup
description: Caramelised onions, broth that tastes of time, a raft of molten gruyère toast.
storage: Soup keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge or freezes 3 months (without the toasts). Reheat on the stove until piping hot and grill fresh toasts to serve.
make_ahead: The soup itself keeps beautifully — make it up to 3 days ahead and do the cheese toasts fresh when serving.
leftovers: Leftover soup reduced down becomes an outrageous onion gravy for bangers and mash.

substitutions.swap: dry sherry, or a splash of white wine vinegar plus extra stock
chicken stock for a lighter, paler soup — still very good
gruyère is the classic if you're buying specially; comté too

steps.title: Slice the onions
steps.content: Thinly slice onions.
steps.detail: 6 large onions thinly sliced — mandoline if you have one, otherwise a sharp knife and patience. Thin matters; thick chunks won't caramelise evenly. Expect to cry. Halve pole-to-pole and slice from stem end to root for elegant half-moons.
tips: A fridge-cold onion and a truly sharp knife slow the tears more than any other trick.

steps.title: Caramelise 45 minutes
steps.content: Cook in butter, low and slow, 45 min — stir often — till deeply brown.
steps.detail: Big heavy pot, 60g butter melted over medium. Tip all the onions in — they'll look like a small mountain. 5 min, then drop heat to low. Sprinkle a pinch of salt and a pinch of sugar (helps caramelisation kick off). Now STIR every few minutes for 45 MIN. The onions will collapse, release water, then start caramelising as the water evaporates. Don't rush — proper colour is dark amber, almost mahogany.
tips: The sticky brown patches on the pot base are gold — scrape them back into the onions with each stir.

steps.title: Add flour, then wine
steps.content: Stir in flour, then wine; reduce.
steps.detail: Sprinkle in 1 tbsp flour, stir 60 sec to cook out raw flour taste. Pour in 1/2 cup white wine — it'll hiss and bubble. Stir as it reduces by half, 3 min. The wine cuts the sweet onion with acid; essential.
tips: Stand back a touch as the wine hits — the first burst of steam is fierce.

steps.title: Simmer with stock
steps.content: Pour in stock and thyme, simmer 15 min.
steps.detail: Pour in 1.2L beef stock. Thyme sprigs dropped in. Bring to a simmer, 15 min for the flavours to marry. Season — taste first; stock varies, often needs more salt. Pepper generously.

steps.title: Make the cheese toasts
steps.content: Toast bread, pile cheese.
steps.detail: Thick slices of sourdough or baguette, both sides under a hot grill till gold. Pile a generous handful of grated cheese (gruyère is traditional, cheddar works) on each slice. Flash under the grill till cheese is bubbling.
tips: Slightly stale bread is better here — it holds up as a raft instead of going to mush.

steps.title: Grill and serve
steps.content: Ladle soup into oven-proof bowls, float toast, grill till bubbling.
steps.detail: Oven-proof bowls (or use the cheese-topped toasts as floating rafts). Ladle soup in, float a cheese toast on each. If bowls are oven-proof, slide under the grill for 2 min to get the cheese to cascade down the sides. Carefully — bowls get screaming hot. Serve with a napkin to hold the handle.

title: Cream of Mushroom
description: Silky, earthy, deeply comforting. Crack black pepper liberally.
equipment: large heavy pot or deep frying pan
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge, or freezes 2 months — cream soups can split, so reheat gently, whisk as you go, and never let it boil.
make_ahead: Make it a day or two ahead up to the cream stage, then stir the cream through as you gently reheat.
leftovers: A cup of leftover soup reduced in a pan is a ready-made mushroom sauce for steak or pasta.

substitutions.swap: milk plus an extra knob of butter for a lighter soup
chicken stock if you're not keeping it vegetarian
about 1/2 tsp dried thyme, added with the onion

steps.title: Brown the mushrooms
steps.content: Brown mushrooms hard in butter — don't crowd the pan.
steps.detail: Big pan, knob of butter, medium-HIGH heat. 600g mushrooms sliced or quartered, in a single layer — no crowding. Crowded mushrooms steam and go grey; they need high heat to deeply caramelise. Don't touch for 2 min. Then toss, another 2 min. Work in batches if needed. You want deep gold.
tips: The pan should never be so full that you can't see metal between the pieces.

steps.title: Soften the aromatics
steps.content: Set half aside, soften onion and garlic with thyme.
steps.detail: Set HALF the mushrooms aside for garnish. Drop heat to medium. More butter if dry. One finely diced brown onion and 3 grated garlic cloves with a few sprigs of thyme. 6 min till onion is translucent and fragrant.
tips: Pull the thyme sprigs' bare stems out before blending — the leaves fall off as they cook.

steps.title: Make the roux, add stock
steps.content: Stir in flour; whisk in stock.
steps.detail: Sprinkle 2 tbsp flour over, stir 90 sec to cook out raw flour taste. Slowly pour in 700ml vegetable stock while whisking constantly — prevents lumps. Bring to a bare simmer, stirring till thickened enough to coat the back of a spoon.
tips: If a few lumps sneak in anyway, don't panic — the blitz in the next step erases them.

steps.title: Simmer, then blitz
steps.content: Return mushrooms, simmer 10 min; blitz half.
steps.detail: Return the reserved (non-garnish) mushrooms to the pot. Simmer 10 min for flavours to meld. Blitz half with a stick blender for silky richness while keeping some texture — or blitz all of it for completely smooth. Your call.
warnings: Keep the stick blender head fully under the surface before switching on — hot soup sprays badly.

steps.title: Finish with cream
steps.content: Fold in cream, reheat gently.
steps.detail: Off heat. Pour in 200ml cream. Stir gently to combine. Reheat very gently — boiling splits cream. Taste and season generously. Pepper is crucial for mushroom soup; grind MORE than feels right.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.content: Top with reserved mushrooms and bread.
steps.detail: Ladle into bowls. Top with the reserved caramelised mushrooms (they add texture and look gorgeous). Extra thyme leaves, a swirl of cream. Crusty bread alongside for dunking — the soup is thick enough to carry generous bread. More black pepper at the table.
tips: Reheat the reserved mushrooms 30 seconds in the empty pan so the garnish isn't cold on hot soup.

title: Choc Chip Cookies
description: Crisp edges, chewy centres, puddles of dark chocolate. Straight from the oven.
equipment: 2 baking trays lined with baking paper
storage: Airtight tin at room temperature 4–5 days — they soften from crisp after day one. Baked cookies also freeze up to 2 months.
make_ahead: The dough balls freeze brilliantly — freeze solid on a tray, then bag for up to 3 months and bake straight from frozen, adding 2 minutes.
leftovers: Cookies past their best blitz into a great topping for ice cream or the base of a cheesecake.

substitutions.swap: a roughly chopped chocolate block, or half chocolate and half chopped walnuts
1/2 tsp bicarb soda gives flatter, chewier cookies

steps.title: Cream butter and sugars
steps.content: Cream butter and sugars till pale.
steps.detail: Stand mixer or beaters: 200g soft butter, 3/4 cup white sugar, 3/4 cup brown sugar. Beat 4 min on medium-high till pale and fluffy. The paleness is air; air is what gives cookies their spread and soft centre. Brown sugar brings caramel notes; white brings crisp edges. Both needed.
tips: Butter is soft enough when a finger leaves a dent without sinking straight through.

steps.title: Beat in eggs, vanilla
steps.content: Beat in eggs and vanilla.
steps.detail: Crack in 2 eggs one at a time, beating between each. 2 tsp vanilla. Mix till combined — should look silky and glossy. If it looks split, another 30 sec of beating usually fixes it.
tips: Crack each egg into a cup first — fishing shell out of the batter is miserable.

steps.title: Fold in the dry
steps.content: Fold in flour, baking powder, pinch of salt.
steps.detail: Sift 2.25 cups flour, 1 tsp baking powder, pinch of salt into the bowl. Mix on LOW speed just till combined — no flour streaks. Overmixing develops gluten and gives you tough cookies. Stop the moment it's uniform.

steps.title: Stir through chocolate
steps.content: Stir through chocolate chips.
steps.detail: Fold in 2 cups chocolate chips (or roughly chopped chocolate — irregular chunks make better cookies than uniform chips). Use a spatula, quick folds.

steps.title: Roll and space balls
steps.content: Roll into balls on lined trays, leave space.
steps.detail: Scoop into 2-tablespoon balls onto lined trays with AT LEAST 5cm between each — cookies spread dramatically. Chilling the dough balls 30 min in the fridge gives taller, thicker cookies (spread less in the oven); skipping it gives flatter, crispier ones. Your call.
tips: An ice-cream scoop gives evenly sized cookies that all finish baking at the same moment.

steps.title: Bake, cool on tray
steps.content: Bake 12 min at 180°C till edges set; cool on tray.
steps.detail: 180°C oven, middle rack, 12 min. Pull when edges are set and deeply gold but MIDDLES STILL LOOK UNDERDONE — cookies keep cooking on the hot tray. Let them cool on the tray 5 min (sets the structure), then transfer to a rack. Soft-centred, slightly crisp-edged, chocolate puddles throughout.
tips: Bake one tray at a time on the middle rack — it beats juggling two trays for evenness.

title: New York Cheesecake
description: Dense, tangy, 1980s-sitcom perfect. Overnight patience required.
equipment: deep roasting tray for the water bath
rolling pin and a sturdy bag for crushing biscuits
storage: Covered in the fridge up to 5 days. Slices freeze well up to a month — wrap individually and thaw overnight in the fridge.
make_ahead: It has to be made ahead — bake it the day before serving. It's at its best on days one to three.

substitutions.swap: any plain sweet biscuit — Marie, Nice, or gingernuts for a spicier base
any berries, or passionfruit pulp spooned straight over
sour cream for an extra-tangy filling

steps.title: Press and bake base
steps.content: Melt butter, stir into crumbs, press into springform base.
steps.detail: Crush 200g of sweet biscuits (digestive or graham crackers) in a bag with a rolling pin — fine crumbs. Melt 100g butter, stir through the crumbs till evenly soaked. Press firmly into the base of a 23cm springform tin (loose-base = easy unmoulding). Use the back of a spoon to compact. 180°C oven 10 min till set. Cool.
tips: The buttered crumbs should clump like wet sand when squeezed — too dry and the base crumbles under the knife.

steps.title: Beat cheese and sugar
steps.content: Beat cream cheese and sugar till smooth.
steps.detail: In a stand mixer: 1kg room-temp cream cheese (critical — cold cream cheese gives lumpy cheesecake) and 1.25 cups sugar. Beat on medium 3 min till completely smooth. Scrape the bowl down often — lumps at this stage mean lumps in the finished cake.
tips: Take the cream cheese out 2 hours ahead — genuinely room temperature, not fridge-cold with soft edges.

steps.title: Add eggs and flavourings
steps.content: Add eggs one at a time, cream, vanilla, lemon, flour.
steps.detail: Add 4 eggs ONE AT A TIME, beating just till incorporated after each (over-beating with eggs in aerates too much and causes cracks). Pour in 1/2 cup cream, 2 tsp vanilla, juice and zest of one lemon, 3 tbsp flour. Mix just till smooth.
tips: Zest the lemon before you juice it — it's near impossible the other way around.

steps.title: Pour over the base
steps.content: Pour over base.
steps.detail: Wrap the outside of the springform tin in two layers of foil (seals against water bath). Pour the cream cheese mix over the biscuit base, smooth the top with a spatula. Tap the tin on the bench to release air bubbles.
tips: Use single unbroken sheets of foil — a seam below the waterline lets the bath in and gives a soggy base.

steps.title: Bake in water bath
steps.content: Bake 70 min at 160°C in a water bath; oven off, door ajar 30 min.
steps.detail: Place the tin in a deep roasting tray. Pour boiling water into the tray to come halfway up the tin sides — the water bath cooks it gently and evenly. 160°C oven (LOW is essential), middle rack, 70 min. Centre should wobble like set jelly when gently shaken, edges set. Turn oven OFF, prop the door ajar, leave inside 30 min — slow cool prevents cracks.
warnings: Set the tray on the oven rack first, then pour in the boiling water — never carry a full tray of it across the kitchen.

steps.title: Chill overnight, top
steps.content: Chill overnight. Top with berries.
steps.detail: Cool to room temp on the bench, then chill OVERNIGHT. Non-negotiable — the flavour, texture, and set all transform over 12 hours. Unmould from the springform, slice with a hot knife dipped in water between cuts for clean edges. Top with macerated strawberries (hulled, halved, tossed with sugar and a squeeze of lemon). Cream cheese cake eats best with something sharp.
tips: Macerate the strawberries about 30 minutes before serving — long enough to go syrupy, not soggy.

title: Lemon Delicious Pudding
description: Self-saucing magic: fluffy sponge above, tangy curd beneath.
equipment: deep roasting tray for the water bath
storage: Covered in the fridge up to 2 days — the sauce soaks in as it sits. Reheat portions gently in the microwave; it won't be as puffed but still tastes right. Don't freeze.

substitutions.swap: 2 oranges plus 1 lemon for a softer citrus version
half milk, half cream for a richer pudding

steps.title: Cream butter, sugar, zest
steps.content: Cream butter, sugar, lemon zest.
steps.detail: Stand mixer or beaters: 60g soft butter, 3/4 cup sugar, zest of 3 lemons. Beat 3 min till pale and fluffy — rub sugar and zest between fingers first (releases lemon oils into the sugar). The zest is where lemon's essential aromatics live; this one step transforms the dish.
tips: Zest only the yellow — the white pith underneath is bitter.

steps.title: Add yolks, flour, milk
steps.content: Beat in yolks and flour alternately with milk.
steps.detail: Separate 3 eggs — yolks into the butter mix, whites into a spotlessly clean bowl. Beat the yolks in one at a time. Sift in 1/2 cup plain flour. Then 1.5 cups milk, alternating with more flour so it doesn't split. Batter will look quite loose — that's right. The milk forms the sauce layer below.
tips: Separate each egg over a small bowl first — one streak of yolk in the whites stops them whipping later.

steps.title: Stir in the juice
steps.content: Stir in lemon juice.
steps.detail: Juice the 3 lemons. Stir into the loose batter. It might look curdled — totally fine, that's the dairy hitting acid. Stir gently, don't fret.

steps.title: Whip and fold whites
steps.content: Whisk whites to soft peaks, fold in.
steps.detail: Clean beaters (any trace of fat prevents whipping), beat whites to SOFT peaks — flops slightly when you lift the beater. Stiffer = brutal to fold. Fold a third into the batter vigorously to loosen it, then fold the rest gently in sweeping strokes.
tips: Fold in figure-eights with a spatula, turning the bowl as you go — you're keeping air in, not mixing a cake.

steps.title: Set up the water bath
steps.content: Pour into buttered dish, sit in water bath.
steps.detail: Butter a 1.5L baking dish. Pour the batter in — looks shallow and liquidy. Place the dish in a deep roasting tray. Pour boiling water into the tray to come halfway up the dish sides. The water bath is what creates the magic separation: sponge on top, curd-sauce below.
warnings: Set the tray on the oven shelf first, then pour the boiling water — don't walk a sloshing tray across the kitchen.

steps.title: Bake till golden, wobbly
steps.content: Bake 45 min at 170°C till golden and wobbly.
steps.detail: 170°C oven, middle rack, 45 min. Done when the top is golden and slightly puffed, centre has a faint wobble, and you can see the sauce bubbling up at the edges. Rest 5 min. Scoop with a big spoon, making sure each serve gets both the sponge top AND the lemon curd underneath. Cream or ice cream.

title: Rocky Road
description: Marshmallows, nuts, glacé strawberries, a chocolate mortar. School-fête classic.
equipment: heatproof bowl and small pot (double boiler)
storage: Airtight in the fridge up to 2 weeks; it goes soft and smeary in a warm room. Freezes 2 months, though the marshmallows firm up.
make_ahead: Built for it — it needs 2 hours to set anyway and keeps for a fortnight, so make it well before the fête or gift day.

substitutions.swap: roasted peanuts or pistachios — salted works if you like the contrast
dried cranberries for a sharper chew
milk chocolate for a sweeter, softer set — school-fête authentic

steps.title: Melt the chocolate
steps.content: Melt chocolate with butter, stir smooth.
steps.detail: Chop 400g dark chocolate into small chunks (small = melts evenly) and into a heatproof bowl with 50g butter. Set over a pan of barely-simmering water — don't let the bowl touch the water, it'll overheat. Stir occasionally till smooth and glossy, 4 min. Off heat. Rest 5 min to cool slightly.
warnings: Keep every drop of water out of the chocolate — even a steam drip makes it seize into a grainy mess.

steps.title: Chop the mix-ins
steps.content: Chop nuts, quarter any large fruit.
steps.detail: Chop 1 cup walnuts roughly into bite-sized pieces — smaller bits crumble, bigger pieces are the right nougat-y chew. Quarter any large dried fruit (glacé cherries, dried strawberries) so they tuck in neatly. Have everything ready before the chocolate starts setting.

steps.title: Stir everything through
steps.content: Stir everything through chocolate.
steps.detail: Into the slightly cooled chocolate: walnuts, 1/2 cup almonds, 1/2 cup raisins, chopped glacé strawberries, and 2 cups marshmallows (pink ones are traditional). Stir till every piece is coated in chocolate. Work fast — chocolate thickens as it cools.
tips: If the chocolate thickens too much to coat, sit the bowl back over the warm pot for 30 seconds.

steps.title: Press into the tin
steps.content: Tip into lined tin, press flat.
steps.detail: Line a 20cm square tin with baking paper, overhanging for easy lifting. Tip the mix in, press flat with the back of a spoon or a spatula. Flatten corners, push down to remove air pockets. The surface doesn't need to be perfect; rustic is the look.

steps.title: Chill till firm
steps.content: Chill 2 hr till firm.
steps.detail: Refrigerate 2 hours till firm. Or freezer 30 min if you're in a hurry. Chocolate needs to be fully set or it smears when you cut.

steps.title: Lift out and slice
steps.content: Cut into slabs.
steps.detail: Lift out of the tin using the paper. Sharp knife to cut into chunky slabs — rocky road should look rustic and uneven, not precision-engineered. Airtight tin in the fridge, up to 2 weeks. School fêtes, gift bags, midnight snacking.
tips: Run the knife under hot water and dry it between cuts for cleaner edges.

title: Apple Pie
description: A lattice top, cinnamon apples, cream or custard. Anyone who says otherwise is wrong.
storage: Fridge, covered, up to 3 days; reheat slices at 160°C for 10 minutes to re-crisp the pastry — the microwave makes it soggy. Baked pie freezes up to 2 months.
make_ahead: Assemble the whole pie, wrap and fridge it up to a day ahead, then egg-wash and bake fresh — or freeze it unbaked up to a month and bake from frozen, adding 15 minutes.
leftovers: Cold pie with a slice of sharp cheddar for breakfast is a legitimate tradition.

substitutions.swap: all Granny Smith works fine; tinned pie apple in a hurry — skip the resting
puff pastry for the lid gives a flakier top — keep shortcrust underneath
mixed spice, or a grating of nutmeg alongside

steps.title: Prep the apples
steps.content: Slice apples, toss with sugar, cinnamon, flour, lemon.
steps.detail: Peel, core, and slice 6 apples (mix of tart Granny Smith and sweet Pink Lady is perfect). In a bowl, toss with 3/4 cup sugar, 2 tsp cinnamon, 2 tbsp flour (thickens the juices into glossy sauce, not water), juice of one lemon (stops browning, brightens flavour). Rest 15 min for juices to start drawing out.
tips: Keep slices around 5 mm — thin enough to soften, thick enough not to collapse into mush.

steps.title: Line the tin, fill
steps.content: Line tin with pastry, fill.
steps.detail: Roll out one sheet of shortcrust pastry into a circle big enough to line a 23cm pie tin with a 2cm overhang. Press into corners without stretching (stretched pastry snaps back and shrinks). Pile in the apple mixture, heaping slightly — it'll settle as it cooks.
tips: Leave most of the pooled juice behind in the bowl — a splash is fine, the lot makes a soggy base.

steps.title: Dot butter, weave lattice
steps.content: Dot with butter, lattice second pastry over top.
steps.detail: Dot 30g of butter across the apple (melts into the sauce, adds richness). Roll out the second pastry sheet. For a lattice: cut into 2cm strips, weave over-under across the top. For simple: lay the whole sheet on top and cut 5 steam vents. Trim overhang to 1cm, fold under and crimp with a fork.
tips: If the pastry goes warm and floppy mid-lattice, slide it back in the fridge for 10 minutes — cold pastry behaves.

steps.title: Egg wash and sugar
steps.content: Egg wash, sprinkle sugar.
steps.detail: Beat one egg with a splash of milk. Brush over the pastry top — this is what gives pie its deep gold glossy shine. Sprinkle a tablespoon of sugar across the top — melts into caramelised sparkle.

steps.title: Bake till bubbling
steps.content: Bake 55 min at 190°C till deep gold and bubbling.
steps.detail: 190°C oven, middle rack, 55 min till the pastry is deep gold and the apple juice is BUBBLING visibly through the vents/lattice. That bubbling means the starch in the flour has thickened the sauce. If the edges brown too fast, cover with foil strips.

steps.title: Rest before cutting
steps.content: Rest 30 min before cutting — juices need to set.
steps.detail: REST 30 min before cutting — the juice needs to set, or it pools out messily and the filling is loose. Cold pie is fine, but warm pie with vanilla ice cream melting into it is transcendent. Custard if you're traditional.

title: Berry Smoothie Bowl
description: Cold, pink, crunchy on top. Breakfast that looks like a postcard.
storage: Doesn't keep — the base melts to a drinkable smoothie within minutes of serving. Blend just before eating; leftover base can be refrozen once and re-blitzed briefly.
make_ahead: Peel and freeze ripe bananas in chunks whenever you have them going spotty — the bowl depends on properly frozen fruit, and the blitzing takes minutes once you do.
leftovers: Thin any leftover base with extra milk and drink it as a regular smoothie.

substitutions.swap: any frozen fruit — mango or a tropical mix works a treat
coconut yoghurt keeps it dairy-free (use a plant milk too)
maple syrup
any nut or seed — walnuts, pepitas or sunflower seeds

steps.title: Blitz the base
steps.content: Blitz frozen banana, berries, yoghurt, milk, honey to thick cream.
steps.detail: Blender or high-powered food processor: 2 frozen bananas (peel and freeze ripe bananas in chunks — the base of every good smoothie bowl), 2 cups mixed frozen berries, 1/2 cup yoghurt, 1/2 cup milk (any kind), 1 tbsp honey. Blitz on high 90 sec till thick and spoonable — should be ice-cream consistency, NOT drinkable. If too thick, splash more milk; too loose, more frozen fruit.
tips: If the blades spin without catching, stop and scrape down, or add milk 1 tbsp at a time — never water.

steps.title: Fill and level bowls
steps.content: Spoon into bowls.
steps.detail: Shallow wide bowls (NOT glasses — smoothie bowls are for topping, and you need surface area). Spoon the mix in, level the surface with a spatula. The bowl should look like a blank pink canvas.
tips: Chill the bowls in the freezer while you blitz — cold bowls buy you a few extra minutes before it melts.

steps.title: Scatter the toppings
steps.content: Top with oats, chopped almond, fresh berries.
steps.detail: Artful scattering: 1/4 cup oats in a small pile, chopped almonds in another, fresh strawberries in a fan, a drizzle of honey, maybe chia seeds and shaved coconut if you're feeling fancy. Smoothie bowls are 50% topping aesthetics. Eat immediately with a spoon — it melts within 5 min in a warm kitchen.
tips: Toppings in neat rows or piles read better than a jumble — and toasting the oats in a dry pan first is worth 3 minutes.

title: Crispy Hash Browns
description: Lacy-edge golden patties. Ideal diner breakfast, elite weekend energy.
storage: Best straight from the pan. Leftovers keep 2 days airtight in the fridge; re-crisp in a hot dry pan or a 200°C oven — the microwave makes them soggy.
leftovers: Top a leftover hash brown with a fried egg, or tuck one into a bacon-and-egg roll.

substitutions.swap: 2 sliced spring onions, or leave it out for plain potato
all oil works and won't burn, though you lose the nutty flavour

steps.title: Grate and wring
steps.content: Grate potato and onion, squeeze very dry.
steps.detail: Coarse-grate 600g potatoes (skin on is fine — more texture, more nutrition) and half a brown onion. Sprinkle over a big pinch of salt, toss, rest 5 min. Tip into a clean tea towel and WRING HARD over the sink. You should get half a cup of liquid out minimum. Wet shreds = greasy, soggy hash browns.
tips: No spare tea towel? Squeeze fistfuls hard over a colander — several small squeezes beat one big one.

steps.title: Season the mix
steps.content: Season well.
steps.detail: Tip the wrung potato and onion into a clean bowl. Season generously — salt, pepper, maybe a pinch of smoked paprika. Potatoes are bland without enough salt. Toss to distribute.
tips: Fry a teaspoonful and taste it — the raw mix won't tell you if the salt is right.

steps.title: Press firm patties
steps.content: Shape into patties, press flat.
steps.detail: Shape into 4 patties, each about 2cm thick and 10cm across. Press firmly so they hold together — the starch in the potato is what binds them. Don't be gentle; you want dense patties that stay intact when flipped.
tips: Slightly wet hands stop the mix sticking while you press.

steps.title: Fry until lace-crisp
steps.content: Fry in hot butter and oil 4 min a side till lace-crisp.
steps.detail: Wide pan, medium heat, 40g butter plus 2 tbsp oil (butter for flavour, oil stops it burning). When foaming, patties in, don't touch for 4 min — CRUCIAL for the lacy crust that's the whole point. Flip once carefully with a wide spatula. 4 min the other side. Deep gold, lace-edged, crisp.
tips: After the first 4 min, lift an edge — it should release easily; if it grips the pan, give it another minute.
warnings: Butter and oil will spit when the patties go in — lower them in away from you.

steps.title: Drain and salt hot
steps.content: Drain on paper, hit with sea salt.
steps.detail: Lift onto paper towel — briefly, just to catch excess fat. Hit with flaky sea salt immediately while hot (salt absorbs best when hot). Serve with fried eggs, bacon, a grill of tomato, whatever breakfast looks like today.

title: The Ultimate Cheese Toastie
description: Butter-soaked sourdough, a river of cheddar, a crack of pepper.
storage: A toastie doesn't keep — the crust softens within the hour. If you must revive one, use a dry frying pan over medium heat, never the microwave.

substitutions.swap: any good melter — tasty, gruyère, or half mozzarella for stretch
wholegrain mustard, or a thin smear of tomato chutney
any sturdy thick-cut loaf — a supermarket vienna or tin loaf both work

steps.title: Butter the outsides
steps.content: Butter outsides of bread heavily.
steps.detail: Softened butter, generous layer on ONE side of each slice of sourdough — this goes on the OUTSIDE of the sandwich, touching the pan. Butter's the whole reason a toastie goes gold and crispy. Don't skimp; it should look thickly buttered, not scraped.
tips: Cold butter tears bread — if you forgot to soften it, grate it over the slices and spread once it slackens.

steps.title: Spread the dijon
steps.content: Spread inside with dijon.
steps.detail: Flip slices butter-side-down on a board. Spread 1 tsp dijon on the NON-butter side of one slice — that's the inside. Mustard under cheese is a life-changer; it cuts the richness and adds zing.

steps.title: Build the sandwich
steps.content: Pile cheese between, close.
steps.detail: Pile 80g freshly grated cheddar (grate it yourself — pre-grated has anti-caking agents that prevent proper melting) on the mustard side. Place the other slice butter-side-UP on top. Butter-butter on the outsides, cheese-mustard in the middle.
tips: A good crack of pepper over the cheese before you close it lifts the whole toastie.

steps.title: Fry gold and pressed
steps.content: Fry over medium till deep gold, 3 min a side, pressing.
steps.detail: Medium heat pan (NOT high — burns before cheese melts). No extra oil needed. Sandwich in, press gently with a spatula. 3 min first side till deep gold — keep checking. Flip once, 3 min the other side. Pressing melts cheese and gives better pan contact.
tips: Listen to the pan — a gentle steady sizzle is right; loud spitting means the heat is too high.

steps.title: Rest, then slice
steps.content: Rest 1 min, slice on the bias.
steps.detail: Rest on a board 60 sec — lets the cheese set slightly, stops it all sliding out when you bite. Slice on the bias (diagonal) with a sharp knife — the classic diner cut, and it gives you better grip. Eat immediately while crispy; toasties go sad fast.

title: French Omelette
description: Pale yellow, soft folded curds, a chive whisper. Julia-approved.
storage: An omelette doesn't keep — the soft curds toughen and weep in the fridge. Make each one fresh; it only takes three minutes.

substitutions.swap: fresh chives are the classic — parsley or dill also work
gruyère or any quiet melting cheese — a little goat's cheese is lovely too

steps.title: Beat until uniform
steps.content: Beat eggs with salt and pepper.
steps.detail: 3 eggs in a bowl. Beat vigorously with a fork for 30 sec — you want them completely homogeneous, pale yellow, slightly frothy. Streaks of white = uneven texture. Pinch of salt, crack of pepper whisked in.
tips: Lift the fork high as you beat — a little air keeps the curds tender.

steps.title: Foam the butter
steps.content: Melt butter in a 20cm pan till foam subsides.
steps.detail: 20cm non-stick pan over medium-low heat (low is crucial — high heat browns eggs, which is NOT what a French omelette wants). Drop 20g butter in. Wait till the butter melts and foams — the foam means water is evaporating and the butter is hot enough. Don't wait for it to brown.
tips: If the butter browns, wipe out the pan and start again — brown specks show on a pale omelette.

steps.title: Swirl and stir
steps.content: Pour in eggs, swirl and stir with fork, 30 sec.
steps.detail: Pour eggs in all at once. Immediately start swirling the pan AND stirring with a fork in rapid small circles (hold both tools). This creates tiny curds while keeping the base smooth. 30 seconds only — the texture should look like soft wet scrambled eggs.
tips: Setting faster than you can stir? Lift the pan off the heat for a few seconds and keep the fork moving.

steps.title: Roll the cigar
steps.content: When set but glossy, tilt pan, fold into a cigar shape.
steps.detail: Stop stirring. Let the base set for 10 sec. Tilt the pan away from you. Use the fork or a spatula to lift one side over the middle, then with a firm wrist, tip the omelette out so it rolls over itself onto the plate — the far edge tucks under. Should look like a plump cigar, pale yellow, no browning.
tips: Run the spatula around the edge first — a freed edge folds cleanly.

steps.title: Plate and scatter
steps.content: Tip onto plate, scatter chives.
steps.detail: Scatter chives (or spring onion greens) across the top. For grown-up: drop grated cheese or a spoon of soft herbs into the middle before rolling. Eat immediately — the inside should still be slightly loose ('baveuse' in French, meaning 'slobbery' — a compliment here).

title: Overnight Bircher Muesli
description: Grated apple, soaked oats, yoghurt, honey. Breakfast that makes itself.
equipment: large jar or bowl with a lid
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge; if keeping past a day, stir yoghurt through each serve rather than the whole batch. Don't freeze — it goes watery.
make_ahead: This is make-ahead by design — assemble it the night before, and a double batch sorts breakfasts for half the week.

substitutions.swap: any plant milk soaks the oats just as well
sultanas, dried cranberries or chopped dates
walnuts or hazelnuts, or pepitas for a nut-free version

steps.title: Soak the oats
steps.content: Stir oats, milk, half the honey, cinnamon.
steps.detail: Big jar or bowl: 2 cups rolled oats (NOT instant — instant goes gluey overnight), 1.5 cups milk, 1.5 tbsp honey, 1/2 tsp cinnamon. Stir till oats are fully submerged. The oats will drink the milk overnight and turn porridge-soft.

steps.title: Grate in the apple
steps.content: Grate in apple, add raisins, cover.
steps.detail: Grate 2 apples directly into the jar — skin on (fibre) if organic, peeled if not. The grated apple adds sweetness, moisture, and Swiss-authentic texture. Stir in 1/4 cup raisins. Lid on tight.
tips: Hold the apple whole and grate down to the core, rotating as you go — quicker than peeling and coring.

steps.title: Let it soak overnight
steps.content: Chill overnight.
steps.detail: Fridge overnight — minimum 4 hours, ideally 8–12. Oats swell, raisins plump, apple melts into the mix, honey disperses. Classic Bircher is a slow cold infusion; you can't rush it.

steps.title: Stir through yoghurt
steps.content: Stir through yoghurt in the morning.
steps.detail: Morning. Stir in 1 cup yoghurt till completely mixed through. The yoghurt brings tang that balances the sweetness. If it's too thick, splash of milk. Too loose, extra spoon of yoghurt.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Top with remaining honey and almonds.
steps.detail: Spoon into bowls. Drizzle with more honey to taste. Top with 1/3 cup chopped almonds and fresh fruit if you have it (sliced banana, berries, pear). Eats chilled; it's summer breakfast in Switzerland and it works anywhere.
tips: Toast the almonds in a dry pan for a couple of minutes first if you have time — it doubles their flavour.

title: Pull-Apart Garlic Bread
description: Buttery, herby, the loaf that vanishes first. Every-night energy.
storage: Best the day it's baked. Leftovers keep 2 days airtight; re-warm wrapped in foil at 180°C for 10 minutes — the microwave turns it leathery.
make_ahead: Butter and wrap the loaf up to a day ahead in the fridge, or freeze it wrapped for a month — bake from cold with a few extra minutes in the foil.
leftovers: Cube leftover garlic bread and crisp it in a pan for outrageous croutons.

substitutions.swap: basil, oregano, or 2 tsp dried Italian herbs at a pinch
any hard grating cheese, or skip it — the loaf carries on fine

steps.title: Slice the accordion
steps.content: Slice loaf at 2cm intervals, almost all the way through.
steps.detail: Crusty loaf (sourdough baguette or vienna works best). Use a serrated knife. Slice at 2cm intervals but DON'T cut all the way through — leave 1cm connected at the bottom. The loaf stays intact but opens up like an accordion for spreading. Pull-apart magic.
tips: Rest a wooden spoon handle either side of the loaf — the knife stops on them instead of cutting through.

steps.title: Mash the garlic butter
steps.content: Cream butter with crushed garlic, chopped parsley, parmesan.
steps.detail: Soft butter (room temp for easy mixing). 5 crushed garlic cloves (use a press or grate on a microplane — finely chopped doesn't distribute as well), a BIG handful of chopped parsley, 50g grated parmesan, pinch of salt. Mash together with a fork into a green-flecked paste. Taste — should be strongly garlicky and herby.

steps.title: Fill every slit
steps.content: Slather between every slice.
steps.detail: Open each slit of the loaf with your fingers, smear the butter mix generously into every gap. Don't skimp — this is the whole point. Also spread a thin layer across the top and cut faces. Messy hands are expected.
tips: The back of a teaspoon pushes the butter deep into each gap without tearing the loaf.

steps.title: Wrap and bake
steps.content: Wrap in foil, bake 12 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: Wrap the whole loaf tightly in foil (keeps moisture in so the bread steams soft while the butter infuses). 200°C oven, middle rack, 12 min. The butter will have fully melted into the bread by now.

steps.title: Grill until crisp
steps.content: Open foil, grill 3 min till crispy tops.
steps.detail: Open the foil, leaving it like a boat around the loaf. Drizzle 2 tbsp olive oil across the top, scatter extra parmesan. Back under the grill on high for 3 min — watch CLOSELY — till the tops are crispy-gold. Tear apart with hands at the table. Hot, garlicky, herby, communal.
warnings: Grills go from gold to burnt in under a minute — stay at the oven and watch through the door.

title: Yorkshire Puddings
description: Sky-high, golden, essential alongside a roast. Rest the batter.
storage: Best straight from the oven while towering. Cooled ones keep 2 days airtight in the fridge or freeze for a month; re-crisp at 200°C for 5 minutes — never the microwave.
make_ahead: The batter improves with rest — make it the night before, keep it covered in the fridge, and give it a brief whisk before pouring.
leftovers: Reheat a leftover yorkie and fill it with roast scraps and gravy — a legitimate lunch.

substitutions.swap: half milk, half water makes an even crisper, lighter pudding
beef dripping is the traditional choice and worth it with a roast

steps.title: Whisk, then rest
steps.content: Whisk eggs, milk, flour, salt smooth. Rest 30 min.
steps.detail: Whisk 3 eggs, 1 cup milk, 1 cup flour, pinch of salt till completely smooth — no lumps. An actual whisk works better than a fork. Rest the batter 30 min on the bench (or overnight in the fridge). Rest relaxes the gluten and lets the flour hydrate, which is the secret to proper rise.
tips: The finished batter should pour like thin cream — if it's thicker, whisk in a splash more milk.

steps.title: Oil each hole
steps.content: Pour 1 tsp oil into each muffin hole.
steps.detail: Muffin tin. Pour 1 tsp of vegetable oil (or beef dripping for traditional) into each hole. Don't use butter — it burns at the temperatures needed. Oil pooling in the bottom is what generates the crucial steam on contact with cold batter.

steps.title: Heat until smoking
steps.content: Heat tin 10 min at 230°C till smoking.
steps.detail: 230°C oven (hot as you dare). Slide the muffin tin WITH the oil in. 10 min till the oil is smoking-hot. You want to see wisps of smoke — that's the temperature you need. Without smoking-hot oil, no rise.

steps.title: Pour in fast
steps.content: Fill each hole 3/4 with batter.
steps.detail: Pull the tin out carefully (steaming fat is dangerous — angle away from you). Quickly but carefully, pour batter to fill each hole 3/4 full. It'll hiss and sizzle violently against the hot oil — that's the magic. Back in the oven FAST — every second the oven's open, temperature drops.
tips: Pour from a jug in one steady motion per hole — dribbling cools the oil and costs you rise.
warnings: The oil is smoking hot and will hiss hard at the batter — keep your face and arms back, mitts on.

steps.title: Bake without peeking
steps.content: Do not open oven. Bake 20 min.
steps.detail: 20 min. DO NOT OPEN the oven door, not even to peek. Yorkshires rise by steam, and steam needs heat. Opening the door collapses them irreversibly. Trust.
tips: Use the oven light and window for progress checks — that's the only peek allowed.

steps.title: Serve them immediately
steps.content: They're done when tall and deep gold.
steps.detail: They're done when towering above the tin, deep gold, shatter-crisp outside, soft inside. Eat IMMEDIATELY — they deflate as they cool. Serve alongside a roast with gravy pooled in the hollow centre. That's the whole point.
tips: If they must wait, hold them in the turned-off oven with the door ajar — better than the bench.

title: Chimichurri Steak
description: Char-kissed steak, vivid green herb sauce. South American Friday.
storage: Cooked steak keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge — slice thin and eat cold or barely warmed, as reheating pushes it past medium-rare. Chimichurri keeps 3 days with a film of oil on top.
make_ahead: Chimichurri keeps 3 days in the fridge and is arguably better the next day — make it ahead and bring it to room temperature before serving.
leftovers: Cold sliced steak and leftover chimichurri make a serious sandwich or steak salad.

substitutions.swap: 1/2 tsp dried chilli flakes, or leave it out for the heat-averse
white wine vinegar, or half the amount of sherry vinegar
porterhouse or rump work well — adjust searing time to thickness

steps.title: Temper and season
steps.content: Bring steak to room temp, season well.
steps.detail: Take steaks out of the fridge 30 min before cooking — cold meat cooks unevenly and tough. Pat very dry with paper towel. Season both sides HEAVILY with sea salt flakes and cracked pepper — steak can take more than you think. Don't oil the steak; oil the pan.
warnings: Wash your hands and anything the raw beef touched before you start on the herbs.

steps.title: Blitz the sauce
steps.content: Blitz parsley, oregano, garlic, chilli, vinegar, oil to a chunky sauce.
steps.detail: Food processor: 2 big handfuls of parsley (stems and leaves, stems have flavour), 1 tsp dried oregano, 4 garlic cloves, 1 red chilli, 2 tbsp red wine vinegar, 1/2 cup good olive oil, juice of a lemon, big pinch of salt. Pulse to a rough chunky sauce — not smooth pesto. Taste — should be sharp, grassy, garlicky. Rest 10 min for flavours to marry.
tips: No processor? Chop everything fine by hand and stir through the oil — the texture is arguably better.

steps.title: Sear hard, flip once
steps.content: Sear steak 3 min a side in a very hot pan.
steps.detail: Cast-iron or heavy pan on the hottest flame you have till you see wisps of smoke. Splash of high-smoke-point oil. Steaks in, don't touch for 3 min — let the crust form. Flip once, 3 min for medium-rare on 2cm thick. Finger test: the base of your thumb when you touch thumb to middle finger is roughly medium-rare. If you own a meat thermometer, pull the steak at 52–54°C in the centre for medium-rare — it climbs a few degrees while it rests.
warnings: A near-smoking pan will smoke the kitchen — rangehood on. Never throw water at a fat flare-up; smother it with a lid.

steps.title: Rest, then slice
steps.content: Rest 5 min, slice against the grain.
steps.detail: Lift onto a board, tent loosely with foil, rest 5 min — non-negotiable. Juice redistributes into the meat instead of pooling on the board. Slice ACROSS the grain (perpendicular to muscle fibres) into thick slabs.
tips: Find the direction of the fibres before cutting — on skirt they run obviously across the steak.

steps.title: Sauce it generously
steps.content: Spoon chimichurri generously.
steps.detail: Plate the slices, pour any resting juices over. Spoon chimichurri GENEROUSLY — don't be shy, it should drown the steak in green flecks. South American steakhouse style. Crusty bread alongside to mop up the sauce-blood mix. Red wine.

title: BBQ Pulled Pork
description: Low-and-slow shoulder that falls into soft strands. Serve on rolls, slaw on top.
equipment: large heavy oven-safe pot with lid
storage: Keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge or freezes in its sauce for 3 months; reheat covered with a splash of stock or water until piping hot. Dress the slaw fresh each time.
make_ahead: Rub the pork the night before for deeper flavour, or cook the lot a day ahead — pulled pork reheats brilliantly in its sauce.
leftovers: Leftover pork stretches into tacos, quesadillas, loaded baked potatoes or fried rice.

substitutions.swap: beef or vegetable stock, or 250 ml apple juice for a sweeter braise
apple cider vinegar — a touch sharper, equally good
Greek yoghurt for a lighter slaw dressing

steps.title: Rub and rest
steps.content: Rub pork with paprika, sugar, cumin, salt.
steps.detail: 2kg pork shoulder (the fattier the better for pulled pork). Pat dry. Mix 2 tbsp smoked paprika, 1/3 cup brown sugar, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tbsp salt, 1 tsp pepper. Rub HARD into every surface of the meat — every fold, every side. Press it in like you mean it. Rest 30 min at room temp (or overnight in the fridge for deeper flavour).
warnings: Wash your hands, board and anything else the raw pork touched before moving on.

steps.title: Sear every face
steps.content: Sear on all sides.
steps.detail: Heavy oven-safe pot with a lid (Dutch oven ideal). Splash of oil over medium-high till smoking. Pork in, sear for 4 min per side till each face is deep mahogany. Don't rush — every side needs colour. The crust you build now is the flavour foundation.
tips: Stand the pork on its ends with tongs so even the awkward faces get colour.

steps.title: Whisk the braise
steps.content: Whisk tomato paste, vinegar, Worcestershire, honey, stock.
steps.detail: Lift pork out. In a jug: 3 tbsp tomato paste, 3 tbsp vinegar, 2 tbsp Worcestershire, 1/3 cup honey, 250ml chicken stock. Whisk till smooth. This is the braising liquid and the early sauce.

steps.title: Assemble the pot
steps.content: Pour into pot, cover tight, bake 4 hr at 150°C.
steps.detail: Pour the liquid into the pot with any leftover rub. Scrape up the fond from the pork searing — that's pure flavour. Pork back in. Liquid should come about a third up the meat; not drowning. Lid on tight.
tips: Too much liquid? Ladle some out and save it — you can add it back when reducing the sauce.

steps.title: Braise low and slow
steps.content: Braise 4 hr at 150C, low and slow.
steps.detail: 150°C oven (LOW and slow is the whole philosophy). 4 HOURS. Don't open the oven for the first 2 hours. After 3, check — meat should be falling-apart tender, a fork twists easily. If it's still resisting, another 30 min. Collagen needs time to transform to gelatin.
tips: Shoulders vary — a bone-in or extra-thick piece can need another 30–60 minutes.

steps.title: Shred and glaze
steps.content: Shred pork with forks, toss in reduced juices.
steps.detail: Lift pork onto a big board. Use two forks to shred — it should pull apart like old rope. Discard any huge lumps of fat. Skim the fat from the braising liquid, reduce on the stovetop for 5 min till glossy. Toss the shredded pork back through this reduced sauce.
tips: Shred while warm — cold pork stiffens and fights the forks.

steps.title: Slaw and stack
steps.content: Make slaw, stack pork in rolls.
steps.detail: Shred 1/4 cabbage and 2 grated carrots in a bowl. Dress with 1/4 cup mayo, 2 tbsp vinegar, pinch salt. Toss. Rest 5 min — the cabbage softens slightly. Split the rolls, pile a generous handful of pork on the bottom half, pile slaw on top (the slaw's job is crunch against the rich pork), top half on. Eat with both hands over a plate. Paper towels.

title: Rosemary Focaccia
description: Dimpled, salty, olive-oil-drenched. The easy yeast bread.
equipment: cling film or damp tea towel
storage: Best warm on baking day. Keeps 2 days airtight at room temperature — refresh in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes — or freeze slices for 2 months.
make_ahead: The first rise can happen slowly overnight in the fridge — bring the dough back to room temperature before dimpling. Baked focaccia also freezes well in slices.
leftovers: Split it through the middle for sandwiches, or cube and toast into croutons for soup.

substitutions.swap: thyme, oregano, sliced olives or halved cherry tomatoes
bread flour gives a chewier, more open crumb if you have it

steps.title: Bloom the yeast
steps.content: Bloom yeast in 400 ml warm water with sugar, 10 min.
steps.detail: Small bowl: 400ml warm water (warm-to-touch, around 38°C — too hot kills the yeast). Sprinkle 2 tsp yeast and 1 tsp sugar over the top. DON'T stir. Rest 10 min — should foam up to a frothy brown layer. That foam confirms your yeast is alive. If it doesn't foam, yeast is dead; start again.
tips: Check the yeast's use-by date first — stale yeast is the usual reason nothing foams.

steps.title: Mix the sticky dough
steps.content: Mix with flour, 1/3 cup oil, salt, no knead.
steps.detail: Big bowl: 4 cups flour, 2 tsp salt (keep salt away from the yeast or it kills it). Pour in the yeast water and 1/3 cup olive oil. Mix with a wooden spoon till no dry flour remains — this is a wet, shaggy, sticky dough. NO KNEADING REQUIRED. The wet-no-knead method is the revelation of easy focaccia.
tips: It will look too wet to be bread — that's right. Resist the urge to add more flour.

steps.title: Let it double
steps.content: Cover, rise 2 hr.
steps.detail: Cover with cling film or a damp tea towel. Leave at room temp 2 hours till DOUBLED in size and full of bubbles. Cold kitchen? On top of the fridge or in a sunny spot. The longer rise gives better flavour.

steps.title: Stretch and dimple
steps.content: Spread in oiled tray, dimple with fingers.
steps.detail: Generously oil a baking tray (focaccia needs FAT to get its crisp bottom — don't skimp). Tip dough into the tray, pour another 2 tbsp oil over the top. With OILY fingertips, press dimples deep into the dough, stretching it to fill the tray. Dimples hold pools of oil = iconic focaccia texture.
tips: Press your fingertips right down to the tray — shallow dents puff out flat in the oven.

steps.title: Dress the top
steps.content: Top with oil, rosemary, sliced garlic, salt.
steps.detail: Drizzle MORE olive oil across the top (this should feel like too much oil — it's not). Scatter rosemary sprigs pressed lightly into the dimples, 2 thinly sliced garlic cloves, generous flaky sea salt. Crunchy salt is the defining flavour.

steps.title: Prove again, bake
steps.content: Rise 30 min, bake 22 min at 220°C.
steps.detail: Rest the topped focaccia 30 min — second rise. Meanwhile preheat oven to 220°C. 22 min bake on the middle rack till deeply gold on top and crisp-bottomed. Tap the bottom; should sound hollow. Drizzle more oil when it comes out. Eat warm — cold focaccia is fine, but warm is a religious experience.
tips: Ovens brown unevenly — turn the tray at the 15-minute mark if one side is racing ahead.

title: Ham & Cheese Jaffle
description: The sealed-edge, molten-middle sandwich press legend.
storage: Doesn't keep once pressed — the seal softens and the molten middle sets. Press to order and eat hot.
make_ahead: Assemble the sandwiches a few hours ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — press straight from cold.

substitutions.swap: shredded leftover chook, or baked beans for the classic bean jaffle
leave it out, or swap for caramelised onion or sliced pickles
any melting cheese — tasty, colby or mozzarella

steps.title: Butter the outsides
steps.content: Butter bread outsides.
steps.detail: Softened butter on the OUTSIDE of each of 4 slices of white bread — thick, no patches missed. This goes against the jaffle iron grill and creates the crispy seal. Don't use pre-melted butter; too runny.

steps.title: Layer the middle
steps.content: Layer ham, cheese, tomato between.
steps.detail: Flip 2 slices butter-side-DOWN. Layer each with 2 slices of ham, a pile of grated cheddar, and 2 thin slices of tomato (pat the tomato dry with paper towel first — wet tomato makes soggy jaffle). Top with the other bread slice, butter-side UP.

steps.title: Press until sealed
steps.content: Press in a jaffle iron 5 min till outside crisp and corners sealed.
steps.detail: Hot jaffle iron (or sandwich press). Sandwich in, close and latch. 5 min till the outside is deep gold and sealed-crisp around the edges (the corners should feel solid — that's the jaffle seal). Don't rush it; inside cheese needs time to go molten.
tips: No latch on your press? Rest something heavy and heatproof on the lid for the same squeeze.

steps.title: Wait out the lava
steps.content: Rest a minute before biting — lava cheese incident otherwise.
steps.detail: Open carefully — steam escapes and can burn. Slide jaffle onto a plate. REST 60 sec — the inside is molten lava and will scald a tongue. Cut diagonally if you want. Eat with baked beans on the side if you're doing it right. Wipe the jaffle iron while still warm; once cheese sets it's concrete.

title: Buttermilk Coleslaw
description: Crunchy, creamy, tangy. BBQ essential, pulled-pork friend.
storage: Keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge — it softens but stays good. Don't freeze; the veg collapses to mush.
make_ahead: It improves with a few hours in the fridge. For maximum crunch, keep the shredded veg and dressing separate and toss up to a day later.
leftovers: Pile it into burgers, fish tacos or sandwiches — anywhere that wants crunch and tang.

substitutions.swap: buttermilk for the namesake version — use a little less, it's thinner
white wine vinegar or lemon juice
2 spring onions, sliced fine

steps.title: Shred all the veg
steps.content: Shred cabbage, grate carrot, thin-slice onion.
steps.detail: Shred half a cabbage on the finest setting of a box grater, sharp knife, or food processor. Fine shreds absorb dressing better than chunks. Coarse-grate 2 carrots. Very thinly slice 1/4 red onion (soak in cold water 5 min if raw onion is too punchy, drain, pat dry). Biggest bowl you have.
tips: Cut the white core wedge out of the cabbage before shredding — it's tough and bitter.

steps.title: Whisk the dressing
steps.content: Whisk mayo, yoghurt, vinegar, dijon, sugar.
steps.detail: Small jug: 1/2 cup mayo, 1/2 cup yoghurt (half-mayo-half-yoghurt is lighter than all-mayo coleslaw), 2 tbsp apple cider vinegar, 1 tsp dijon, 1 tsp sugar, big pinch of salt, pepper. Whisk smooth. Taste on a cabbage shred — tangy, creamy, a touch sweet.

steps.title: Toss and rest
steps.content: Toss through veg, rest 15 min.
steps.detail: Pour dressing over the veg. Toss with hands or big tongs till every shred is coated. Rest at LEAST 15 min before eating — the salt in the dressing draws water from the veg and softens it from crunchy-raw to satisfyingly-crunchy-wilted. Rests up to 2 days in the fridge and gets better.
