steps.title: Finish with parsley
steps.content: Finish with parsley and a crack of pepper.
steps.detail: Just before serving, scatter chopped parsley and a generous crack of pepper. Serve alongside BBQ, pulled pork, fish and chips, burgers. Acid partner to anything rich and fatty.

title: Silky Mashed Potato
description: Buttery, smooth, the platform every stew deserves.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. It freezes up to 2 months but comes back a little grainy — reheat gently with a splash of milk, stirring often.
make_ahead: Mash holds happily for a few hours — smooth the surface, dot with butter, cover, and reheat gently with an extra splash of warm milk.
leftovers: Cold mash makes brilliant potato cakes — mix in an egg and pan-fry, or use it to top a quick cottage pie.

substitutions.swap: cream, or half milk half cream, for a richer mash
good olive oil for a dairy-free mash — different but lovely

steps.title: Prep the potatoes
steps.content: Peel and chunk potatoes, cover in cold salted water.
steps.detail: Peel 1.2kg potatoes (floury varieties like Maris Piper, Sebago, or a good all-rounder). Cut into even 4cm chunks — same size means same cooking time. Into a pot, cover with cold salted water by 5cm. STARTING cold means the centre cooks at the same rate as the outside; starting in boiling water gives you mushy outsides and raw centres.
tips: Give the peeled chunks a quick rinse before they go in the pot — washing off surface starch keeps the mash from turning gluey.

steps.title: Simmer till fork-tender
steps.content: Bring to boil, simmer 18 min till fork-tender.
steps.detail: Bring to a boil, drop to a gentle simmer. 18 min till a knife slides through with ZERO resistance — you should be able to break a chunk with a spoon. Underdone potatoes mash lumpy. Test several pieces.
tips: Keep it at a gentle burble, not a rolling boil — hard boiling bashes the outsides apart before the centres are done.

steps.title: Steam-dry the potatoes
steps.content: Drain, dry in the pot 2 min.
steps.detail: Drain in a colander. Tip back INTO THE HOT POT (off the heat). Rest 2 min — steam rises off and takes excess moisture with it. Dry potato mashes fluffier. Counterintuitive but essential.
tips: Give the pot a gentle shake — roughed-up edges release more steam.

steps.title: Mash with warm dairy
steps.content: Mash with warm butter and milk.
steps.detail: Warm 200ml milk and 120g butter together in a small pan till butter is melted (cold dairy chills the mash and hardens it). Pour over the potatoes. Use a potato masher or, for extra silky, a ricer. Avoid electric beaters — over-work the starch and you get wallpaper paste.
tips: Add the warm milk mixture in two lots — much easier to control the final texture than pouring it all at once.

steps.title: Season and serve
steps.content: Season well, beat to silky.
steps.detail: Season GENEROUSLY — mash is bland without enough salt. Crack of pepper. Beat briefly with a wooden spoon till silky and smooth. Should be loose enough to slump when spooned. Extra butter on top before serving is traditional and correct. Pile under stews, roasts, sausages.
tips: White pepper instead of black keeps the mash speckle-free, if looks matter tonight.

title: Pork Chops with Apple & Sage
description: Pan-seared chops, caramelised apples, crispy sage. A 30-minute Sunday feel.
storage: Keeps 2–3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat gently, covered, with a splash of water so the pork doesn't dry out. The apples don't freeze well.
leftovers: Slice leftover pork cold into a crusty roll with the apples and a smear of extra dijon.

substitutions.swap: firm pears — treat them exactly the same
rosemary or thyme sprigs, added with the butter and pulled out before serving
maple syrup or a spoon of brown sugar

steps.title: Dry and season the chops
steps.content: Pat chops dry, season generously.
steps.detail: Take chops out of the fridge 15 min before cooking. Pat completely dry with paper towel — surface moisture steams instead of sears. Season both sides heavily with sea salt flakes and cracked pepper.
tips: Snip through the fat rim in two or three spots so the chops stay flat instead of cupping in the pan.
warnings: Wash hands and anything the raw pork touched before moving on — same hygiene rules as chicken.

steps.title: Wedge the apples
steps.content: Core and wedge apples.
steps.detail: Quarter the apples, cut out cores, then slice each quarter into 3–4 wedges. Thick pieces hold shape and caramelise. Granny Smith or Pink Lady work best.
tips: Leave the skin on — it holds the wedges together and colours beautifully in the butter.

steps.title: Sear the chops
steps.content: Sear chops 3 min a side.
steps.detail: Heat olive oil in a heavy pan over medium-high until shimmering. Lay chops in and don't touch them for 3 full minutes — leave them alone to form a crust. Flip once. Total 6–7 min for 2cm thick. Rest on a plate tented with foil. They're done when the centre reads about 63°C or the juices run just clear — a faint blush of pink is fine once rested; grey all through means overdone.
tips: The chops will lift freely when the crust has set — if they're sticking, they're not ready to flip.
warnings: Hot oil can spit as the chops go in — lay them down away from you.

steps.title: Caramelise the apples
steps.content: Butter, apple, sage, garlic — 5 min.
steps.detail: Same pan (the browned bits are flavour). Melt butter. When it foams, add apple wedges, whole sage leaves, and smashed garlic. Spoon foaming butter over the apples for 5 min till deep gold on both sides. The sage leaves crisp — save for garnish.
tips: The butter should foam gently and smell nutty — an acrid smell means the pan's too hot, so lift it off for a moment.

steps.title: Build the pan sauce
steps.content: Deglaze with dijon and honey, return chops.
steps.detail: Stir dijon and honey into the apple-butter with a splash of water. Nestle chops back in with any resting juices, warm through 1 min, spooning sauce over.
tips: If the sauce tightens too much, another splash of water loosens it straight back.

steps.title: Assemble the plates
steps.content: Plate with crispy sage on top.
steps.detail: Pork, apples piled alongside, sauce spooned over, crispy sage leaves perched on top. Mashed potato or buttered greens catch the pan sauce.

title: Smoked Salmon Bagel
description: Cream cheese, ribbons of salmon, capers, red onion. Brunch royalty.
storage: Assembled bagels don't keep — build them to order. Opened smoked salmon keeps 1–2 days airtight in the fridge; don't refreeze it.
leftovers: Stir spare salmon and capers through scrambled eggs or a creamy pasta.

substitutions.swap: labneh or thick crème fraîche
chives snipped fine work just as well as dill or parsley
finely sliced spring onion for a gentler bite

steps.title: Toast the bagels
steps.content: Split and toast bagels.
steps.detail: Slice in half horizontally. Toast cut-side-up till gold at the edges — you want structure to hold toppings without going so hard they crack. Warm, not crunchy.
tips: Day-old bagels toast up better than fresh — this is the recipe for them.

steps.title: Shave the onion
steps.content: Thinly slice red onion.
steps.detail: Paper-thin slices are the goal. If raw onion is too punchy, drop slices into iced water for 5 min, drain, pat dry — mellows them while keeping the crunch.
tips: Slice into rings rather than half-moons — they sit prettier on a round bagel.

steps.title: Layer the cream cheese
steps.content: Schmear cream cheese thick.
steps.detail: At least 1 cm layer on each bagel half, right to the edges. If the cheese is fridge-cold and firm, soften at room temp 10 min or beat with a fork first.

steps.title: Drape the salmon
steps.content: Lay salmon in loose folds.
steps.detail: Drape in gentle folds, not flat sheets — every bite wants pockets of fish. Three or four pieces per half.
tips: Tear larger slices in half — smaller folds are easier to bite through cleanly.

steps.title: Finish and eat
steps.content: Scatter capers, onion, herbs, lemon.
steps.detail: Drained capers, a few onion slices, torn dill or parsley. Squeeze lemon, crack pepper. Don't salt — capers and salmon carry it. Eat immediately while the bagel's warm.

title: Slow-Cooked Beef Brisket
description: Falls apart under a fork. Low oven, patience, and a crown of herbs.
equipment: large heavy oven-safe pot with lid
storage: Keeps 3–4 days airtight in the fridge, stored in its sauce. Freezes up to 3 months; thaw overnight in the fridge and reheat covered until piping hot.
make_ahead: Brisket is better the next day — cook it fully, cool it in its liquid and refrigerate; the fat lifts off in one set layer and the flavour deepens.
leftovers: Shred leftovers into soft rolls with slaw, or fold through tacos with pickled onion.

substitutions.swap: soy sauce with a small splash of vinegar
chicken stock, or water with 2 crumbled beef stock cubes
1 tsp dried thyme stirred straight into the liquid

steps.title: Sear the brisket
steps.content: Season heavily, sear all sides.
steps.detail: Pat brisket dry and really massage in salt, pepper, paprika. Heat a heavy oven-safe pot over high with a splash of oil until just smoking. Lay brisket in and don't touch for 3–4 min per side until each face is deep mahogany. This is where your gravy's flavour comes from. Remove to a plate.
tips: If the brisket won't sit flat, cut it in two and sear the halves separately — a proper crust beats a wedged fit.
warnings: Raw beef juices: wash hands, board and tongs before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Soften the vegetables
steps.content: Soften onions, carrots, garlic.
steps.detail: Lower heat to medium. Add roughly chopped onion and carrot. Scrape up the browned bits (fond = flavour) as you stir. 8 min till soft; add smashed garlic last minute.
tips: A splash of water lifts stubborn browned bits if scraping alone won't shift them.

steps.title: Cook out the paste
steps.content: Stir in tomato paste till brick-red.
steps.detail: Make a well, squeeze paste into the clear spot, cook 60 sec so it darkens and loses its tinny edge, then stir through. This one step lifts the whole dish.

steps.title: Build the braise
steps.content: Add stock, Worcestershire, herbs; return brisket.
steps.detail: Pour in stock and Worcestershire. Tie herbs with string (easier to fish out) and drop in. Nestle brisket back — liquid should come halfway up, not cover. Bring to a gentle simmer.
tips: Taste the liquid now — it should sit a touch under-salted, because it concentrates over the hours in the oven.

steps.title: Braise low and slow
steps.content: Cover, braise 3–4 hr at 150°C.
steps.detail: Lid on tight, into a 150°C oven. Don't open for the first 2 hours. After 3, check: a fork should slide in with no resistance. Brisket is done when it tells you, not the clock. Up to 4 hr on a thick cut.
tips: If the liquid is boiling hard when you check at 3 hours, your oven runs hot — drop it 10 degrees.

steps.title: Rest, skim and slice
steps.content: Rest 30 min, slice against the grain.
steps.detail: Rest on a board tented with foil 30 min — critical. Meanwhile skim fat off the liquid, reduce on the stove till glossy. Slice across the grain (perpendicular to the fibres) into 1cm slabs. Reduced sauce spooned over.
tips: Tilt the pot slightly and the fat pools at one end — much easier to spoon off.

title: Aloo Gobi
description: Potato and cauliflower roasted dry with turmeric, cumin, coriander. Nan-approved.
equipment: wide frying pan or sauté pan with lid
fine grater for garlic and ginger
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat covered until steaming. It freezes, but the potato comes back softer — better kept to the fridge.
make_ahead: Happily made a day ahead — the spices settle in overnight. Reheat covered with a splash of water, then finish with fresh lemon and coriander.
leftovers: Fold leftovers into a wrap with a spoon of yoghurt, or crush them into a toastie.

substitutions.swap: half a tin of tinned tomatoes, drained a little
garam masala for a warmer, sweeter profile
lime

steps.title: Cut potato and cauli
steps.content: Cube potato, break cauli into florets.
steps.detail: Cut potatoes into 2cm cubes — they'll cook at the same speed as the cauli florets. Break cauli into bite-sized pieces, roughly the same size as the potato for even cooking.
tips: Cut florets through the stem with a knife rather than snapping them off — fewer crumbs to catch and burn in the pan.

steps.title: Soften the aromatics
steps.content: Fry onion, garlic, ginger till soft.
steps.detail: Finely chop onion, grate garlic and ginger. Heat oil in a wide pan — room for everything to spread. Sauté medium 6 min till translucent and gold at the edges.

steps.title: Bloom the spices
steps.content: Add spices, toast 30 sec.
steps.detail: Stir in turmeric, cumin, curry powder and keep moving for 30 seconds. Spices wake up and release oils — but burn fast. Have the tomatoes ready.
tips: Turmeric stains — use a dark spoon and keep it off tea towels and plastic containers.

steps.title: Break down the tomato
steps.content: Add tomato, cook 3 min to a pulp.
steps.detail: Finely diced tomato in, stir and cook 3 min till it breaks down into a pulpy spice-base. That's the sauce coating everything.

steps.title: Coat the vegetables
steps.content: Toss potato and cauli to coat.
steps.detail: Tip in veg, stir till every piece is coated. Add a splash of water (1/4 cup), season, cover.

steps.title: Steam under the lid
steps.content: Cover 20 min, stir occasionally.
steps.detail: Medium-low, lid on. Veg steams in its own moisture. Stir every 5 min, splash of water if dry. After 20, potato should be tender — pierce to check.
tips: Fold gently with a lifting motion when you stir — cauliflower breaks up if it's bashed about.

steps.title: Crisp and brighten
steps.content: Uncover, crisp 5 min; lemon, coriander.
steps.detail: Lid off, heat up. Let moisture cook off for 5 min — some edges will brown, that's caramelisation. Off heat, squeeze lemon, scatter torn coriander. Eat with rice or flatbread.
tips: Listen for the sizzle to change from wet to dry — that's the moisture gone and the browning starting.

title: Kung Pao Chicken
description: Silky chicken, tingling chillies, crunchy peanuts, sticky glaze. Sichuan at home.
equipment: wok or large heavy frying pan
small jug or bowl for the sauce
storage: Keeps 2–3 days airtight in the fridge; reheat until piping hot. Cool leftover rice quickly and use it within a day — the chicken freezes up to 2 months, the rice is best not frozen.
make_ahead: Cube the chicken and whisk the sauce up to a day ahead, both covered in the fridge — then the wok work is only minutes.
leftovers: Pile cold leftovers into lettuce cups with extra peanuts for a fast lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — cook it a touch less and don't walk away, it dries out faster
cashew or almond butter
roasted cashews
sambal oelek or any garlic-chilli sauce

steps.title: Cube and marinate chicken
steps.content: Cube chicken, marinate 10 min.
steps.detail: Cut into 2cm cubes. Toss with 1 tbsp of the soy and 1 tsp cornflour if you have it — creates a velvet coating that keeps meat juicy in high heat. Rest while you prep.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash hands, knife and board in hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Mix the kung pao sauce
steps.content: Whisk the sauce.
steps.detail: Remaining soy, rice vinegar, honey, sriracha, peanut butter. Whisk smooth, loosen with a splash of water if stiff. Sauce pours in quickly later, so ready-to-go matters.
tips: Taste it — it should lean salty-sharp; the sweetness rounds out once it hits the hot wok.

steps.title: Start the rice
steps.content: Cook rice.
steps.detail: Rinse till water runs clear. 1 part rice to 1.5 parts water, lid on, 12 min low. Off heat 5 min before fluffing. Start this first — runs in parallel.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Blast chicken in very hot wok, 3 min.
steps.detail: Wok smoking-hot. 1 tbsp oil, swirl, chicken in a single layer. Don't stir for 90 sec — let it sear. Then toss till just cooked, 3 min total. Plate. Cut the biggest piece in half to check — opaque all the way through with no pink and you're right.
warnings: A smoking wok spits when chicken hits — hold pieces low and lay them in away from you.

steps.title: Fry aromatics and capsicum
steps.content: Aromatics 30 sec, then veg.
steps.detail: Lower heat. More oil if needed. Dried chillies (snap for heat, whole for flavour), garlic, ginger, white parts of spring onion. 30 sec fragrant. Diced capsicum, 2 min.
warnings: Frying dried chillies throws a peppery fume that catches the throat — run the rangehood or crack a window.

steps.title: Glaze and finish
steps.content: Return chicken, sauce, toss till glossy.
steps.detail: Chicken back with resting juices. Pour sauce, toss 1 min till thick and sticky. Off heat: drizzle sesame oil, scatter green parts of spring onion and the roasted peanuts.
tips: If the sauce tightens past sticky into gluggy, a tablespoon of water brings it straight back.

steps.title: Serve over rice
steps.content: Over rice.
steps.detail: Pile over steaming rice. Cucumber slices on the side cool it down if the dried chilli bit back.

title: Mongolian Beef
description: Crisp strips of beef in a sweet-soy glaze, spring onion everywhere.
equipment: wok or deep heavy frying pan
fine grater for garlic and ginger
heatproof jar or bowl for the spent oil
storage: Keeps 3 days airtight in the fridge, though the crisp coating softens — reheat in a hot pan rather than the microwave. Cool leftover rice quickly and use it within a day.
leftovers: Toss leftovers through fresh egg noodles with a splash of water for a five-minute lunch.

substitutions.swap: chicken thigh strips — same coating, but fry until cooked through with no pink
white sugar, or honey for a stickier glaze
rice flour crisps up much the same

steps.title: Slice and dust beef
steps.content: Slice beef thin, dust in cornflour.
steps.detail: Freeze 15 min first — easier to slice thin. Cut across the grain into 5mm strips, 7cm long. Toss in cornflour till every strip is coated — that's the restaurant-crisp exterior.
tips: Get the rice on now — rinsed, 1 part rice to 1.5 water, 12 min on low — so it's ready when the beef is.
Shake excess cornflour off in a sieve — loose powder turns the frying oil gluey.
warnings: Raw beef: wash hands, board and knife in hot soapy water before they touch anything else.

steps.title: Fry beef in batches
steps.content: Shallow-fry 2 min in hot oil.
steps.detail: 1 cm oil, hot till a test piece sizzles. Fry beef in batches — crowding drops heat and steams. 90 sec per batch till crisp-edged. Drain on paper.
tips: The oil temperature drops with each batch — let it come back to a lively sizzle before the next goes in.
warnings: Hot oil spits — lower the strips in away from you, and never add water to hot oil.

steps.title: Pour off the oil
steps.content: Drain most oil, keep 1 tbsp.
steps.detail: Carefully pour out oil (save for other stir-fries). Wipe, keep 1 tbsp. Same pan has the fond you need.
warnings: Pour hot oil slowly into a metal or heatproof jar — never plastic, and never down the sink.

steps.title: Bloom garlic and ginger
steps.content: Fry garlic and ginger, 30 sec.
steps.detail: Grated garlic and ginger over medium-high. Stir constantly for 30 seconds — you'll smell when ready.
warnings: Garlic scorches fast in this hot pan — have the soy and sugar measured and ready to pour straight in.

steps.title: Reduce the glaze
steps.content: Add soy, sugar, splash water; reduce 2 min.
steps.detail: Pour in soy, sugar, and 1/3 cup water. Boil hard 2 min till glossy glaze that coats the back of a spoon.
tips: Drag a spoon across the pan — the glaze is ready when the trail holds for a second before closing over.

steps.title: Glaze the beef
steps.content: Return beef and spring onion, toss 60 sec.
steps.detail: Beef back, toss just 1 minute till glossy and coated. No longer or you'll lose the crunch. Spring onion (3cm lengths) at the very end.

steps.title: Serve it crisp
steps.content: Over rice, sesame drizzle.
steps.detail: Jasmine rice, beef over. Drizzle sesame oil, scatter reserved spring onion tops. Immediately while crisp.

title: Cacio e Pepe
description: Three ingredients, one silky sauce. Technique is everything.
storage: Best eaten on the spot — the emulsion doesn't survive well. Fridge 1–2 days airtight if you must, reheated gently in a pan with a splash of water; don't freeze.

substitutions.swap: all pecorino romano is the classic; half pecorino, half parmesan is gentler on salt
tonnarelli, linguine or bucatini

steps.title: Cook pasta, save water
steps.content: Cook pasta lightly salted.
steps.detail: LESS salt than usual — pecorino/parmesan is salty and the pasta water becomes the sauce. Cook 1 min shy of al dente. Reserve 2 CUPS of pasta water before draining — more than you think.
tips: Use less water in the pot than usual — starchier pasta water makes a stronger sauce.

steps.title: Toast the pepper
steps.content: Toast pepper in dry pan, 1 min.
steps.detail: Crack pepper coarsely (not fine). Dry pan, no oil. Toast 60 sec till fragrant. Off heat.
tips: No coarse grinder? Crush the peppercorns under a heavy pan — those craggy bits are the point.

steps.title: Grate the cheese
steps.content: Grate cheese very fine.
steps.detail: Microplane or smallest holes. Fine = smoother melt. Set aside.
tips: Let it sit at room temperature while the pasta cooks — fridge-cold cheese seizes into clumps.

steps.title: Make the pepper broth
steps.content: Splash pasta water into pepper pan.
steps.detail: Low heat. 1/3 cup starchy pasta water into the peppered pan. Swirl to a peppery broth.

steps.title: Marry pasta and broth
steps.content: Pasta in, toss hard.
steps.detail: Tongs pasta in, dripping with water (good). More splash of water. Pasta releases starch, water thickens.

steps.title: Emulsify the cheese
steps.content: Off heat. Cheese in thirds, toss silky.
steps.detail: CRITICAL: OFF THE HEAT. Third of cheese, toss hard 20 sec to emulsify. Next third. Last. Splashes of water as you go. Sauce clings like silk. Clumps = too hot; rescue with more water and vigorous tossing.
tips: Test the pan first: a drop of water should sizzle softly, not crackle — too hot and the cheese will clump.

steps.title: Serve straight away
steps.content: Immediately.
steps.detail: This dish waits for no one. Plate as soon as emulsified, more cracked pepper, dusting of cheese. Eat the second it lands.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
description: Garlic, chilli, olive oil. Fifteen minutes, no excuses.
storage: Best straight from the pan; keeps 2 days airtight in the fridge, reheated in a frying pan with a splash of water to bring the oil back together. Don't freeze.
leftovers: Cold leftovers fry into a crisp pasta frittata — press into an oiled pan with a beaten egg.

substitutions.swap: basil, or a handful of rocket wilted in at the end
1/2 tsp chilli flakes
linguine or any long pasta

steps.title: Shave the garlic
steps.content: Slice garlic paper-thin.
steps.detail: The star. Sharp knife or mandoline, thin as a coin. Uneven chunks burn unevenly. Don't crush — gentle heat infuses, crushing = sharp allium.
tips: Drop the slices into a little of the measured oil as you go — it keeps them from drying out and colouring unevenly later.

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti just under al dente.
steps.detail: Salt the water well (only seasoning the pasta gets). Cook 1 min less than packet. Reserve a cup of pasta water.
tips: Set a timer for 1 min under packet time the moment the pasta hits the water — this dish moves fast at the end.

steps.title: Infuse the garlic
steps.content: Warm oil and garlic from cold.
steps.detail: Garlic AND oil in a cold pan, then turn heat to medium-low. Cold start lets garlic infuse without burning. Pale gold and fragrant, NEVER dark — dark = bitter. 3 min.
tips: Tilt the pan now and then so the oil pools and the slices swim — they colour more evenly.
warnings: Garlic keeps cooking in the hot oil even off the heat — pull it at pale gold, not golden brown.

steps.title: Bloom the chilli
steps.content: Chilli, off heat 30 sec.
steps.detail: Crumble in dried chilli or a good pinch of flakes. Off heat 30 sec for infusion.

steps.title: Toss to emulsify
steps.content: Toss pasta with oil and water.
steps.detail: Low heat. Tongs pasta into pan with 1/4 cup pasta water. Toss hard 60 sec — starch emulsifies with oil into a loose sauce.
tips: The sauce is right when it looks slightly milky and clings to the strands — glossy pools of plain oil mean keep tossing.

steps.title: Finish with parsley
steps.content: Parsley, pepper, done.
steps.detail: Off heat, torn parsley, plenty of pepper. Traditionally no cheese — the garlic and oil are the whole point. Right away.

title: Spaghetti Puttanesca
description: Salty, briny, punchy. Pantry heroes doing their best work.
equipment: large frying pan or sauté pan
storage: The sauce keeps 4 days airtight in the fridge or freezes for 3 months; tossed pasta is best within 2 days. Reheat in a pan with a splash of water.
make_ahead: The sauce keeps well — make it up to 3 days ahead and reheat in the pan while the pasta cooks.
leftovers: Leftover sauce is brilliant spooned over baked white fish or a pan-fried chicken breast.

substitutions.swap: kalamatas are ideal, but any pitted olive works
400 g ripe fresh tomatoes, chopped, in high summer
a big pinch of chilli flakes

steps.title: Boil the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook pasta just under al dente.
steps.detail: Salt well. 1 min less than packet. Reserve a cup of pasta water.

steps.title: Infuse garlic and chilli
steps.content: Warm oil with garlic and chilli, 2 min.
steps.detail: Thin-sliced garlic, sliced chilli, oil over medium-low till fragrant and pale gold. Don't brown — puttanesca is big flavour, no bitterness on top.

steps.title: Fry olives and capers
steps.content: Olives and capers, 1 min.
steps.detail: Roughly chopped olives, drained capers. Stir through the oil a minute — they release brine into the base. Whole point.
tips: Pat the capers dry first — wet capers spit in hot oil.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Tomatoes, simmer 8 min.
steps.detail: Add tomatoes (crush whole ones with your hand). Simmer 8 min till thick, raw edge gone. Taste — intensely savoury. No more salt needed.
tips: It's thick enough when a spoon dragged through leaves a trail that holds for a second.

steps.title: Toss pasta through
steps.content: Pasta in with splash of water.
steps.detail: Drained pasta in with a splash of reserved water. Toss 30 sec to emulsify.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.content: Parsley, pepper.
steps.detail: Off heat, torn parsley and plenty of cracked pepper. Traditionally no cheese — olives and capers are the salty work.

title: Salade Niçoise
description: Côte d'Azur on a plate. Tuna, eggs, green beans, olives, new potato.
equipment: jar with a lid for the dressing
storage: Once dressed it doesn't keep — the leaves and beans go limp. Undressed components keep 2 days airtight in the fridge; opened tinned tuna 1–2 days in a covered container, not the tin.
make_ahead: Eggs, potatoes, beans and dressing can all be done the morning ahead and kept chilled — compose the platter just before serving.
leftovers: Undressed leftovers pack straight into tomorrow's lunchbox with the dressing in a small jar.

substitutions.swap: seared fresh tuna, sliced thick, if you're showing off
white wine vinegar or lemon juice
asparagus in spring, blanched 2 minutes
any soft or crisp leaf — oakleaf, baby gem, iceberg at a pinch

steps.title: Boil the eggs
steps.content: Boil eggs 8 min, iced water.
steps.detail: Lower eggs gently into simmering water. Timer for EXACTLY 8 min — set yolk, still a touch custardy. Straight into iced water to stop the cook. Peel.
tips: Older eggs peel more easily than very fresh ones — start peeling from the fat end, where the air pocket sits.

steps.title: Simmer the potatoes
steps.content: Boil potatoes tender.
steps.detail: Halve baby potatoes. Start in COLD salted water (start in boiling and outsides cook before centres). Bring to boil, simmer 12 min till a knife slides in easily. Drain, cool.
tips: Cool them spread out on a tray — piled hot in a bowl they steam themselves soggy.

steps.title: Blanch and shock beans
steps.content: Blanch green beans 3 min, shock.
steps.detail: Same pan. Beans in for exactly 3 min — snap, not softness. Straight into iced water to stay vivid green.

steps.title: Shake the dressing
steps.content: Whisk dressing.
steps.detail: Jar: dijon, red wine vinegar, good olive oil, pinch of salt. Shake till creamy-emulsified, not separated. Taste on a potato; adjust salt.
tips: A drop of water in the jar helps the dressing hold together longer once shaken.

steps.title: Compose the platter
steps.content: Arrange, don't toss.
steps.detail: Lettuce as base. Arrange potatoes, beans, halved tomatoes, olives, red onion, quartered eggs in ZONES — the French compose so each ingredient stands. Flake tuna on top in big chunks.
tips: Dry everything well before it goes on — water pooling under the leaves thins the dressing.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.content: Dress and finish.
steps.detail: Spoon half the dressing over, leave the rest for the table. Scatter capers. Crusty bread alongside. Chilled rosé feels right.

title: Cobb Salad
description: Big, layered, protein-forward. Lunch that actually fills you up.
equipment: jar with a lid for the dressing
storage: Once dressed it won't keep. Undressed components keep 2 days airtight in the fridge (cooked chicken up to 3); an assembled salad with avocado is best eaten the day it's made.
make_ahead: Chicken, bacon, eggs and dressing all keep a day ahead in the fridge — cut the avocado last, it browns fast.
leftovers: Leftover chicken, bacon and egg are a ready-made sandwich or wrap filling.

substitutions.swap: feta or shaved parmesan if blue isn't your thing
shredded barbecue chook — skip the first step entirely
apple cider or white wine vinegar

steps.title: Pan-cook the chicken
steps.content: Cook chicken, rest, slice.
steps.detail: Season with salt and pepper. Hot pan with oil, 5 min a side to 74°C internal. Rest 5 min (juicy!). Slice across the grain. No thermometer? Cut into the thickest part — opaque all the way through with clear juices means it's done.
tips: Flatten thicker breasts to an even 2 cm with the heel of your hand first — they cook evenly and stay juicy.
warnings: Raw chicken: wash hands, board and knife in hot soapy water before touching anything else.

steps.title: Render the bacon
steps.content: Crisp bacon, tear.
steps.detail: Lay bacon flat in cold pan, medium heat. Render slowly 6 min flipping once. Drain on paper. Tear into big bits — more texture.
tips: Pour the rendered fat into a jar — it's gold for frying eggs later in the week.

steps.title: Boil the eggs
steps.content: Boil eggs 8 min, peel, halve.
steps.detail: Simmer 8 min for just-set yolks. Iced water. Peel under a gentle stream (easier). Quarter lengthways.

steps.title: Shake the dressing
steps.content: Whisk dressing.
steps.detail: Jar: olive oil, vinegar, dijon, honey, salt. Shake till emulsified. Taste on a lettuce leaf.

steps.title: Chop the cos
steps.content: Cos as base.
steps.detail: Chop into bite-sized pieces, spread across a wide shallow platter. Shallow = everything reachable.
tips: Wash and spin the leaves properly dry — dressing slides straight off wet lettuce.

steps.title: Stripe the toppings
steps.content: Stripes — ingredient per row.
steps.detail: Cobb tradition: each ingredient gets its own stripe across the platter. Chicken, bacon, egg, avocado, tomatoes, crumbled cheese. Scatter spring onion.
tips: Slice the avocado last and add it straight to the platter — it browns quickly once cut.

steps.title: Bring to the table
steps.content: Dressing on the side.
steps.detail: Bring dressing to table and let guests add their own — the salad looks best pre-toss. Right away.

title: Tomato Basil Soup
description: Thick, tangy, silky. Grilled cheese mandatory.
storage: Keeps 4 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze for up to 3 months (it freezes best before the cream goes in). Reheat gently — don't let it boil hard once the cream is added.
make_ahead: The soup keeps beautifully — make it up to 2 days ahead, but hold the cream and basil and stir them in as you reheat.
leftovers: Leftover soup doubles as a pasta sauce — simmer it down a little and toss through penne.

substitutions.swap: full-cream milk with a knob of butter, or coconut cream for a dairy-free finish
chicken stock if that's what's in the cupboard
another tin of tomatoes in winter when fresh ones are pale — you'll lose a little roasty depth

steps.title: Roast the tomatoes
steps.content: Roast fresh tomatoes 25 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: Halve, toss with olive oil, salt, pepper. Cut-side-up on a tray. Roast 25 min till edges char and juice pools. Deepens the flavour massively.
tips: Riper is better, but pale supermarket tomatoes still work — roasting concentrates whatever flavour they have.

steps.title: Sweat the onion
steps.content: Sauté onion and garlic.
steps.detail: Butter over medium, chopped onion. Sweat 8 min till soft and translucent — no colour. Garlic final minute.
tips: If the onion starts browning, the heat is too high — you want soft and glassy, not golden.

steps.title: Toast the tomato paste
steps.content: Tomato paste brick-red.
steps.detail: Well in the veg, squeeze paste in. Cook 90 sec in the clear spot till darker and loses tinny taste. Stir through.
tips: You'll smell it turn from tinny to sweet — that's your cue it's done.

steps.title: Simmer everything
steps.content: Tins + roast tomatoes + stock. Simmer 15 min.
steps.detail: Tinned tomatoes (crush with hands), roasted tomatoes with juices, stock, sugar. Simmer 15 min stirring occasionally.
tips: Squeeze the tinned tomatoes under the surface of the pot so they don't squirt juice at you.

steps.title: Blend until silky
steps.content: Blitz silky.
steps.detail: Stick blender in the pot (safest) or blender in batches. Tea towel when blending hot — pressure builds. Blitz 2–3 min for silky texture.
warnings: Keep the stick blender head fully submerged before switching on, or you'll paint the kitchen red.

steps.title: Finish with cream and basil
steps.content: Cream and basil.
steps.detail: Off heat, cream (keeps bright colour) and most of the basil (torn not chopped — bruising releases flavour). Save a few leaves. Taste, adjust salt.
tips: Too sharp? Another pinch of sugar softens the acid without making it sweet.

steps.title: Serve with a toastie
steps.content: With a toastie.
steps.detail: Bowl it, extra cream swirl, basil. Cheese toastie on the side for dipping — non-negotiable.

title: Pork Wonton Soup
description: Feather-light dumplings, clear broth, green onion. Hands-down comfort.
equipment: small bowl of water for sealing
storage: Best eaten fresh — cooked wontons go soft sitting in broth. Store leftover wontons and broth separately for up to 2 days in the fridge (raw wontons freeze for 2 months) and reheat the broth to a full simmer.
make_ahead: Fold the wontons up to a day ahead (covered, on their floured tray in the fridge), or freeze them raw in a single layer — cook straight from frozen, adding a couple of minutes and checking one from the first batch.
leftovers: Pan-fry leftover uncooked wontons in a little oil with a splash of water, lid on — instant potstickers.

substitutions.swap: chicken mince — a little leaner, so add an extra teaspoon of sesame oil
choy sum, or a handful of baby spinach wilted at the end
a spoonful of chilli oil or chilli crisp instead of fresh

steps.title: Make the filling
steps.content: Mix filling.
steps.detail: Bowl: pork, grated ginger and garlic, 1 tbsp soy, half the sesame oil, finely chopped spring onion whites. Mix with chopsticks in one direction until slightly sticky — meat tightens up, keeps fillings juicy.
tips: Fry a teaspoon of filling and taste it — easier to fix the seasoning now than after 40 are folded.
warnings: Wash hands, bowl and board after handling raw pork — keep it away from anything served raw.

steps.title: Fill and fold
steps.content: Fold wontons.
steps.detail: 1 tsp filling centred on each wrapper. Wet two edges with water, fold to a triangle, press out air, bring the two long corners together, pinch. Little bundle. Onto a lightly floured tray so they don't stick.
tips: Keep the wrapper stack under a damp tea towel — they dry out and crack fast.
Your first few will look rough — they cook up fine, and you'll be quick by wonton ten.

steps.title: Simmer the broth
steps.content: Simmer stock with ginger.
steps.detail: Gentle simmer with a few ginger slices. Don't boil hard — graceful bubbles or wontons burst. Remaining soy, taste.
tips: Taste before the wontons go in — the broth should be a touch saltier than you'd sip; the wontons dilute it.

steps.title: Poach the wontons
steps.content: Cook wontons 3 min.
steps.detail: Drop in batches of 10–12. They float when done, about 3 min. Slotted spoon out.
tips: Give them a gentle nudge off the bottom after 30 seconds so they don't stick.
warnings: Cut one open from the first batch — the pork should be grey-brown right through, no pink.

steps.title: Blanch the greens
steps.content: Wilt bok choy, divide.
steps.detail: Bok choy (split lengthways) into broth for 60 sec — vivid green, just-softened. Lift into bowls. 6–8 wontons per.
tips: Wash bok choy well down between the stems — grit hides at the base.

steps.title: Assemble the bowls
steps.content: Broth over, finish.
steps.detail: Ladle piping hot broth. Green parts of spring onion, remaining sesame oil drizzle. Chilli slices if you want heat. Steaming.
tips: Warm the bowls with a splash of hot broth first — the soup stays hot to the last wonton.

title: Vietnamese Summer Rolls
description: Rice paper wrapped around prawns, herbs, noodles. Cool, crisp, addictive.
equipment: wide shallow bowl (bigger than the wrappers)
small bowl and whisk for the sauce
storage: These don't keep — rice paper toughens and the herbs wilt within hours, though at a pinch you can wrap each roll in damp paper towel and cling wrap and eat within 4 hours. The peanut sauce keeps 3 days in the fridge.
make_ahead: Prep all the fillings and whisk the sauce up to half a day ahead, covered in the fridge — but roll just before eating.
leftovers: Leftover fillings become tomorrow's noodle salad — toss with the peanut sauce and a squeeze of lime.

substitutions.swap: shredded poached chicken, or pan-fried strips of firm tofu
any chilli sauce, or a pinch of chilli flakes
extra mint and coriander, or Thai basil if you can get it

steps.title: Soften the noodles
steps.content: Cook noodles, cool in cold water.
steps.detail: Rice vermicelli only needs hot water or quick boil — check packet. Rinse cold till cool. Cut with scissors in the strainer so they're easier to wrap.
tips: Drain them really well — wet noodles make soggy rolls that slide apart.

steps.title: Line up the fillings
steps.content: Prep fillings.
steps.detail: Peel cucumber and carrot, matchsticks. Halve prawns lengthways — pink curve shows through the wrapper. Pile all fillings on a large tray within easy reach.
tips: Pick the herb leaves off their stems now — fumbling with stems mid-roll is how wrappers tear.

steps.title: Soak a wrapper
steps.content: Soak one wrapper 10 sec.
steps.detail: Wide shallow bowl of warm water. Dip one for 10 sec — should still feel a touch firm lifting out. Keeps softening on the board. Too soft tears; too firm cracks.
tips: One wrapper at a time, always — two soaked wrappers glue themselves together instantly.

steps.title: Roll them tight
steps.content: Layer and roll tight.
steps.detail: Lay 2 prawn halves pink-side-DOWN on the wrapper (shows on top of the finished roll). Add noodles, herbs, carrot, cucumber, torn lettuce leaf. Fold bottom, then sides, then roll up tightly like a burrito.
tips: Less filling than you think — an overstuffed roll splits at the seam.
Your first one will be ugly. Eat the evidence and keep going.

steps.title: Mix the dipping sauce
steps.content: Whisk peanut sauce.
steps.detail: Peanut butter, soy, lime, sriracha, warm water splash. Whisk till a ribbon-falls-off-spoon dip. Thin with more water if thick.
tips: Natural peanut butter needs an extra splash of warm water to loosen — keep whisking, it comes together.

steps.title: Serve straight away
steps.content: Eat within 20 min.
steps.detail: Summer rolls are best within 20 min of making. If waiting, cover with damp tea towel — they dry out and crack. Dip and go.
tips: Keep finished rolls from touching on the platter — they weld together.

title: Carrot Cake with Cream Cheese Icing
description: Moist, spiced, piled with cream cheese frosting. Birthday-approved.
storage: The iced cake keeps 5 days in an airtight container in the fridge — let slices sit out 20 minutes so the crumb softens. Un-iced layers freeze, well wrapped, for up to 3 months.
make_ahead: Bake the layers a day ahead and wrap them well once completely cool. The icing can also be made a day ahead — bring it to room temperature and give it a quick beat before spreading.

substitutions.swap: pecans, or leave them out entirely for nut-free
sultanas, or well-drained crushed pineapple for extra moisture
any neutral oil — canola or sunflower

steps.title: Whisk the wet mix
steps.content: Whisk wet: eggs, oil, sugar, vanilla.
steps.detail: Large bowl. Whisk eggs, oil, sugar, vanilla 2 min till pale — aerating gives a tender crumb.
tips: Done when the mix falls off the whisk in slow ribbons rather than drips.

steps.title: Fold in the dry
steps.content: Sift in dry, fold in carrot, nuts, raisins.
steps.detail: Sift flour, cinnamon, baking powder, bicarb, pinch salt over the wet. Fold with spatula — DON'T beat (develops gluten, toughens). Near-combined: fold in grated carrot, walnuts, raisins.
tips: Grate the carrot on the coarse holes — fine grating turns it to wet mush and weighs the cake down.

steps.title: Bake the layers
steps.content: Two 20cm tins, bake 40 min at 170°C.
steps.detail: Grease and line. Divide equally (weigh for evenness). Middle oven 40 min — skewer should come out with a few moist crumbs, not wet batter. Cool 10 min in tins, then on a rack completely.
tips: If one tin is browning faster, swap their positions at the 30-minute mark.
warnings: Don't open the oven in the first 30 minutes — the rise can collapse.

steps.title: Make the icing
steps.content: Beat icing.
steps.detail: Start with SOFT (not melted) butter. Beat smooth. Add room-temp cream cheese a quarter at a time so it doesn't split. 2 cups icing sugar and vanilla, beat 2 min fluffy. If loose, chill 15 min.
tips: Stop beating once it's fluffy — overworked cream cheese icing turns runny and won't come back.

steps.title: Stack and ice
steps.content: Layer and ice.
steps.detail: Trim domed tops flat with a serrated knife for stable stacking. Icing layer, stack second, ice top and sides. Swirl rather than smooth — more inviting. Chopped walnuts on top, classic.
tips: Save the trimmed cake tops — cook's treat, or crumble them over the finished cake.

steps.title: Rest, then slice
steps.content: Chill 30 min before slicing.
steps.detail: Brief chill firms up icing. Hot knife dipped between slices gives neat edges. 5 days in the fridge.

title: Dark Chocolate Mousse
description: Clouds of chocolate, a whisper of cream. Eat with a small spoon.
equipment: heatproof bowl over a small saucepan
storage: Because of the raw egg, keep it no more than 2 days, covered, in the fridge — and don't freeze it. No reheating here; it's served cold.
make_ahead: It needs 4 hours to set anyway, so make it the day before — the texture is even better overnight.

substitutions.swap: 50–60% dark if 70% is too bitter — sweeter, slightly softer set. Avoid milk chocolate; it won't set properly

steps.title: Melt the chocolate
steps.content: Melt chocolate and butter gently.
steps.detail: Chop chocolate small for even melting. Bowl over barely-simmering water (not touching water). Stir occasionally till glossy. Off heat 5 min — hot chocolate hitting cold cream will seize.
warnings: Keep every drop of water and steam out of the chocolate — one splash and it seizes into a grainy lump.

steps.title: Whip the cream
steps.content: Whip cream soft peaks.
steps.detail: Cold cream, cold bowl. Medium till just holding shape when beaters lift — SOFT pillows, not stiff. Over-whipping = butter. Chill.
tips: Ten minutes in the freezer gets the bowl and beaters properly cold.

steps.title: Whisk the yolks
steps.content: Yolks with half the sugar.
steps.detail: Crack eggs one at a time. Separate whites into a spotlessly clean bowl (any fat = whites won't whip). Whisk yolks with half the sugar till pale and ribbony, 2 min.
tips: Separate each egg over a spare cup — one broken yolk won't wreck the whole bowl of whites.

steps.title: Whip the whites
steps.content: Whites to soft peaks + sugar.
steps.detail: Beat slow ramping to high. Foamy: trickle in remaining sugar. Stop at soft peaks — flops slightly when you lift the beater. Stiffer = brutal to fold.
tips: If you're not sure the bowl is grease-free, wipe it with a little lemon juice or vinegar first.

steps.title: Combine yolks and chocolate
steps.content: Yolks + vanilla into chocolate.
steps.detail: Chocolate now cool but still fluid (finger test: warm not hot). Whisk in yolks and vanilla till glossy.
tips: If the mix suddenly stiffens, the chocolate was too cool — a brief spell back over the warm pan loosens it.

steps.title: Loosen with whites
steps.content: Fold third of whites to loosen.
steps.detail: SACRIFICE FOLD — lightening the chocolate. Vigorous. Don't be precious.

steps.title: Fold until just combined
steps.content: Fold rest of whites + cream gently.
steps.detail: Big sweeping strokes with a large spatula. Bottom up and across — scoop, fold, rotate bowl. Stop the moment no white streaks. Overmixing flattens.
tips: Check the bottom of the bowl for hidden pockets of chocolate before you stop.

steps.title: Chill until set
steps.content: Glasses, chill 4 hr.
steps.detail: Pretty glasses or ramekins. Chill 4 hr minimum — overnight better. Extra whipped cream and chocolate shavings on top to serve. Note: this contains raw egg — use very fresh or pasteurised eggs, and skip it for anyone pregnant, very young, elderly or immunocompromised.

title: Crème Brûlée
description: The crack of caramelised sugar, silk custard below. A restaurant classic.
storage: Untopped custards keep 3 days covered in the fridge; they don't freeze. Once the sugar is torched you have about 15 minutes of crack — there's no storing that.
make_ahead: Made for it — bake and chill the custards up to 2 days ahead, then sugar and torch just before serving.

substitutions.swap: pure (pouring) cream also works — avoid light cream, which sets thinner

steps.title: Infuse the cream
steps.content: Heat cream with vanilla, rest 10 min.
steps.detail: Cream into a saucepan. Pod (split, seeds scraped, both in) or extract later. Warm till first steam and edge bubbles — DON'T boil. Off heat, cover, infuse 10 min. Fish out the pod.
tips: Keep the lid on while it rests so the vanilla aroma stays in the cream, not the kitchen.

steps.title: Beat the yolks
steps.content: Whisk yolks with sugar till pale.
steps.detail: Yolks and sugar, 2–3 min till pale and thickened. Colour change tells you sugar is dissolved. Extract goes in now if using.
tips: Save the whites in the freezer — future pavlova.

steps.title: Temper the yolks
steps.content: Temper yolks with warm cream.
steps.detail: VERY slowly pour warm cream into yolks while whisking constantly. Too fast = scrambled. Strain through a sieve into a jug — catches any stray cooked bits.
tips: Sit the bowl on a damp tea towel so it stays put while you pour and whisk one-handed.

steps.title: Set up the water bath
steps.content: Ramekins in a water bath.
steps.detail: Divide between 4 ramekins in a deep roasting tin. Boiling water into the tin to halfway up the ramekins — bath cooks custard gently and evenly.
warnings: Pour the boiling water with the tin already on the oven shelf if you can — carrying a full water bath is a scald waiting to happen.

steps.title: Bake until just set
steps.content: Bake 40 min at 150°C till just set.
steps.detail: Middle rack. 40 min — centre should wobble like set jelly, not slosh. Carefully out, cool to room temp, then fridge 3 hr minimum (overnight perfect).
tips: Ovens vary — start checking at 35 minutes rather than trusting the clock.

steps.title: Caramelise the top
steps.content: Sugar on top, torch or grill.
steps.detail: Just before serving: 1 tbsp sugar on each, tilt to spread. Torch till deep amber (sweep, don't dwell), or very hot grill 1–2 min watching like a hawk. Caramel hardens 1 min.
tips: Under the grill, sit the ramekins right up near the element on an upturned tray.
warnings: Hot caramel sticks and burns badly — no fingers near the surface until it has fully hardened.

steps.title: Crack and serve
steps.content: Crack with the back of a spoon.
steps.detail: The moment. Tap caramel with back of a spoon — cracks like ice. Scoop through to cold custard. Eat now — caramel softens in 15 min from condensation.

title: Strawberry Eton Mess
description: Broken meringue, cream, macerated strawberries. Effortlessly glamorous.
storage: Assembled, it doesn't keep — the meringue dissolves into the cream within the hour. Store spare components separately: berries and whipped cream 2 days in the fridge, meringues airtight in the pantry.
make_ahead: Macerate the berries and whip the cream up to 4 hours ahead, both covered in the fridge — assemble only when everyone's nearly ready for it.
leftovers: Spare macerated strawberries are brilliant over ice cream or porridge.

substitutions.swap: any soft fruit — raspberries, chopped mango, or a mix
a broken-up bought pavlova shell works just as well

steps.title: Macerate the strawberries
steps.content: Hull and halve, macerate 15 min.
steps.detail: Twist out the green stem, halve (quarter if large). Toss with sugar and a squeeze of lemon. 15 min — sugar draws out juice, glossy syrup. Mash about a third with a fork for colour.
tips: Taste one first — dull berries can take an extra spoon of sugar and a bit more lemon.

steps.title: Whip the cream
steps.content: Whip cream to soft peaks.
steps.detail: Cold cream, cold bowl. Vanilla. Medium till gentle pillows — soft, not stiff. Firms further when folded with berries.
tips: Stop early rather than late — you can always give it a few more turns by hand.

steps.title: Smash the meringues
steps.content: Crush meringues into chunks.
steps.detail: Break meringues between your fingers into bite-sized pieces AND dust. Mix of sizes — some bits melt into cream, others crunch.

steps.title: Build the layers
steps.content: Layer in glasses.
steps.detail: Spoon cream, berries with syrup, meringue, repeat. Layers look prettier than a fold. Don't overmix — lose the stripes.
tips: Save a little of everything so each glass finishes with berries and a shard of meringue on top.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Mint and serve.
steps.detail: Torn mint on top. 15 min window — meringue starts dissolving from the cream's moisture. That's fine but don't make hours ahead.

title: Margherita Pizza
description: Blistered crust, tomato, mozzarella, basil. The perfect starter recipe.
equipment: pizza stone or your heaviest baking tray
storage: Leftover slices keep 3 days in the fridge; reheat in a dry frying pan with a lid on for a crisp base and melted top. Dough balls freeze for a month — thaw overnight in the fridge.
make_ahead: The dough gets better with time — make it the night before and let it rise slowly in the fridge, then bring it to room temperature for an hour before stretching.

substitutions.swap: a block of regular mozzarella — drier and melts well; pat fresh mozzarella dry either way
passata — a few tablespoons, seasoned the same way
plain flour if you can't find bread flour — slightly less chew, still great

steps.title: Mix and prove the dough
steps.content: Make dough, rest 1 hr.
steps.detail: Yeast into 200 ml warm water (warm-to-touch, not hot). 5 min till foamy — yeast is alive. Add flour, salt, oil, mix to shaggy dough. Knead 5 min smooth, or no-knead 1 hr with a few folds. Cover, rise 1 hr till doubled.
tips: No foam after 10 minutes means dead yeast — start again with a fresh sachet before wasting the flour.
Doubled test: poke the dough — the dimple should stay.

steps.title: Crush the sauce
steps.content: Sauce.
steps.detail: Drain tomatoes of excess juice. Blitz or crush with olive oil, 1 grated garlic clove, salt. That's it. DON'T cook — the oven does that. Great pizza is restraint.
tips: Taste it — it should be bright and a touch salty; it mellows in the oven.

steps.title: Get the oven blazing
steps.content: Preheat oven MAX with stone.
steps.detail: Stone or heaviest tray in oven, crank to max (250°C+). Preheat 20 min. A blazing surface is the difference between pub and frozen.

steps.title: Stretch the bases
steps.content: Stretch by hand.
steps.detail: Divide in two. Floured bench, press out from the centre with fingertips, then backs of knuckles to stretch — edge stays a touch thicker (the crust). 25cm rounds. Rolling flattens air bubbles and toughens.
tips: If the dough keeps springing back, walk away for 5 minutes — rested gluten stretches without a fight.

steps.title: Dress the bases
steps.content: Top sparingly.
steps.detail: Onto baking paper. Thin sauce layer, rim bare. Tear mozzarella into chunks and dot — don't carpet. Olive oil drizzle. Fewer toppings, better pizza.
tips: Fresh mozzarella weeping? Pat the chunks dry first or the middle goes soupy.

steps.title: Slide and bake
steps.content: Bake 8–10 min till blistered.
steps.detail: Slide onto the stone with paper underneath. 8–10 min till crust is puffed and spotted with char. Bottom should have crisp patches.
tips: Slide the baking paper out from under the pizza halfway through for a crisper base.
warnings: The stone holds ferocious heat — good mitts and long sleeves when sliding the pizza in and out.

steps.title: Finish with basil
steps.content: Basil AFTER.
steps.detail: Basil on AFTER baking, never before — burns. Tear leaves (don't chop), scatter while piping hot so they wilt into the cheese. More oil if you like. Cut, inhale.

title: Quiche Lorraine
description: Buttery pastry, smoky bacon, silky custard. Picnic legend.
equipment: baking paper and rice or dried beans (pastry weights)
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge, covered, or freeze slices for up to 2 months. Reheat in a 160°C oven for about 15 minutes — the microwave turns the pastry soggy.
make_ahead: Blind-bake the shell and fry the bacon and onion a day ahead. The whole quiche is arguably better baked in the morning and eaten at room temperature.
leftovers: A cold slice in the lunchbox with a spoonful of chutney is a very good day.

substitutions.swap: ham off the bone, or skip it and add a sautéed leek for a vegetarian version
true gruyère if you're feeling fancy, or plain tasty — anything that grates and melts

steps.title: Blind-bake the shell
steps.content: Line and blind-bake 12 min at 190°C.
steps.detail: Shortcrust pressed into a 23cm tart tin (loose base = easy unmoulding). Trim, prick base, chill 10 min. Line with baking paper, fill with rice/beans as weights. Bake 12 min. Remove paper, 3 min more till dry to touch.
tips: Patch any cracks with a scrap of raw pastry as soon as the shell comes out — leaks are custard's escape route.

steps.title: Render the bacon
steps.content: Crisp bacon and onion.
steps.detail: Sliced bacon and onion. Dry-fry bacon over medium till fat renders and edges crisp, 5 min. Add onion, 4 min soft. Drain on paper — too wet and your custard separates.
tips: Start the bacon in a cold pan — the fat renders out slowly instead of scorching.

steps.title: Mix the custard
steps.content: Whisk custard.
steps.detail: Eggs in a jug. Whisk in cream, milk, pinch of nutmeg, black pepper. Don't salt — bacon and cheese do that.

steps.title: Fill the shell
steps.content: Scatter in the shell.
steps.detail: Bacon and onion first, then grated cheese. Tart tin on a baking tray to catch any leaks.

steps.title: Pour in the custard
steps.content: Pour custard carefully.
steps.detail: Slowly pour in, almost to the top but not spilling. Fill in the oven if the tray's already in so you don't slosh.
tips: Any custard that won't fit, leave out — an overfull shell boils over and glues to the tin.

steps.title: Bake until just set
steps.content: Bake 30–35 min at 170°C just set.
steps.detail: Middle rack. Till centre has a slight wobble, top pale gold. Overbake and custard splits. Cool 10 min before slicing — finishes setting as it rests.
tips: The wobble test: nudge the tray — the centre should shudder as one piece, not ripple like liquid.

title: Chicken & Mushroom Pie
description: Creamy filling, crispy puff pastry lid. Pub classic, home trophy.
equipment: wide deep frying pan or sauté pan
storage: The baked pie keeps 3 days in the fridge; reheat slices in a 180°C oven until piping hot in the middle. The filling alone freezes for 3 months — thaw before topping and baking.
make_ahead: Make the filling up to 2 days ahead and chill it — cold filling under the pastry actually bakes up crisper, with no soggy lid.
leftovers: Leftover filling is a ready-made dinner spooned over mash or thick toast.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — it cooks faster, so simmer just 6–7 minutes or it dries out
crème fraîche, or evaporated milk in a pinch
1/2 tsp dried thyme, added with the stock

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Brown chicken in batches.
steps.detail: Chicken cut into bite chunks, seasoned well. Butter in a wide pan, brown in batches — crowding steams instead of sears. 3 min per batch, one turn only. Remove.
tips: Dry the chicken with paper towel before it hits the pan — wet chicken steams grey instead of browning.
warnings: Raw chicken rules: wash hands, board and knife before touching anything else.

steps.title: Fry the mushrooms hard
steps.content: Sauté mushrooms hard, then leek + onion.
steps.detail: Mushrooms halved if large. Hot pan till deep gold — mushrooms need high heat or they go grey. Remove. Lower heat, more butter, sliced leek (whites and pale green), diced onion, garlic, 6 min.
tips: Leave the mushrooms alone in the pan — constant stirring stops them colouring.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.content: Roux then stock.
steps.detail: Flour sprinkled over veg, stir and cook 90 sec (raw flour taste off). Slowly whisk in stock constantly — thickens as it heats. Lumps? Keep whisking, they'll smooth out.

steps.title: Simmer the filling
steps.content: Return chicken + mushrooms, simmer 10 min.
steps.detail: Back in with any juices. Thyme sprigs. 10 min till chicken cooked and sauce glossy. Stir in cream and dijon. Taste for salt. Cut the biggest chunk of chicken in half to check — it should be white right through, with no pink.

steps.title: Lid the pie
steps.content: Pie dish, pastry on top.
steps.detail: Pour into pie dish. Pastry on top, trim past the edge, tuck/crimp with a fork. Small cross in the centre to vent steam. Egg wash.
tips: If the pastry has gone floppy in a warm kitchen, 10 minutes in the freezer firms it for neat crimping.

steps.title: Bake till golden
steps.content: Bake 25 min at 200°C.
steps.detail: Till pastry deep gold and puffed, filling bubbling at the vent. Rest 5 min before cutting — filling is lava-hot straight out.

title: Bread & Butter Pudding
description: Buttered bread soaked in vanilla custard, sultanas, a sugar crust. Nan food at its finest.
storage: Keeps 3 days covered in the fridge, and freezes for a month if you must. Reheat portions in a 160°C oven so the top re-crisps — the microwave works but the crust goes soft.
make_ahead: Assemble and soak it up to 4 hours ahead, covered in the fridge — a longer soak only makes it more custardy. Add the sugar top just before baking.
leftovers: Cold squares straight from the fridge, eaten standing at the bench, are a time-honoured tradition.

substitutions.swap: day-old brioche, fruit loaf or hot cross buns — all gloriously good here
sultanas, or dark choc bits for the kids
all milk (500 ml total) for a lighter, softer set

steps.title: Butter every slice
steps.content: Butter bread, cut to triangles.
steps.detail: Softened butter on one side of each slice — generously. Diagonal cut into triangles. Day-old bread holds its shape; fresh goes to mush.
tips: Leave the crusts on — they turn into the best crunchy bits.

steps.title: Layer the dish
steps.content: Layer bread and raisins.
steps.detail: Rub a dish with butter. Overlap bread triangles in rows, butter-side-up, raisins scattered between layers. Distributes fruit evenly.
tips: Tuck most of the raisins between the slices — exposed ones on top can scorch.

steps.title: Make the custard
steps.content: Whisk custard.
steps.detail: Eggs in a jug. Whisk milk, cream, sugar, vanilla, cinnamon, pinch of salt. Strain through a sieve for extra silkiness.

steps.title: Soak the bread
steps.content: Pour over, rest 15 min.
steps.detail: Slowly pour custard over the bread, soak every slice. Press gently with a spatula. REST 15 min — bread drinks the custard, whole point. Visibly soaked through.

steps.title: Crust the top
steps.content: Sugar top, grate nutmeg.
steps.detail: 2 tbsp sugar evenly on top — becomes crunchy gold crust. Fresh nutmeg grated over (pre-ground fine, fresh transforms).

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Bake 40 min at 180°C.
steps.detail: Middle shelf. Custard just set (slight centre wobble OK, no sloshing), peaks deep gold. 40–45 min. Rest 10 min. Warm with cream or ice cream.
tips: Peaks browning too fast? Loosely tent with foil for the last 10 minutes.

title: Tandoori Chicken
description: Yoghurt-marinated, fire-charred chicken. Grill or oven.
equipment: wire rack set over a baking tray
storage: Cooked chicken keeps 3 days in the fridge, or freezes for 2 months. Reheat until piping hot right through — a 180°C oven keeps the crust better than the microwave.
make_ahead: The marinade IS the make-ahead — overnight in the fridge gives the most tender, flavour-soaked result.
leftovers: Shred leftovers into wraps with yoghurt, red onion and a handful of salad.

substitutions.swap: drumsticks (same method), or breast — shorten the cook and watch it closely, breast dries fast
sweet paprika plus a pinch of chilli powder if you don't have smoked
plain curry powder holds the fort if garam masala is out of reach — a touch less fragrant

steps.title: Score to the bone
steps.content: Slash chicken to the bone.
steps.detail: 2–3 deep slashes into each piece down to the bone with a sharp knife. Marinade penetrates, heat reaches the centre faster. Pat dry first.
warnings: Wash hands, board and knife straight after — raw chicken touches nothing else.

steps.title: Make the marinade
steps.content: Mix marinade.
steps.detail: Yoghurt, lemon juice, grated garlic and ginger, paprika, turmeric, cumin, garam masala, big pinch of salt. Vivid orange, fragrant. Yoghurt is the trick — lactic acid tenderises.
tips: Use full-fat Greek yoghurt — low-fat splits in the oven's heat.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Marinate 4 hr, ideally overnight.
steps.detail: Coat every piece — get marinade into the slashes with your fingers. Cover, fridge 4 hr min. Overnight = magic transformation. Out 30 min before cooking.
warnings: Marinate in the fridge, never on the bench, and bin any marinade left in the bowl.

steps.title: Roast on a rack
steps.content: Oven 220°C, rack, 30 min.
steps.detail: Rack on tray lets air circulate and fat drip. 30 min till marinade is dry and crusted, black-edged char patches — that char IS the flavour. 75°C internal at the thickest point.
tips: Line the tray under the rack with foil — the drips burn hard and are miserable to scrub.

steps.title: Char under the grill
steps.content: Grill 3 min for char.
steps.detail: Max top-heat grill — watch closely. Singed peaks, not burnt. 2–4 min plenty. Rest 5 min tented.
tips: Leave the oven door ajar if your grill cycles off when it's closed — you want constant top heat.

steps.title: Finish and serve
steps.content: Red onion, lemon, coriander.
steps.detail: Thin red onion slices (soak 5 min in cold water to mellow), scatter with torn coriander. Extra lemon wedges. Naan, rice, or crunchy salad alongside.

title: Honey Soy Chicken Wings
description: Sticky, glossy, finger-licking wings. The weekend default.
equipment: wire rack set over a baking tray
large bowl or zip-lock bag for marinating
fine grater for the garlic and ginger
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze cooked wings for up to 2 months. Reheat in a 200°C oven for 10 to 15 minutes until piping hot — the microwave turns the skin rubbery.
make_ahead: Marinate the wings overnight in the fridge — the flavour deepens and the honey browns even better. Keep the reserved glaze in a jar alongside.
leftovers: Pull leftover meat off the bones for fried rice or a sticky chicken roll.

substitutions.swap: white wine vinegar or apple cider vinegar
any hot sauce you have, or leave it out for the kids
1/2 tsp ground ginger in the marinade if fresh is out

steps.title: Dry the wings
steps.content: Pat wings DRY.
steps.detail: The single step that transforms oven wings from rubbery to crispy. Every wing with paper towel till completely dry. Moisture is the enemy of crisp skin. Even better: salt and air-dry uncovered in the fridge for an hour first.
tips: Wingettes and drumettes cook more evenly than whole wings — ask the butcher to joint them if you can.
warnings: Wash your hands, the board and anything the raw wings touched before you move on.

steps.title: Mix the sticky marinade
steps.content: Whisk marinade.
steps.detail: Soy, honey, grated garlic and ginger, sesame oil, rice vinegar, sriracha if using. RESERVE 1/3 for glazing later — never dip cooked chicken in raw marinade.
tips: Rub a drop of oil around the measuring cup first and the honey slides straight out.

steps.title: Marinate the wings
steps.content: Toss and marinate 30 min.
steps.detail: 2/3 of the marinade onto wings in a bowl or bag. 30 min room temp (or overnight fridge). The sugar in honey helps browning.
tips: A zip-lock bag lets you turn the wings over without dirtying another bowl.
warnings: Room temperature tops out at 30 minutes — any longer than that, marinate in the fridge.

steps.title: Bake on a rack
steps.content: Rack bake 40 min at 220°C.
steps.detail: Rack over a tray — no crowding, space crisps them instead of steaming. 40 min, turn once halfway. Deep gold skin, 75°C internal.
tips: Line the tray under the rack with foil or baking paper — honey drips burn hard onto bare metal.

steps.title: Glaze and caramelise
steps.content: Brush with reserved marinade, 5 min more.
steps.detail: Out, brush with reserved marinade generously. Back in 5 min till glaze caramelises to sticky lacquer. Watch — sugar burns fast.
tips: If the glaze isn't sticky after 5 minutes, give it 2 more — it should bubble at the edges of each wing.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.content: Spring onion, sesame seeds.
steps.detail: Platter, drizzle sesame oil, sliced spring onion, sprinkle of sesame seeds if you have them. Immediately. Plenty of napkins.
tips: Slice the spring onions on a sharp angle for longer, prettier curls.

title: Baba Ganoush
description: Charred eggplant whipped with tahini and lemon. Smoke in a bowl.
equipment: gas burner, hot grill or oven
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge with a thin film of olive oil over the top. It doesn't freeze well — the texture turns watery. Serve at room temperature rather than reheating.
make_ahead: Make it a day ahead — the smoke settles and the garlic mellows. Hold the oil, sumac and parsley until just before serving.
leftovers: Spread it in a wrap with roast veg, or thin with a little water into a sauce for grilled lamb.

substitutions.swap: a little lemon zest, or smoked paprika for a different but good finish
mint or coriander — both sit happily with the smoke

steps.title: Blacken the eggplants
steps.content: Char the whole eggplants until collapsed and blistered.
steps.detail: The whole point of baba ganoush is smoke. Sit the eggplants directly on a gas flame or under a screaming-hot grill, turning with tongs every few minutes, until the skin is blackened all over and the flesh has fully slumped — 12 to 15 min on flame. No gas? Roast whole at 220C for 45 min, prick first so they don't burst. They should look ruined; that's correct.
tips: Lay foil around the burner first — the sticky drips are a pain to scrub off later.
warnings: The eggplants get scalding and can spit steam — turn them with long tongs, never fingers.

steps.title: Empty the skins
steps.content: Cool, then scoop the flesh and drain it.
steps.detail: Let them cool enough to handle, slit open and scoop the soft flesh away from the charred skin — a few flecks of black are flavour, but don't include the bitter skin itself. Tip the flesh into a sieve and let it drain 10 min. Eggplant holds a lot of water and skipping this gives you a watery, sad dip.
tips: Save a spoonful of the smoky juice from the sieve — you can stir it back in if the dip needs more smoke.

steps.title: Mash and season
steps.content: Mash or pulse with tahini, lemon, garlic and salt.
steps.detail: Traditionalists chop by hand with a fork for a rustic texture; a quick pulse in the processor is fine too — just don't puree it to baby food, you want some body. Add the tahini, juice of the whole lemon, one finely grated garlic clove and a good pinch of salt. Taste: it should be tangy and nutty. Tahini varies wildly in bitterness, so adjust lemon and salt until it sings.
tips: Stir the tahini jar well before measuring — the oil separates and the top pour is thin.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.content: Spread, pool with olive oil, dust with sumac and parsley.
steps.detail: Swoosh into a shallow bowl with the back of a spoon, leaving a well in the middle. Pour the olive oil into the well, scatter the sumac for a lemony-purple tang and tear over parsley. Serve with warm flatbread. It tastes even better an hour later once the garlic mellows.
tips: Warm the flatbread in a dry pan just before it hits the table.

title: Fattoush Salad
description: Crunchy chopped salad, crisp bread shards, a sharp sumac dressing.
equipment: oven and baking tray (or frying pan for the bread)
jar with a lid for the dressing
storage: Dressed fattoush doesn't keep — the bread and leaves go soggy within hours, so only dress what you'll eat. Undressed chopped veg keeps a day, covered, in the fridge.
make_ahead: Crisp the bread and make the dressing up to a day ahead — store the shards airtight at room temperature and shake the jar again before using. Don't dress the salad until the last minute.

substitutions.swap: extra lemon juice plus a pinch of zest — you lose the colour but keep the tang
flat-leaf parsley, or half and half
2 thinly sliced spring onions for a gentler bite

steps.title: Crisp the bread
steps.content: Tear the bread and toast or fry it to shards.
steps.detail: Fattoush exists to use up stale flatbread, so the bread is the star, not an afterthought. Tear into rough shards, toss with a little olive oil and bake at 200C for 6 to 8 min until snapping-crisp and golden, or shallow-fry for the proper indulgent version. They must be fully crisp or they go soggy the second they meet the dressing.
tips: Spread the shards in a single layer — piled up they bend instead of snap.

steps.title: Cut everything chunky
steps.content: Chop the veg into bold, even pieces.
steps.detail: Fattoush is a chopped salad, so cut cucumber and tomato into 2 cm chunks, slice the cos across into ribbons and the red onion paper-thin. Keep everything roughly the same size so each forkful is balanced. Use the ripest tomatoes you can find — they bleed sweet juice into the dressing.
tips: If raw onion is too sharp for you, soak the slices in cold water for 5 minutes and drain.

steps.title: Shake the dressing
steps.content: Whisk the sumac dressing.
steps.detail: In a jar: the tablespoon of sumac, juice of the whole lemon, the olive oil, a small grated garlic clove if you like, salt and pepper. Sumac is the soul here — it's a ground dried berry that brings a tart, almost cranberry-like zing without vinegar. Shake hard so it emulsifies into a cloudy ruby dressing.
tips: Taste it on a lettuce leaf, not a spoon — on its own it should seem slightly too sharp.

steps.title: Dress at the last minute
steps.content: Toss everything just before serving.
steps.detail: Combine the veg, torn mint and most of the bread in a big bowl, pour over the dressing and toss with your hands. Do this at the last possible minute — dressed too early and the bread softens and the leaves wilt. Scatter the reserved bread and a final pinch of sumac on top for crunch and colour.

title: Muhammara
description: Roasted red capsicum and walnut dip with a smoky, sweet-sour hit.
equipment: heatproof bowl and plate for steaming the capsicums
storage: Keeps 4 to 5 days in an airtight container in the fridge, or freeze for up to 2 months — thaw overnight and give it a good stir. Serve at room temperature.
make_ahead: It genuinely improves after a night in the fridge as the smoke, walnuts and lemon settle together — make it up to 2 days ahead and finish with the oil and nuts on the day.
leftovers: Brilliant smeared in a chicken wrap, or tossed through hot pasta with a splash of the cooking water.

substitutions.swap: pecans, or cashews for a milder, sweeter dip
a slice of stale bread, blitzed
a pinch of dried chilli flakes or a teaspoon of harissa

steps.title: Char and peel the capsicums
steps.content: Blacken and steam the capsicums, then peel.
steps.detail: Roast the whole capsicums at 230C (or over a flame) until the skins are charred and blistered all over, about 20 to 25 min. Tip them into a bowl and cover with a plate for 10 min — the trapped steam loosens the skins so they slip off with your fingers. Peeling matters: leftover skin makes the dip stringy. Pull out the cores and seeds too.
tips: Don't rinse the peeled flesh under the tap — you'd wash the smoky flavour away. Wipe stray seeds off with your fingers.
warnings: Lift the plate off the bowl away from you — the trapped steam is hot.

steps.title: Toast the nuts
steps.content: Toast the walnuts until fragrant.
steps.detail: Spread the walnuts on a dry tray and toast at 180C for 6 to 8 min, or in a dry pan, until they smell nutty and look a shade darker. Toasting deepens their flavour and chases off any raw bitterness. Keep a few back to chop and scatter on top later for texture.
tips: Set a timer — nuts burn quietly, and burnt walnuts make the whole dip bitter.

steps.title: Pulse everything together
steps.content: Blitz everything to a coarse paste.
steps.detail: Into the processor: peeled capsicum, toasted walnuts, breadcrumbs, olive oil, juice of the lemon, cumin, the chilli if using and a good pinch of salt. Pulse to a thick, slightly grainy paste — muhammara should have a little rubble to it, not be silky like hummus. The breadcrumbs soak up moisture and give it body.
tips: If it looks wet, pulse in another spoonful of breadcrumbs — capsicums vary in juiciness.

steps.title: Let it settle, then taste
steps.content: Rest, then taste and balance.
steps.detail: Let it sit 20 min so the flavours marry, then taste. It should land sweet, smoky, tangy and a touch hot all at once — push it with more lemon for sour or a pinch of sugar if your capsicums weren't sweet. Spread on a plate, drizzle with oil and scatter the toasted pine nuts and reserved walnuts.

title: Saffron Rice Pilaf
description: Golden basmati threaded with saffron, pine nuts and preserved lemon.
equipment: heavy-based pot with a tight-fitting lid
bowl or sieve for rinsing the rice
small dry frying pan for the pine nuts
storage: Cool leftovers quickly and refrigerate airtight within the hour — cooked rice keeps 1 day only, reheated until piping hot. Freezing dulls the saffron and softens the pine nuts, so it's best eaten fresh.
leftovers: Turn leftovers into a quick fried rice with an egg — straight from the fridge into a hot pan.

substitutions.swap: chicken stock if you're not cooking vegetarian
finely grated zest of a fresh lemon plus an extra pinch of salt
slivered almonds or chopped pistachios

steps.title: Steep the saffron
steps.content: Bloom the saffron in a splash of warm water.
steps.detail: Crush the saffron threads between your fingers into a small cup and cover with 2 tbsp of warm (not boiling) water. Let it steep while you cook — this draws out the colour and honeyed aroma evenly. Adding dry threads straight to the pot wastes the precious stuff and gives patchy colour.
tips: Half boiling water from the kettle, half cold tap water lands at about the right warmth.

steps.title: Rinse the basmati
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear.
steps.detail: Swirl the basmati in a bowl of cold water, drain and repeat three or four times until the cloudiness goes. That cloud is surface starch — rinsing it off is the difference between separate, fluffy grains and a sticky clump. A final 10 min soak makes the grains lengthen beautifully.
tips: Drain the soaked rice really well — clinging water throws off the stock ratio.

steps.title: Coat every grain
steps.content: Soften the onion, toast the rice in butter.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in a heavy pot and cook the diced onion gently for 6 to 8 min until soft and golden. Tip in the drained rice and stir for a minute so every grain gets coated in butter — this seals the grains so they stay distinct, the heart of the pilaf method.
tips: The rice is ready when the grains turn from translucent back to chalky-white at the edges.

steps.title: Steam without peeking
steps.content: Add stock and saffron, then steam undisturbed.
steps.detail: Pour in the stock and the saffron water with its threads, season, and bring to a boil. The moment it boils, drop to the lowest heat, lid on, and leave it alone for 12 min — no peeking, no stirring, or you'll knock out the steam and turn it gloopy. Then off the heat, still covered, 10 min to finish steaming.
tips: If your smallest burner runs hot, slide the pot half off the flame for the gentlest heat.

steps.title: Finish and fluff
steps.content: Toast pine nuts, fold through preserved lemon and fluff.
steps.detail: While it rests, toast the pine nuts in a dry pan over medium until golden — watch like a hawk, they go from pale to burnt in seconds. Finely chop the preserved lemon, discarding the soft pulp and using mostly the salty, intense rind. Fork through the rice with the pine nuts, preserved lemon and parsley, lifting the grains apart rather than stirring.

title: Sumac-Roasted Cauliflower with Tahini
description: Deeply roasted cauliflower and chickpeas over a lemony tahini pool.
equipment: large pot if cooking dried chickpeas
storage: Keeps 3 days in the fridge, sauce and veg stored airtight and separately if you can. Reheat the veg in a hot oven for 10 minutes to bring back some crunch; the sauce is served cold anyway. Doesn't freeze well.
