Browned leeks taste scorched and turn the soup beige — if you see gold appearing, drop the heat and add a splash of water.

steps.title: Simmer the potatoes
steps.content: Add potato, stock and bay; simmer 20 minutes until collapsing.
steps.detail: Add the potato chunks, pour in the stock and drop in the bay leaf if using. Bring it up to a gentle simmer and cook with the lid ajar for 20 minutes. It's ready when a knife slides through the biggest piece of potato with no resistance at all — undercooked potato blends grainy, so when in doubt, give it 5 more minutes.

steps.title: Blend it silky
steps.content: Fish out the bay leaf and blend until completely smooth.
steps.detail: Take the pot off the heat and remove the bay leaf. A stick blender straight in the pot is easiest — keep the head fully submerged so it doesn't spray, and work it around for a good minute or two until there are no flecks left. In an upright blender, do it in two or three batches. The difference between a nice soup and a great one is 60 more seconds of blending than you think it needs.
warnings: Hot soup in a sealed blender builds steam pressure and can blow the lid off. Fill it half at most, remove the centre cap and hold a folded tea towel over the hole while you blend.

steps.title: Finish with cream
steps.content: Stir cream through, season well, warm gently — don't boil.
steps.detail: Return the pot to low heat and stir in the cream. Warm it through gently without letting it boil hard, which can split the cream and dulls the fresh leek flavour. Now season properly: potato soups drink salt, so add a little, stir, taste and repeat until the flavour suddenly opens up — you'll know the moment when it happens. Ladle into bowls with a swirl of cream, a crack of pepper and the spring onion scattered over.

title: Cauliflower Cheese
description: The British side that upstages the roast — cauliflower under a mustardy cheddar mornay with a crisp golden crumb.
storage: Keeps 2 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat uncovered at 180°C fan until piping hot — the oven revives the crunchy top, where a microwave turns it watery.
make_ahead: Assemble it completely (unbaked), cover and refrigerate up to a day ahead — bake straight from the fridge, adding about 10 extra minutes.
leftovers: Mash leftovers roughly onto buttered toast and flash it under a hot grill — an accidental masterpiece.

substitutions.swap: any good melting cheese — a block of tasty is perfect, and half cheddar half parmesan is even punchier
a teaspoon of mustard powder, or wholegrain mustard
blitzed stale bread, or crushed plain crackers
broccoli, or half-and-half, works beautifully under the same sauce

ingredients.amount: 200 g, grated (150 g for the sauce, 50 g for the top)

steps.title: Blanch the cauliflower
steps.content: Big florets into boiling salted water for 3 minutes only.
steps.detail: Get the oven heating to 200°C fan. Cut the cauliflower into large florets — big enough that they hold their shape under the sauce. Drop them into a large pot of boiling salted water for just 3 minutes; you're taking the raw edge off, not cooking them through, and a knife should still meet real resistance in the stems. They finish softening in the oven — fully cooked florets turn to mush in the bake.

steps.title: Dry it thoroughly
steps.content: Drain and let the florets steam completely dry, 5 minutes.
steps.detail: Drain the florets into a colander and leave them to steam-dry for a good 5 minutes, giving the colander a shake now and then, then pat them with paper towel. This step matters more than it looks: cauliflower holds water in all its little branches, and that water leaks out in the oven and turns a beautiful thick mornay into a thin puddle. Dry cauliflower is the whole difference between cauliflower cheese and cauliflower soup.

steps.title: Make the roux
steps.content: Melt butter, stir in flour, cook 2 minutes until biscuity.
steps.detail: In a medium saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat, then tip in all the flour at once and stir it into a thick paste — this is a roux, the thickener behind a hundred good sauces. Keep stirring for about 2 minutes until it smells toasty and biscuity rather than of raw flour, and looks like wet sand. Don't let it brown — you want it blond.

steps.title: Build the mornay
steps.content: Whisk in milk gradually, simmer thick, melt in cheese off heat.
steps.detail: Add the milk a splash at a time, whisking each addition completely smooth before the next — the sauce will look alarmingly lumpy and claggy at the start, and that's normal; keep whisking and it loosens into silk. Once all the milk is in, let it bubble gently for 2–3 minutes, whisking, until it thickly coats the back of a spoon. Take it off the heat, then stir in 150 g of the cheddar with the mustard, nutmeg, salt and pepper. Off the heat matters: boiling melted cheese turns the sauce grainy.
tips: Lumps anyway? Whisk hard, or blitz the sauce with a stick blender — no one will ever know.

steps.title: Assemble and top
steps.content: Cauliflower in the dish, sauce over, crumbs and cheese on top.
steps.detail: Arrange the florets snugly in the baking dish in one rough layer, stems down. Pour the mornay over slowly so it runs down into every gap — nudge the florets around so nobody's left bald. Mix the breadcrumbs with the remaining 50 g of cheddar and the parmesan, and scatter this evenly over the top; the crumb bakes into the crunchy contrast all that soft cauliflower needs.

steps.title: Bake until blistered
steps.content: 20–25 minutes at 200°C fan — deep gold, bubbling at the edges.
steps.detail: Bake for 20–25 minutes until the top is deep golden brown with darker blistered patches and the sauce is bubbling up around the edges — pale and blond means not done yet, so hold your nerve. Rest it for 5 minutes before serving; the sauce thickens as it cools from molten to gorgeous.

title: Caramelised Onion and Feta Tart
description: Golden puff pastry piled with sweet, jammy slow-cooked onions, salty feta and thyme. Patience with the onions is the whole recipe.
storage: Best the day it's baked. Keeps 2 days covered in the fridge — re-crisp in a 180°C fan oven for 8 minutes rather than the microwave, which turns puff pastry sad and soft.
make_ahead: The caramelised onions keep a week in a jar in the fridge and freeze well — make a double batch and the next tart takes 25 minutes.
leftovers: A cold slice is a genuinely good lunchbox win, and spare onions are brilliant in a cheese toastie.

substitutions.swap: crumbled blue cheese for a punchier tart, or grated cheddar for a milder one
red wine vinegar plus an extra pinch of brown sugar
rosemary, chopped fine — use a little less

steps.title: Slice the onions
steps.content: Peel and slice all 5 onions into thin half-moons.
steps.detail: Halve the onions through the root, peel, and slice into half-moons about 5 mm thick. Yes, it looks like a ridiculous mountain — it cooks down to less than a quarter of the size. A sharp knife bruises the onion less, which means fewer tears; breathing through your mouth genuinely helps too. Take the pastry out of the freezer now so it thaws while the onions cook.

steps.title: Caramelise them slowly
steps.content: Medium-low heat, 30 minutes, stirring occasionally — no shortcuts.
steps.detail: Melt the butter with the oil in your largest frying pan over medium-low, add all the onions and the salt, and stir to coat. Now cook for a full 30 minutes, stirring every 4–5 minutes. They'll sweat, collapse, turn gold and finally deep amber and jammy — real caramelising takes 30 minutes, not 10, and cranking the heat just gives you burnt edges on raw onion. If the pan base catches, that's flavour: splash in a tablespoon of water and scrape it back into the onions.
tips: The salt at the start pulls moisture out and speeds the collapse — don't skip it.
Done means: reduced to a soft tangle, deep even brown, and tasting sweet with no crunch left.

steps.title: Add balsamic and sugar
steps.content: Stir in sugar and balsamic, cook 5 minutes until jammy, then cool.
steps.detail: Stir in the brown sugar and balsamic and cook for another 5 minutes, stirring, until the liquid has cooked away and the onions look glossy and jam-like — a spoon dragged through should leave a track that closes slowly. Taste a bit: sweet and tangy with no harshness. Scrape them onto a plate and let them cool for 10 minutes; hot filling melts the butter in puff pastry before the oven gets a chance, and the pastry won't rise properly.

steps.title: Score the pastry
steps.content: Pastry on a lined tray; score a 2 cm border, prick the middle.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Lay the thawed pastry sheets side by side on a baking-paper-lined tray, pressing the joining edges together with damp fingers so they bake as one big rectangle. With the tip of a knife, score a border 2 cm in from the edge all the way around — cut about halfway through the pastry, never all the way. Prick the middle all over with a fork so it stays flat while the scored border puffs into a golden frame. Brush just the border with the beaten egg.

steps.title: Top the tart
steps.content: Onions inside the border, crumble feta over, scatter thyme.
steps.detail: Spread the cooled onions evenly across the middle of the pastry, keeping them off the egg-washed border — anything sitting on the border stops it puffing. Crumble the feta over in rough chunks (big pockets of salty cheese beat a fine sprinkle), scatter the thyme leaves and finish with a good crack of pepper. No extra salt needed — the feta has that covered.

steps.title: Bake until deep gold
steps.content: 20–25 minutes at 200°C fan until the border is deep golden.
steps.detail: Bake for 20–25 minutes until the border is puffed and deep golden and the base — lift a corner gently with a spatula to check — is browned and crisp underneath. A pale blond tart tastes of raw flour, so hold your nerve and give it the extra minutes. If the centre has ballooned anywhere, press it gently flat with the back of a spoon as soon as the tray comes out.
warnings: Don't open the oven for the first 15 minutes — puff pastry rises on trapped steam, and a cold draught can flatten it.

steps.title: Cut and serve
steps.content: Rest 5 minutes, scatter rocket, slice into squares.
steps.detail: Let the tart sit for 5 minutes on the tray — the base sets crisper as it cools slightly. Scatter the rocket over just before serving so it stays lively against the warm, sweet onions, then slice into big squares with a serrated knife. Lovely warm or at room temperature, which is what makes it a picnic and party natural.

title: Huevos Rancheros
description: Eggs set straight into a smoky tomato-chilli salsa, spooned over warm tortillas with black beans, avocado and salty feta. Breakfast that eats like a reward.
equipment: large frying pan with a lid
storage: Best eaten straight away — the eggs don't reheat well. Leftover salsa and beans keep 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge; reheat and crack in fresh eggs.
make_ahead: The salsa can be made up to 3 days ahead — reheat it in the pan until bubbling, then carry on from the egg step.
leftovers: Spare salsa is brilliant spooned over corn chips with melted cheese for quick nachos.

substitutions.swap: queso fresco if your deli has it, or a mild goat's cheese — salty and crumbly is the goal
a pinch of chilli flakes, or leave it out entirely for a gentler breakfast
any tinned bean — kidney or borlotti work fine, rinsed the same way
any flour wrap, warmed the same way and torn to fit the plate

steps.title: Prep everything first
steps.content: Dice onion, mince garlic, slice chilli, drain beans, slice avocado, crumble feta.
steps.detail: This dish moves quickly once the pan is hot, so get everything ready now. Finely dice the half onion, mince the garlic and slice the chilli thinly — scrape the seeds out if you want less heat. Drain and rinse the black beans in a sieve. Halve the avocado, flick out the stone with a spoon, and slice the flesh while it's still in the skin so you can scoop it straight out at the end. Crumble the feta and set it all within arm's reach of the stove.
tips: Wash your hands after handling the chilli — the oils cling, and a rubbed eye an hour later is no fun.

steps.title: Build the salsa
steps.content: Soften onion, add spices and tomatoes, simmer 8 min until thick.
steps.detail: Heat the oil in the large frying pan over medium. Cook the onion for 3–4 minutes, stirring now and then, until soft and translucent. Add the garlic and chilli for 30 seconds, then the cumin and paprika for another 30 — the pan should smell warm and toasty, not acrid. Tip in the tomatoes, crushing any whole ones with your spoon, add a good pinch of salt and simmer for 8 minutes. It's ready when it's thick enough that your spoon leaves a brief trail across the pan base.
tips: Swirl a splash of water in the empty tomato tin and tip it in — no tomato left behind, and it loosens the salsa for simmering.
warnings: Garlic and ground spices burn fast — the moment they smell toasty, get the tomatoes in. If they catch and turn dark, start that part again; burnt spice makes the whole salsa bitter.

steps.title: Warm the beans
steps.content: Warm beans in a small saucepan with a splash of water; mash a few.
steps.detail: Tip the rinsed beans into the small saucepan with a splash of water and a pinch of salt. Warm them over low heat for 2–3 minutes, mashing a few against the side of the pan with your spoon — half-mashed beans hold onto the tortilla instead of rolling off. Turn the heat right down and let them sit while you finish everything else; add another splash of water if they look dry.

steps.title: Warm the tortillas
steps.content: Toast tortillas in a dry pan, 20–30 seconds a side, and wrap in a tea towel.
steps.detail: The salsa has the big pan, so heat the small frying pan — dry, no oil — over medium-high on a spare burner. Warm each tortilla for 20–30 seconds a side, until soft with a few pale-brown spots — you want them floppy and steamy, not crisp. Stack them inside a clean tea towel as you go; the trapped steam keeps them warm and pliable while the eggs cook.

steps.title: Set the eggs
steps.content: Make wells in the salsa, crack in eggs, lid on, 4–5 min until whites set.
steps.detail: With the salsa at a gentle simmer, press four wells into it with the back of a spoon. Crack each egg into a cup first, then slide it into a well — much easier than aiming from a height, and no shell surprises. Lid on and cook over low heat for 4–5 minutes. Done is whites fully set and opaque with yolks that still wobble when you nudge the pan. If a patch of white stays glassy and raw, spoon a little hot salsa over it and give it another minute.
tips: Like your yolks firmer? Leave the lid on 2 more minutes. The steam under the lid does the cooking, so resist lifting it to peek before 4 minutes.

steps.title: Plate it up
steps.content: Tortillas, beans, salsa and eggs, then avocado, feta and coriander.
steps.detail: Lay two warm tortillas on each plate, slightly overlapping. Spoon the beans over, then use a big spoon to lift each egg with a generous blanket of salsa and settle it on top. Scoop over the avocado, scatter the feta — it's standing in for Mexican queso fresco, and does the same salty, crumbly job — and finish with coriander if you're using it. Eat straight away while the yolks are still runny.

title: Chicken Burrito Bowl
description: Cumin-paprika chicken over fluffy rice with black beans, charred corn, avocado and a lime sour cream. Everyone builds their own bowl.
storage: Box the components separately and most keep 3 days in the fridge — but cool the rice quickly (spread it on a plate) and eat it within 1 day, and slice avocado fresh each time.
make_ahead: Spice the chicken the night before and let it sit covered in the fridge — the flavour sinks in and dinner is 30 minutes door to bowl.
leftovers: Pile leftover chicken, beans and corn into a warmed tortilla with cheese for a next-day burrito.

substitutions.swap: chicken breast — halve thick pieces so they cook evenly, and pull them off the heat right at 74°C so they stay juicy
Greek yoghurt — same tang, a little lighter
fresh corn sliced off the cob, or a drained tin patted dry so it still chars
basmati, cooked the same way

steps.title: Get the rice on
steps.content: Rinse rice, simmer with lid on 12 min, then rest 10 off the heat.
steps.detail: Rinse the rice in a sieve under the tap until the water runs mostly clear — this washes off surface starch so the grains stay separate. Tip it into the saucepan with 2 1/4 cups of water and a pinch of salt. Bring to the boil, then clap the lid on and drop to the lowest heat for 12 minutes. Off the heat, leave it — lid still on — for 10 more minutes. No peeking either time; the trapped steam is doing the cooking. Fluff with a fork just before serving.
tips: If the bottom layer catches slightly, don't scrape it — the loose rice above will still be perfect, and the toasty layer is a cook's snack.

steps.title: Spice the chicken
steps.content: Toss thighs with oil, cumin, paprika, garlic, salt and lime juice.
steps.detail: In a bowl, mix 1 tablespoon of the oil with the cumin, paprika, garlic, 1 teaspoon of salt and the juice of half a lime. Open each thigh out flat and press on any thick lumps with your palm so the whole piece is roughly even — even thickness means even cooking. Toss the thighs through the spice paste until every surface is rusty red. They can sit while the rice simmers.
warnings: Raw chicken rules: wash your hands, the board and anything the chicken touched with hot soapy water before you touch other food.

steps.title: Sear the chicken
steps.content: Cook thighs 4–5 min a side to 74°C, rest 5 min, then slice.
steps.detail: Heat the remaining oil in the large frying pan over medium-high until it shimmers. Lay the thighs in and leave them alone for 4–5 minutes — moving them early tears the spiced crust off. When the underside is deeply browned, flip and give the other side 4–5 minutes. Done is 74°C on a thermometer at the thickest point, with juices running clear. Rest on a board for 5 minutes so the juices settle, then slice into strips.
tips: The paprika will make the pan look dark — that's spice toasting, not burning. It should smell smoky-sweet; if it smells acrid, nudge the heat down.
warnings: No thermometer? Cut into the thickest thigh — the meat must be opaque all the way through with no pink, and the juices clear.

steps.title: Char the corn
steps.content: Char corn in the empty dry pan, 5 min, until blackened in spots.
steps.detail: Keep the empty chicken pan on high heat — no need to wash it, those browned bits are flavour. Add the corn straight from frozen, spread it into one layer and leave it for a full 2 minutes before you stir. A few kernels may pop and jump — that's normal. Keep going, stirring only occasionally, for about 5 minutes until plenty of kernels have dark, charred spots. A dry pan chars better than an oiled one.

steps.title: Make the lime cream
steps.content: Mix sour cream with lime zest and juice; warm the beans; slice avocado.
steps.detail: Stir the sour cream with the zest of one lime, the juice of one lime and a pinch of salt — it should be pourable and sharp enough to make you blink; add more juice if it tastes flat. Push the corn to one side of its pan and warm the drained beans in the empty half (heat off, residual warmth is plenty), or give them 60 seconds in the microwave with a splash of water. Halve, stone and slice the avocados, and cut any remaining lime into wedges.

steps.title: Build the bowls
steps.content: Rice base, then chicken, beans, corn and avocado in sections; lime cream over.
steps.detail: Fluff the rice and divide it between four bowls. Arrange the sliced chicken, beans, charred corn and avocado in separate sections on top — keeping components apart isn't just for looks, it means everyone can dodge what they don't like and meal-preppers can box things separately. Drizzle the lime cream over, scatter coriander and finish with a squeeze of lime. If you're boxing for later, cool the rice fast on a plate first and keep the cream and avocado aside.

title: Honey Mustard Chicken Traybake
description: Chicken thighs and potatoes roasted on one tray in a sticky honey-dijon glaze, green beans tossed in at the end. Dinner with one thing to wash.
storage: Keeps 3 days in an airtight container in the fridge. Reheat in a 180°C fan oven for 10–15 minutes until piping hot — the microwave works but the skin goes soft.
make_ahead: Mix the glaze and coat the chicken in the morning; keep it covered in the fridge and it only gets better by dinner.
leftovers: Shred leftover chicken into a wrap with mayo and whatever salad is in the crisper.

substitutions.swap: wholegrain mustard for a milder, seedier glaze
broccoli florets, added at the same 10-minutes-to-go mark
sweet potato in 4 cm chunks — check it a little earlier, it softens faster
maple syrup — slightly less sweet, just as sticky

steps.title: Heat oven, make glaze
steps.content: Oven to 200°C fan; whisk honey, dijon, garlic, lemon, oil, salt and pepper.
steps.detail: Turn the oven to 200°C fan-forced so it's properly hot when the tray goes in — a cold start means pale, sad chicken. In the large bowl, whisk the honey, dijon, garlic, lemon juice, 1 tablespoon of the oil, the salt and a good crack of pepper until smooth and glossy. Taste a dot on your finger: it should be sweet first, then sharp. That balance is the whole dish.
tips: Measure the oil first, then use the same spoon for the honey — it slides straight off instead of clinging.

steps.title: Coat potatoes and chicken
steps.content: Toss potatoes with oil on the tray; pat chicken dry and turn through the glaze.
steps.detail: Potatoes first, so raw-chicken hands never touch them: tip the chunks onto the roasting tray, drizzle with the remaining oil, add a pinch of salt and toss until every piece is glossy. Now pat the chicken skin thoroughly dry with paper towel — dry skin crisps, wet skin steams — then add the thighs to the glaze bowl and turn them until coated all over. Keeping the potatoes out of the glaze matters: honey on the tray base burns before the potatoes cook, but honey on chicken skin turns to lacquer.
warnings: Wash your hands, the bowl and anything raw chicken touched with hot soapy water before moving on.

steps.title: Space out the tray
steps.content: Chicken skin-up, potatoes around, gaps between everything.
steps.detail: Push the potatoes towards the edges and arrange the thighs skin-side up among them — and leave visible gaps between every piece. A crowded tray traps steam, and steam gives you boiled-tasting chicken with flabby skin; space lets hot air brown everything. If it looks tight, split across two trays without hesitating. Tuck the thyme sprigs between the pieces if using, and drizzle any glaze left in the bowl over the chicken only.
tips: Cut side down matters for potatoes too — a flat cut face against the hot tray is where the deepest golden crust forms.

steps.title: Roast for 30 minutes
steps.content: Roast at 200°C fan for 30 min without opening the door.
steps.detail: Slide the tray into the middle of the oven and leave it for 30 minutes. Every time the door opens the temperature drops and the browning stalls, so trust it and use the light instead. By the end the kitchen should smell of honey and roast chicken, the glaze bubbling at the edges and the skin turning deep amber. If the chicken is darkening too fast at 20 minutes, loosely lay a sheet of foil over the top.

steps.title: Add the green beans
steps.content: Tuck oiled beans around the tray, spoon over juices, roast 10 more min.
steps.detail: Pull the tray out and work quickly. Toss the beans with a small drizzle of oil and tuck them into the gaps between chicken and potatoes, then spoon some of the sticky tray juices over the chicken skin — that's your second coat of glaze. Back in the oven for 10 minutes. The beans should come out bright green and just tender with a little blistering; if they're still squeaky-raw when you bite one, they needed to be spread thinner, so give them 3 more minutes.

steps.title: Check doneness and rest
steps.content: Chicken must read 74°C at the bone; rest 5 min, spoon over juices.
steps.detail: Push the thermometer into the thickest thigh, right next to the bone without touching it — you need 74°C. No thermometer? Pierce the same spot: juices must run clear with no pink at the bone. If it's not there yet, back in for 5 minutes and test again. Once done, rest the tray on the bench for 5 minutes so the juices settle back into the meat, then serve straight from the tray with the glazey pan juices spooned over everything.
tips: Potatoes should give no resistance to a knife tip. If they're firm but the chicken is done, lift the chicken off to rest and give the potatoes 5 more minutes alone.

title: Crispy Chicken Burger
description: Buttermilk-marinated thigh in a craggy golden crust, stacked with crunchy slaw and sriracha mayo on a toasted bun. Better than the drive-through.
storage: Fried chicken is best the day it's made but keeps 2 days in the fridge — re-crisp in a 200°C fan oven for 10 minutes, never the microwave. The slaw keeps 1 day before it goes watery.
make_ahead: Marinate the chicken in the buttermilk overnight in the fridge — the longer soak makes it noticeably more tender.

substitutions.swap: 1 cup milk with 1 tbsp lemon juice stirred in and stood for 10 minutes
breast, halved horizontally and bashed to even thickness — watch it closely, it dries out faster
a bag of coleslaw mix — skip the carrot too
any hot sauce you like, or sweet chilli for the kids

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Flatten thighs to even thickness, soak in seasoned buttermilk 30 min.
steps.detail: Open each thigh out flat and press or lightly bash the thick spots with your fist or a rolling pin until the whole piece is an even 2 cm — even thickness is the difference between juicy and half-raw-half-dry. In a shallow bowl, stir 1 teaspoon of salt and 1 teaspoon of the paprika into the buttermilk, then turn the thighs through it until coated. Cover and leave in the fridge for 30 minutes, or overnight if you're ahead of the game — raw chicken always marinates in the fridge, never on the bench. The buttermilk's gentle acid tenderises the meat.
warnings: Wash hands, board and anything the raw chicken touched with hot soapy water straight away.

steps.title: Make slaw and mayo
steps.content: Shred cabbage, grate carrot, dress with vinegar and mayo; spike the rest with sriracha.
steps.detail: Cut the cabbage quarter in half, slice out the hard white core, then shred it as finely as you can — thin ribbons, not chunks, is what makes slaw eat well in a burger. Grate the carrot on the coarse side of the box grater. Toss both with the vinegar, a pinch of salt and 2 tablespoons of the mayo; it should be lightly coated, not swimming. Stir the sriracha into the remaining mayo, tasting as you go until it bites back the way you like.
tips: Salt the slaw 10 minutes before serving and it softens just enough while keeping its crunch.

steps.title: Dredge the chicken
steps.content: Press each thigh hard into seasoned flour; rest on a rack.
steps.detail: In the second shallow bowl, mix the flour with the remaining paprika, remaining salt and the pepper. Drizzle a spoonful of the buttermilk marinade into the flour and rub it through with your fingertips — those little clumps become the craggy, extra-crunchy bits. Lift each thigh from the buttermilk, let the excess drip off, then press it into the flour firmly, both sides, really packing it on. Set the coated thighs on the wire rack while the oil heats — the resting time helps the coating stick instead of sliding off in the pan.
tips: Use one hand for wet and one for dry, or you'll end up wearing flour-and-buttermilk mittens.

steps.title: Heat the oil
steps.content: Heat 1 cm of oil over medium-high until a pinch of flour sizzles instantly.
steps.detail: Pour oil into the heavy pan to a depth of about 1 cm and set it over medium-high. Give it 4–5 minutes, then test: flick in a pinch of the seasoned flour. If it sizzles and bubbles instantly, you're at frying temperature (around 180°C); if it sinks quietly, wait another minute; if it smokes and browns immediately, pull the pan off the heat briefly. Getting this right matters — too-cool oil soaks into the coating and makes it greasy.
warnings: Hot oil and water are enemies: never add water to the pan, and pat away drips before frying. Keep the handle turned inward and a lid within reach — if oil ever flames, lid on, heat off, never water.

steps.title: Fry until deep golden
steps.content: Fry 4–5 min a side to deep golden and 74°C; drain on the rack.
steps.detail: Lay the thighs in gently, lowering them away from you so any splash goes the other way, and don't crowd the pan — fry in two batches if they touch. Leave them alone for 4–5 minutes until the underside is a deep golden brown, not pale blonde, then turn with tongs and do the other side. Done is 74°C at the thickest point on your thermometer, or opaque all the way through with clear juices when you cut into the thickest bit. Drain on the wire rack, never paper towel — the rack keeps the base crisp — and salt lightly while hot.
tips: If the crust is browning faster than the chicken is cooking, drop the heat a notch and give it an extra minute or two per side.

steps.title: Toast buns and stack
steps.content: Toast buns cut-side down, then mayo, slaw, chicken, more mayo, lid on.
steps.detail: Split the buns and toast them cut-side down in a dry pan for about a minute, until golden at the edges — a toasted face is a moisture barrier that stops the bun going soggy. Build in this order: sriracha mayo on the base, a generous tangle of slaw, the crispy chicken, another swipe of mayo on the lid, lid on. Press down gently so it holds together, and eat while the crust still crackles.

title: Haloumi Burger
description: Thick slabs of pan-seared haloumi, golden and squeaky, stacked with avocado, rocket, tomato and sriracha mayo. The vegetarian burger meat-eaters order twice.
storage: This one doesn't keep — haloumi turns rubbery as it cools, so eat it straight from the pan. Store any uncooked haloumi in its brine in the fridge for a couple of weeks.

substitutions.swap: thick slices of paneer, patted dry and seared the same way — it won't squeak but it holds together just as well
baby spinach or shredded lettuce
Greek yoghurt for a lighter, tangier burger

steps.title: Make the sriracha mayo
steps.content: Stir sriracha through mayo; slice tomato; mash avocado with lemon.
steps.detail: Stir the sriracha into the mayo in the small bowl and taste — it should tingle, not burn; add more or less to suit. Slice the tomato into thick rounds and season them with pepper. Halve the avocado, flick out the stone and mash the flesh roughly with a fork, with a squeeze of lemon and a little pepper. Having every layer ready before the haloumi hits the pan matters more here than usual — haloumi is at its best for about five minutes.

steps.title: Slice and dry haloumi
steps.content: Cut the block into 4 slabs about 1 cm thick and pat completely dry.
steps.detail: Slice the haloumi lengthways into four even slabs about 1 cm thick — thick enough to stay soft in the middle while the outside browns. Pat every surface really dry with paper towel. Haloumi comes packed in brine, and any water left on the surface has to boil off before browning can start, which is how people end up with pale, rubbery cheese.

steps.title: Toast the buns
steps.content: Toast buns cut-side down in the dry pan, then spread with mayo.
steps.detail: Set the pan over medium-high heat. While it warms, toast the split buns cut-side down for about a minute, until the faces are golden with a little crunch. Set them on plates and spread the sriracha mayo over both halves. The buns go first on purpose: once the haloumi is cooked you want to build and eat immediately, not stand around toasting.

steps.title: Sear the haloumi
steps.content: Sear slabs in oil, 2 min a side, until deep golden — no longer.
steps.detail: Add the oil to the hot pan and lay the slabs in — they should sizzle on contact. Leave them completely alone for about 2 minutes until the underside is a deep, patchy gold, then flip and give the second side 1–2 minutes. Straight out of the pan, haloumi is soft inside with a gentle squeak between your teeth; cook it too long or let it sit and it tightens into a rubber thong, so the moment it's golden, it's done. It doesn't need salt — the cheese brings plenty.
tips: If the slabs stick when you try to flip, they're not ready — properly browned haloumi releases from the pan on its own.

steps.title: Stack and eat now
steps.content: Rocket, tomato, hot haloumi, avocado on the lid — eat immediately.
steps.detail: Build fast: rocket on the mayo base, tomato slices on top, then two hot haloumi slabs per burger. Spread the mashed avocado on the underside of the lid — it acts as glue and stops the top sliding off — and press it on. Eat straight away while the haloumi is still warm, soft and squeaky; this is a burger that rewards zero patience.

title: Roast Pumpkin and Feta Salad
description: Caramelised roast pumpkin over rocket with creamy feta, toasted pine nuts and a sweet-sharp balsamic dressing. The cafe favourite, done properly at home.
storage: Once dressed, this salad doesn't keep. Store the components separately — roast pumpkin keeps 3 days in the fridge, the dressing a week in its jar — and assemble fresh.
make_ahead: Roast the pumpkin and shake the dressing up to a day ahead; bring the pumpkin back to room temperature (or warm it 5 minutes in a low oven) before assembling.
leftovers: Fold undressed leftovers through warm couscous with a squeeze of lemon for tomorrow's lunch.

substitutions.swap: slivered almonds or chopped walnuts, toasted the same careful way
soft goat's cheese, or shaved parmesan for something sharper
baby spinach — milder, and it holds up a little longer under warm pumpkin
sweet potato wedges, roasted exactly the same way

steps.title: Roast the pumpkin hot
steps.content: Toss 3 cm wedges with oil and salt; roast at 200°C fan, 30–35 min, turning once.
steps.detail: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced — colour on pumpkin needs a genuinely hot oven. Cut the peeled pumpkin into wedges about 3 cm thick and toss with 2 tablespoons of the oil, salt and pepper. Spread them on the tray in a single layer with space between every piece: crowded pumpkin steams in its own moisture and never browns. Roast for 30–35 minutes, turning once around the 20-minute mark, until the edges are deeply golden — properly caramelised, some almost mahogany — and a knife slides in with no resistance.
tips: The darkest, stickiest caramelised edges are the best-tasting bits — deep colour is the goal, not a flaw.
Line the tray with baking paper and the sweet pumpkin juices won't cement themselves to the metal.

steps.title: Mellow the onion
steps.content: Soak the sliced red onion in cold water for 10 min, then drain and pat dry.
steps.detail: While the pumpkin roasts, slice the red onion as finely as you can — paper-thin half-moons, not chunky crescents. Drop them into a bowl of cold water and leave them for 10 minutes. The soak pulls out the harsh, sulphurous bite so you get sweet crunch instead of raw-onion breath. Drain well and pat dry with paper towel; wet onion waters down the dressing.

steps.title: Toast the pine nuts
steps.content: Toast in a dry pan over medium-low, moving constantly, until just golden.
steps.detail: Put the pine nuts in the small dry frying pan — no oil, they carry their own — over medium-low heat. Keep them moving with a shake or a stir the entire time. For 2 minutes nothing seems to happen, then they turn gold and smell rich and nutty almost at once. The instant they're pale gold, tip them onto a cold plate: left in the pan, its stored heat keeps cooking them from blonde to burnt.
warnings: Pine nuts go from golden to scorched in seconds — this is a stand-there-and-watch job, not a walk-away one.

steps.title: Shake the dressing
steps.content: Shake balsamic, honey, oil, salt and pepper in a jar until thickened.
steps.detail: Put the balsamic, honey, remaining tablespoon of oil, a pinch of salt and a crack of pepper in the jar, screw the lid on tight and shake hard for 10 seconds until it looks slightly thickened and cloudy. Taste it on a rocket leaf: it should hit sharp first, then sweet. Too puckery, add a little more honey; too sweet, a splash more balsamic.

steps.title: Assemble while warm
steps.content: Rocket first, then warm pumpkin, onion, feta, pine nuts; dress at the last minute.
steps.detail: Let the pumpkin sit 5 minutes off the tray first — warm is perfect, but straight-from-the-oven hot wilts rocket into a sad green mat. Spread the rocket over the platter, arrange the pumpkin wedges on top, scatter the onion, then crumble the feta over in big soft chunks rather than fine crumbs. Finish with the pine nuts and drizzle the dressing over just before it hits the table — dress it early and the leaves collapse. Serve while the pumpkin still has a little warmth in it.

title: Creamy Banana Porridge
description: Proper stovetop porridge — creamy, gently sweet, topped with banana coins and a ribbon of honey. Ten minutes of stirring buys you a very good morning.
storage: Best eaten straight away. Leftover porridge keeps 2 days in the fridge but sets solid — loosen it with a good splash of milk over low heat and it comes back creamy.
leftovers: Cold set porridge fries surprisingly well — cut it into slabs, pan-fry in butter until golden on both sides and eat with honey.

substitutions.swap: half milk, half water for a lighter bowl — all water works too, you just lose some creaminess
maple syrup, or a spoon of brown sugar stirred in at the end
a handful of frozen berries, dropped in for the last minute so they warm through

steps.title: Measure and slice
steps.content: Measure the oats and milk, and slice the banana into coins.
steps.detail: Porridge is all ratio, so measure rather than guess — 1 cup of oats to a touch over 2 cups of milk gives you creamy, not gluey. Slice the banana into coins about 5 mm thick and set it aside for the top. Get everything to the stove before you turn it on, because once the porridge is going it wants your attention.
tips: Rolled oats are the ones you want here — quick oats turn to paste and steel-cut take three times as long.

steps.title: Start the porridge cold
steps.content: Combine oats, milk and salt in the saucepan and bring to a bare simmer.
steps.detail: Tip the oats, milk and a pinch of salt into the saucepan together, cold — starting everything cold means no lumps and a creamier finish. Put it over medium heat and stir now and then as it warms. You're looking for small bubbles creeping in around the edge of the pan, not a rolling boil. Milk boils over fast, so stay close. The salt isn't optional: without it porridge tastes flat no matter how much honey you add.

steps.title: Stir low and slow
steps.content: Drop the heat to low and stir often for 8 minutes until thick and creamy.
steps.detail: As soon as it simmers, drop the heat right down. Stir in a lazy figure-eight every 30 seconds or so — the stirring rubs starch off the oats and that starch is what makes porridge creamy. It's done when a spoon dragged across the base leaves a trail that closes slowly, like wet cement. If it turns gluggy and stodgy, don't panic: pull it off the heat, stir in a splash of cold milk, and it loosens straight back to silky. If it catches on the bottom, lower the heat and stop scraping that spot — leave any burnt bits where they are.
tips: Porridge keeps thickening off the heat, so stop while it's slightly looser than you want to eat it.

steps.title: Top and serve
steps.content: Spoon into bowls, top with banana, honey and cinnamon.
steps.detail: Divide the porridge between two bowls and lay the banana coins over the top. Drizzle the honey from a bit of height so it falls in thin ribbons rather than one blob, then dust with cinnamon. A little extra cold milk poured around the edge is the old-school touch — it makes a cool moat against the hot middle.

title: French Crepes
description: Thin, lacy crepes the French way — a rested batter, a hot pan and one confident swirl. Lemon and sugar is all they need.
storage: Stack cooled crepes with baking paper between them; they keep 2 days in the fridge or freeze for 2 months. Refresh in a dry pan, 30 seconds a side.
make_ahead: The batter can rest in the fridge overnight — give it a good whisk and check the consistency before cooking.
leftovers: Leftover crepes plus a slice of ham and a handful of cheese, folded into a square in a hot pan, is a five-minute galette-style lunch.

substitutions.swap: orange wedges, or the pulp of a passionfruit spooned over
vegetable oil for greasing the pan works, though butter gives you the lacy brown edges
half milk, half water makes an even lighter, crisper crepe

ingredients.amount: 50 g (30 g melted for the batter, the rest for the pan)

steps.title: Make the batter
steps.content: Whisk flour, eggs and milk into a smooth batter the thickness of pouring cream.
steps.detail: Put the flour and salt in a big bowl and make a well in the centre. Crack in the eggs and start whisking from the middle, pulling in a little flour at a time while you add the milk in a slow stream — this inside-out method is how you avoid lumps. Finish by whisking in 30 g of melted butter. The batter should be the consistency of pouring cream: it coats the back of a spoon but runs off freely. If you've ended up with lumps anyway, just push the batter through a sieve and nobody will ever know.
tips: Melt the butter first so it has a minute to cool — piping hot butter poured onto eggs can scramble them.

steps.title: Rest the batter
steps.content: Rest the batter for 30 minutes at room temperature.
steps.detail: Don't skip this — the rest is what separates crepes from rubbery pancakes. Thirty minutes lets the flour fully absorb the milk and the gluten you built up whisking relax again, which is what gives you tender, lacy crepes. The batter will thicken slightly as it sits, so check it afterwards: if it's thicker than pouring cream, whisk in a splash of milk.

steps.title: Heat and grease the pan
steps.content: Get the pan properly hot over medium-high, then wipe it with butter.
steps.detail: Put the pan over medium-high heat and give it a good 2 minutes — a drop of water flicked in should dance and vanish, not just sit there. Rub the surface with a scrunched bit of paper towel dipped in the remaining butter. You want a thin shine across the whole pan, not a pool; too much butter and the crepe fries crisp instead of staying supple.

steps.title: Swirl the first crepe
steps.content: Ladle in batter, lift and swirl the pan to a thin circle — the first one is a tester.
steps.detail: Lift the pan off the heat, pour in a small ladle of batter (about 60 ml) at the centre, and immediately tilt and roll your wrist so the batter races out to a thin, even circle before it sets. Back on the heat for about a minute — it's ready to flip when the edges turn lacy brown and lift from the pan, and the top looks dry. Loosen with the spatula, then flip with your fingers and the spatula together; the second side needs only 30 seconds. Know this now: the first crepe is always a bit sad — torn, blotchy, wrongly thick. That's the pan finding its temperature, not you failing. It's the cook's treat. Eat it and carry on.
tips: If the batter sets before it covers the pan, you're pouring too slowly or the pan is too hot — swirl faster and nudge the heat down a touch.

steps.title: Cook the rest
steps.content: Keep going — swirl, flip, stack — re-buttering every few crepes.
steps.detail: From crepe two onwards you'll find a rhythm: butter-wipe every second or third crepe, pour, swirl, count to sixty, flip. Adjust the heat as you go — too dark too fast means turn it down; pale and floppy after 90 seconds means turn it up. Stack the finished crepes straight on top of each other on a plate; their own steam keeps the pile soft and warm. You should get 10 to 12.

steps.title: Serve with lemon and sugar
steps.content: Sprinkle each crepe with sugar, squeeze over lemon, fold into triangles.
steps.detail: Work while they're warm: a scatter of sugar right to the edges, a good squeeze of lemon, then fold each crepe in half and in half again into a fat triangle. The sugar half-melts into the lemon juice and that sweet-sharp syrup is the whole point. Two or three each is a serve, in theory.

title: Creamy Scrambled Eggs on Toast
description: Low heat, constant folding, off the stove while they're still glossy — the scrambled egg technique everyone should own, piled onto hot buttered toast.
storage: Scrambled eggs are a cook-and-eat dish — they turn rubbery and weepy reheated, so only make what you'll eat right now.

substitutions.swap: any bread you like — a thick-cut sourdough holds up best under soft eggs
a few torn parsley leaves, or nothing at all

ingredients.amount: 40 g, plus extra for the toast

steps.title: Whisk the eggs
steps.content: Crack the eggs into a bowl with a pinch of salt and whisk until no streaks remain.
steps.detail: Crack the eggs into a bowl — crack on the bench, not the bowl's edge, and you'll get less shell in. Add a pinch of salt and whisk for a good 20 to 30 seconds until the mixture is one even pale yellow with no ropes of clear white left. Salting now, before cooking, actually makes the eggs more tender. Skip the milk — it dilutes the flavour and makes the eggs weep on the plate.

steps.title: Get the toast going
steps.content: Drop the bread in the toaster so it's ready as the eggs finish.
steps.detail: Timing is the only trick in this dish: toast waits better than eggs, but only just. Drop the bread in as you start the eggs so it pops around the time they're done. Butter each slice the moment it pops, while it's hot enough to melt the butter into the surface rather than sit on top.

steps.title: Cook low and fold
steps.content: Melt the butter over low heat, add the eggs and fold slowly until soft curds form.
steps.detail: Melt the butter in the pan over low heat — genuinely low; the butter should foam gently, never brown. Pour in the eggs and wait about 20 seconds, then start drawing the spatula slowly across the base, pushing the setting egg from the edges into the middle. Big, soft folds — you're not stirring a risotto. Rolling waves of creamy curd will build over 3 to 4 minutes. If things are moving fast or the eggs are catching, just lift the pan off the heat and keep folding; the pan's stored heat keeps cooking them gently.
tips: Low and slow is the whole recipe. If you can hear aggressive sizzling, the pan is too hot.

steps.title: Stop while glossy
steps.content: Pull the pan off the heat while the eggs still look slightly underdone and glossy.
steps.detail: This is the step that changes your eggs forever: take the pan off the heat when the eggs look about 80 per cent done — glossy, wet, barely holding together. That's carryover cooking at work; the residual heat finishes setting them in the 30 to 60 seconds it takes to reach the plate, and they arrive perfect. Wait until they look done in the pan and they'll be dry and pebbly on the plate. Grind in pepper now. If you do overshoot, a small knob of cold butter folded through softens the damage.

steps.title: Pile onto toast
steps.content: Heap the eggs onto the buttered toast, scatter with spring onion and eat immediately.
steps.detail: Buttered toast on plates, eggs piled generously on top, a scatter of spring onion and one more crack of pepper. Eat straight away — soft scrambled eggs have about a two-minute window of perfection and it's rude to waste it.

title: Pumpkin Scones
description: The CWA classic — tall golden scones with mashed pumpkin folded through a barely-handled dough. Warm, split and buttered is the only way to eat them.
storage: Best the day they're baked. Keep airtight for 2 days and refresh in a warm oven for 5 minutes, or freeze for up to 2 months and thaw in a low oven.
make_ahead: Cook and mash the pumpkin up to 2 days ahead and keep it in the fridge — cold mash is actually easier to handle and makes a less sticky dough.
leftovers: Split day-old scones, toast the cut sides under the grill until golden, and eat with butter and honey.

substitutions.swap: sweet potato, cooked and mashed exactly the same way
buttermilk makes them even more tender

ingredients.amount: 350 g, peeled and cubed (makes about 1 cup mash)
40 g, softened, plus extra to serve
375 g (2 1/2 cups), plus extra for the bench

steps.title: Cook the pumpkin
steps.content: Boil the pumpkin until fork-tender, drain very well, mash smooth and cool.
steps.detail: Cut the peeled pumpkin into 2 to 3 cm chunks, drop into a saucepan of boiling water and cook for about 15 minutes, until a fork slides in with no resistance. Now the important bit: drain it thoroughly, then tip it back into the hot dry saucepan for a minute so the steam escapes — watery mash makes a sticky, heavy dough. Mash with a fork until completely smooth, then let it cool right down. Hot mash melts the butter in the next step and turns the dough to glue.
tips: You need 1 cup (about 250 g) of mash. A little over is fine; a lot over makes the dough too wet.

steps.title: Preheat the oven
steps.content: Heat the oven to 200°C fan-forced and line the baking tray.
steps.detail: Get the oven going now so it's properly hot when the dough is ready — scones need to hit real heat immediately for their big rise. Line the tray with baking paper and flour your bench, because once the dough comes together you want to move fast, not hunt for the flour.

steps.title: Cream butter and sugar
steps.content: Beat the butter and sugar until pale, beat in the egg, then stir in the pumpkin.
steps.detail: In a large bowl, beat the softened butter and sugar with a wooden spoon until pale and creamy — a minute or two of honest effort. Beat in the egg; the mixture will look curdled and split, and that's completely normal. Stir in the cooled pumpkin mash until it's one even orange cream.

steps.title: Fold in the flour
steps.content: Add the flour and salt and fold until it only just comes together.
steps.detail: Add the self-raising flour and salt in two lots, folding with a butter knife or spatula — cutting and turning, not beating. Stop the moment there's no dry flour left; the dough should look shaggy and rough, not smooth. This is the secret to light scones: every extra stir builds gluten, and gluten makes scones tough and flat. If it's far too sticky to handle, work in a tablespoon of flour; if there are dry crumbly patches, a splash of milk.
tips: A rough, slightly ugly dough bakes into a light scone. A smooth, well-kneaded dough bakes into a rock.

steps.title: Pat out and cut
steps.content: Pat the dough 3 cm thick and cut rounds — press straight down, never twist.
steps.detail: Tip the dough onto the floured bench and pat it out gently with your hands to about 3 cm thick — no rolling pin, it presses out the air. Dip the cutter in flour, then press straight down and lift straight up. Do not twist. Twisting smears the cut edge closed and the scone rises lopsided and short. Gather the scraps, pat once more and cut again — you should get about 12. Sit them on the tray with their sides just touching; they hold each other up and climb higher.

steps.title: Bake until golden
steps.content: Brush the tops with milk and bake for 15 minutes until risen and golden.
steps.detail: Brush just the tops with milk for shine — don't let it dribble down the sides, it can glue the rise. Into the hot oven for 15 minutes. They're done when they've jumped up tall, the tops are golden, and one lifted from the tray sounds hollow when you tap its base. Underdone scones feel heavy for their size.
warnings: Don't open the oven for the first 10 minutes — the rise depends on steady heat, and a cold draught flattens them.

steps.title: Cool briefly and serve
steps.content: Rest 5 minutes on a wire rack, then split and butter while warm.
steps.detail: Move the scones to a wire rack so their bottoms don't sweat and go soggy, but only give them 5 minutes — pumpkin scones are at their absolute best warm. Split with your hands or a knife and butter generously. Watch the butter melt in. This is why you made them.

title: Self-Saucing Chocolate Pudding
description: Kitchen magic for cold nights — one strange-looking assembly goes into the oven and comes out as fudgy pudding floating on its own hot chocolate sauce.
storage: Best hot from the oven while the sauce is molten. Leftovers keep 2 days covered in the fridge — the sauce soaks in, but 30 seconds in the microwave turns it back into a fudgy pudding.
make_ahead: Assemble the ramekins up to the sprinkled topping a few hours ahead and keep them covered in the fridge — the batter has raw egg and milk in it. Add the boiling water just before baking and allow an extra minute or two.

substitutions.swap: white sugar works in the topping — you just lose a little of the caramel note
a pour of thickened cream, or nothing — the pudding makes its own sauce

ingredients.amount: 4 tablespoons (half for the batter, half for the topping)
60 g, melted, plus extra for greasing

steps.title: Preheat and grease
steps.content: Heat the oven to 170°C fan-forced, butter the ramekins and put the kettle on.
steps.detail: Set the oven to 170°C fan-forced. Butter the inside of six 250 ml ramekins and sit them on a baking tray — the tray makes them easy to move and catches any sauce that bubbles over. Put the kettle on now too; you'll need about 375 ml of boiling water in a few minutes.

steps.title: Make the batter
steps.content: Whisk the dry ingredients, then stir in milk, melted butter, egg and vanilla.
steps.detail: Whisk the self-raising flour, sugar and half the cocoa in a large bowl so the cocoa is evenly through — cocoa loves to hide in lumps. In a jug, whisk the milk, melted butter, egg and vanilla together, then pour into the dry mix and stir until just smooth. It's a thick batter, more like mud than pancake mix. Stop stirring as soon as the flour disappears.

steps.title: Divide and sprinkle topping
steps.content: Divide the batter between ramekins, then sprinkle the brown sugar and cocoa mix on top.
steps.detail: Spoon the batter evenly between the six ramekins — they'll each be about half full, which looks stingy but leave room, because there's more coming. Mix the brown sugar with the remaining cocoa in a small bowl, rubbing out any lumps with your fingers, and sprinkle it evenly over the top of each pudding. It looks like far too much sugar sitting there. It isn't — that layer is about to become the sauce.

steps.title: Pour the boiling water
steps.content: Gently pour boiling water over the back of a spoon onto each pudding. Don't stir.
steps.detail: Here's the leap of faith. Hold a dessert spoon upside down just above each ramekin and pour about 60 ml of boiling water onto its back, so the water spreads softly across the surface instead of punching a crater in the batter. Whatever you do, do not stir. You now have three layers — batter, sugar, water — and it looks like a mistake. Trust it: in the oven the batter rises up through the water while the sugar layer dissolves beneath it into a glossy chocolate sauce.
warnings: You're pouring boiling water over your hand's path — keep the kettle spout low, pour slowly, and keep kids back.

steps.title: Bake until just set
steps.content: Bake for 20 minutes until the tops are set but the centres still have a slight wobble.
steps.detail: Slide the tray into the oven for 20 minutes. The tops will rise and look like little chocolate cakes — done means the surface is firm at the edges with the barest wobble in the middle, and a skewer poked just into the top layer comes out with moist crumbs. Don't push the skewer to the bottom; down there it's meant to be liquid. And don't be tempted by five more minutes: overbaking is the one way to ruin this pudding, because the batter drinks up its own sauce and you're left with plain cake.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.content: Stand for 5 minutes, then serve in the ramekin with a scoop of ice cream.
steps.detail: Let the puddings stand for 5 minutes — the sauce thickens from watery to glossy in that time. Serve them in their ramekins on a small plate, and crack through the cakey top with a spoon to find the sauce underneath. A scoop of vanilla ice cream melting into the hot chocolate is the full experience.
warnings: The sauce holds heat like a syrup — a spoonful straight from the oven will burn. The 5-minute rest matters.

title: Choc Peanut Bliss Balls
description: Dates, oats, cocoa and peanut butter blitzed into fudgy no-bake balls and rolled in coconut. Lunchbox gold, and the food processor does all the work.
storage: Keep airtight in the fridge for up to a week — they get better as they firm up — or freeze for 2 months and eat straight from frozen; they stay chewy.

substitutions.swap: tahini keeps them safe for nut-free schools
maple syrup keeps them vegan
roll them in cocoa instead for a truffle look

steps.title: Soak the dates
steps.content: If the dates are firm, soak them in boiling water for 10 minutes, then drain well.
steps.detail: Squeeze a date: if it's soft and squishy like a caramel, skip straight to the next step. If it's firm or leathery — most supermarket pitted dates are — put them in a heatproof bowl, cover with boiling water and leave for 10 minutes. Then drain thoroughly and pat them dry with paper towel. This last bit matters: waterlogged dates make a wet mix that never rolls into balls, just smears.

steps.title: Blitz the oats
steps.content: Process the oats alone to a rough flour, about 20 seconds.
steps.detail: Oats first, on their own — once the sticky ingredients go in, whole oats never grind down properly and you get chewy flakes through your bliss balls. Run the processor for 20 to 30 seconds until they look like rough, sandy flour. A few bigger bits are fine; powder-fine isn't the goal.

steps.title: Blitz to a dough
steps.content: Add dates, cocoa, peanut butter, honey and salt; process until one sticky mass forms.
steps.detail: Add everything else and process in long pulses, stopping once or twice to scrape down the sides. The mix goes through stages: dry crumbs, then damp clumps, then suddenly it gathers into one dark mass thumping around the blade — that's done, usually about a minute. Test it: pinch a bit between your fingers, and if it holds together it's ready. Too dry and crumbly? Add a teaspoon of water and pulse again. Too wet and smeary? A tablespoon of oats.

steps.title: Roll with wet hands
steps.content: Roll heaped tablespoons of mixture into balls with damp hands.
steps.detail: Keep a small bowl of water next to you and wet your palms — this is the trick that makes rolling pleasant instead of a sticky nightmare. Scoop a heaped tablespoon of mixture, squeeze it together, then roll firmly between your palms until smooth. Re-wet your hands whenever the mix starts clinging. You should get about 18 balls; line them up on a plate as you go.

steps.title: Coat and chill
steps.content: Roll each ball in desiccated coconut, then chill for 30 minutes to firm up.
steps.detail: Tip the coconut onto a plate and roll each ball through it while the surface is still slightly tacky, pressing gently so it sticks. Then into the fridge for 30 minutes — they go in soft and come out with that firm, fudgy bite that makes them feel like a proper treat rather than raw dough. They'll seem too soft before chilling; that's normal.
